meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B005, B006, and B007 are in place. This is the integration block that binds them together. Flag as urgent if signals indicate systemic allergen management failures or multiple allergen-related complaints.

blocks.name: Handwashing Protocol
blocks.purpose: Establishes a clear, enforced handwashing protocol specifying when, how, and for how long staff must wash their hands, with compliance monitoring to prevent pathogen transmission. This block is the foundation of personal hygiene in any food business and a key element inspected by EHOs.

detailed_objectives: Define every trigger point that requires handwashing — arrival, after toilet use, after handling raw food, after touching bins, after handling money, after sneezing or coughing
Standardise the handwashing technique to the NHS/FSA recommended 20-second method with soap and warm water
Ensure adequate handwashing facilities are available, stocked, and maintained
Implement visible compliance monitoring so handwashing becomes a verified habit, not an assumed one
Create documentation that demonstrates due diligence for EHO inspections
secondary_objectives: Reduce the risk of Norovirus, E. coli, and other pathogen transmission through hand contact
Build a hygiene-first culture where staff remind each other to wash hands without embarrassment
Support a Food Hygiene Rating of 5 by demonstrating robust personal hygiene controls

blocks.why_this_matters: Hand contamination is the single most common route of pathogen transmission in food businesses. The Food Standards Agency's Safer Food Better Business (SFBB) pack identifies handwashing as a cornerstone of the '4 Cs' framework under Cross-contamination prevention. EU-retained Regulation (EC) 852/2004, Annex II, Chapter I requires food premises to provide an adequate number of washbasins for cleaning hands, supplied with hot and cold running water, soap, and hygienic drying facilities. These washbasins must be separate from those used for washing food. During an EHO inspection, the officer will check that handwashing facilities are adequate, accessible, and in use — they will often observe staff behaviour to see if handwashing is actually happening, not just documented. A cafe where the handwash basin is blocked by boxes, lacks soap, or uses a shared towel will lose points on the Food Hygiene Rating scheme. Norovirus, which causes the majority of foodborne illness outbreaks in the UK, is primarily transmitted through contaminated hands. A single infected staff member who does not wash their hands properly after using the toilet can contaminate hundreds of portions of food. The consequences range from customer illness and negative reviews to local authority investigation, media coverage, and business closure. Handwashing is the cheapest, simplest, and most effective food safety measure available — but only if it is done correctly, consistently, and monitored.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Inspect all handwashing facilities in the cafe. There must be at least one dedicated handwash basin that is not used for food preparation or washing equipment. The basin must have hot and cold (or warm) running water, antibacterial soap in a wall-mounted dispenser (not a shared bar of soap), and hygienic hand drying — either paper towels from a dispenser or a hot air dryer. If any of these are missing, fix them immediately — this is a legal requirement. Post a handwashing instruction poster above every handwash basin showing the correct 20-second technique: wet hands, apply soap, rub palms together, interlace fingers, rub backs of hands, rub thumbs, rub fingertips in palms, rinse thoroughly, dry with paper towel. Define and communicate the mandatory handwashing trigger points to all staff: when arriving for a shift, after using the toilet, after handling raw meat or eggs, after touching bins or waste, after handling money, after sneezing, coughing or touching face, after eating or drinking, after handling cleaning chemicals, and between different food preparation tasks. Week 2: The manager observes each staff member's handwashing at least twice during the week and provides immediate corrective feedback if the technique is wrong, too brief, or skipped. Begin a simple handwashing compliance log — a tick sheet posted near the basin where staff initial after washing. This is not a perfect system but creates visibility and accountability from Day 1.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Handwashing SOP document that codifies the technique, trigger points, facility requirements, and monitoring procedures. Include photographs showing correct technique. Integrate handwashing compliance into the daily manager walk-around — during every shift, the manager should observe at least one handwashing event and check that facilities are stocked. Introduce a weekly facility check: soap levels, paper towel stock, water temperature, basin cleanliness, and poster condition. Record this check on a dedicated facilities log. Train all staff as part of their Level 2 Food Hygiene certification — handwashing technique is a core element tested in the qualification. For new starters, the handwashing demonstration should be one of the first things they learn, before they touch any food or equipment. Implement a peer accountability system: encourage staff to remind each other to wash hands without creating a punitive culture. The language should be collaborative ('Hey, don't forget to wash your hands after that') rather than accusatory. By Week 8, the cafe should have an SOP, regular facility checks, observed compliance, and documented training for every team member.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Handwashing should now be automatic behaviour — staff should feel uncomfortable if they have not washed their hands, like leaving the house without keys. Conduct quarterly handwashing audits where the manager observes technique and timing without announcing the audit in advance. Track compliance trends and share results with the team — aim for 100% observed compliance. Consider installing a timed handwashing station that plays a 20-second audio cue. Review facility standards annually — are the soap and paper towels high quality enough that staff actually want to use them? Cheap, harsh soap discourages handwashing. Integrate handwashing into broader hygiene culture initiatives (B012) so it becomes part of the cafe's identity as a clean, professional operation.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Inspect all handwashing facilities for compliance: hot/cold water, soap dispenser, paper towels
Fix any deficiencies immediately — missing soap, blocked basin, no paper towels
Post NHS/FSA-compliant handwashing instruction posters above every basin
Communicate mandatory handwashing trigger points to all staff
Demonstrate correct 20-second handwashing technique to every team member
Begin a handwashing compliance tick sheet near the basin
Manager observes each staff member washing hands at least twice in the first week

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft a Handwashing SOP with technique, trigger points, and monitoring procedures
Implement weekly facility checks: soap, towels, water temperature, basin cleanliness
Integrate handwashing observation into the daily manager walk-around
Include handwashing demonstration as the first element of new starter induction
Establish peer accountability culture for handwashing reminders
Document all handwashing training in individual staff records
File facility check logs and SOP in the SFBB diary

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly unannounced handwashing audits
Track and share compliance trends with the team
Review soap and towel quality to encourage use
Consider timed handwashing stations with audio cues
Integrate handwashing into broader hygiene culture programme (B012)

tools.description: A4 laminated poster showing the correct 20-second handwashing technique step by step, positioned above every handwash basin in the cafe.
Daily tick sheet posted near the handwash basin where staff initial after washing at key trigger points, creating visibility and accountability.
Weekly checklist for inspecting handwashing facilities: soap levels, paper towel stock, water temperature, basin cleanliness, poster condition, and overall accessibility.
Standard Operating Procedure covering handwashing technique, trigger points, facility requirements, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions for non-compliance.
Structured audit form for unannounced quarterly handwashing observations, recording technique quality, timing, trigger point compliance, and facility condition.

failure_modes: Handwash basin is used for rinsing cups or storing items, making it unavailable for handwashing
Soap dispenser runs empty and is not refilled promptly, so staff rinse with water only
Staff wash hands too briefly (under 10 seconds) without using proper technique
Compliance log is filled in retrospectively or fabricated, giving false assurance
kpis: 100% of handwashing facilities stocked and functional at all times during service
Zero observed instances of staff handling food without proper handwashing
Quarterly audit scores averaging 95% or above for technique and trigger point compliance
All new starters complete handwashing demonstration before handling any food
constraints: At least one dedicated handwash basin must be available that is not used for food preparation
Antibacterial soap and paper towels must be continuously available during all operating hours
Handwashing instruction posters must be permanently displayed above every basin

time_load.L1: 3 hours (facility inspection, poster installation, staff demonstration)
time_load.L2: 5 hours (SOP drafting, facility check setup, training integration)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/quarter (audits, trend analysis, facility reviews)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily observation by shift manager during service. Weekly facility check logged and signed. Monthly compliance log review. Quarterly unannounced handwashing audit. SOP reviewed annually or when facility changes occur.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block establishes a clear handwashing protocol covering when, how, and for how long staff must wash their hands, with proper facilities, instruction posters, and compliance monitoring. It is the most fundamental personal hygiene measure in any food business.
implementation_risks: Handwashing compliance drops during busy periods when staff feel rushed
Facilities in poor condition (cold water, harsh soap) discourage proper handwashing
Staff may view monitoring as micromanagement — frame it as professional standards, not distrust
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate immediately for all cafes. Flag as urgent if signals indicate handwashing facility deficiencies, observed non-compliance, EHO hygiene concerns, or food illness reports. Foundation block for all personal hygiene.

blocks.name: Cross-Contamination Prevention
blocks.purpose: Prevents the transfer of harmful bacteria and allergens between foods, surfaces, and equipment through colour-coded chopping boards, dedicated utensils, and workflow separation. This block addresses one of the most common causes of foodborne illness and EHO non-compliance findings in UK cafes.

detailed_objectives: Implement a colour-coded chopping board and utensil system to prevent bacterial cross-contamination
Establish physical and temporal workflow separation between raw and ready-to-eat food preparation
Create clear rules for equipment use, storage, and cleaning that prevent cross-contact
Address allergen cross-contamination through dedicated preparation areas and equipment
Train all staff to understand why cross-contamination occurs and how their actions prevent it
secondary_objectives: Reduce the risk of E. coli, Salmonella, Campylobacter, and Listeria transmission in the kitchen
Support HACCP compliance by controlling cross-contamination as a critical hazard
Eliminate confusion about which equipment to use for which task

blocks.why_this_matters: Cross-contamination is one of the four pillars of the Food Standards Agency's Safer Food Better Business (SFBB) framework — the '4 Cs' of Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and Cross-contamination. It occurs when harmful bacteria or allergens are transferred from one food, surface, or piece of equipment to another. In a cafe kitchen, this can happen in seconds: a knife used to cut raw chicken is then used to slice bread; a prep surface is wiped with a cloth that was previously used on raw meat; or a barista touches a nut-topped cake and then handles a customer's sandwich. The consequences range from customer illness to fatal allergic reactions. Campylobacter, the most common cause of food poisoning in the UK (with over 280,000 cases annually according to the FSA), is primarily transmitted through cross-contamination from raw poultry. E. coli O157, which can cause kidney failure, spreads through contact between raw and ready-to-eat foods. For cafes that handle both raw ingredients and ready-to-eat items like sandwiches, salads, and baked goods, the risk is particularly acute because the ready-to-eat items will not undergo further cooking to kill any transferred bacteria. EHO inspectors specifically look for evidence of cross-contamination controls: colour-coded boards, separate storage, clean-as-you-go practices, and staff knowledge. Failures in this area commonly result in a Food Hygiene Rating of 3 or below and may trigger a revisit inspection.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Purchase and implement a colour-coded chopping board system following the UK industry standard: Red for raw meat, Blue for raw fish, Yellow for cooked meat, Green for salad and fruit, Brown for vegetables, White for bakery and dairy. Label each board clearly and store them in a visible rack so the correct board is always easy to grab. If the cafe does minimal raw food preparation (many cafes do not handle raw meat), simplify to three boards minimum: one for allergen-free items, one for items containing allergens, and one general purpose. Assign dedicated utensils (knives, tongs, spoons) to each preparation area — do not allow a knife that has touched raw food to be used for ready-to-eat items without thorough washing and sanitising first. Inspect the kitchen layout: is there physical separation between raw food storage/preparation and ready-to-eat food preparation? At minimum, raw foods must be stored below ready-to-eat foods in the fridge to prevent drip contamination. Ideally, raw and ready-to-eat preparation happens on different surfaces. If space is limited, establish temporal separation: prepare raw items first, thoroughly clean and sanitise the surface, then prepare ready-to-eat items. Week 2: Brief all kitchen staff on the colour-coding system and the reasons behind it. Conduct a walk-through of the kitchen with the team, identifying every cross-contamination risk point and the control measure for each. Post a colour-code reference chart in the kitchen.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Cross-Contamination Prevention SOP covering: the colour-coding system, utensil assignment, workflow separation, fridge storage rules, cleaning procedures between tasks, and allergen cross-contact controls. Map the kitchen workflow to identify potential cross-contamination points — where do raw and ready-to-eat preparation zones overlap? Can the layout be improved to create better separation? Implement clean-as-you-go procedures: after any task involving raw food or allergens, the surface, utensils, and hands must be cleaned and sanitised before the next task begins. Provide colour-coded cleaning cloths that match the chopping board system — a red cloth for raw meat areas, green for salad prep areas, and so on. Establish allergen-specific controls: if the cafe serves nut-containing items alongside nut-free items, designate specific preparation areas, utensils, and storage for each. Consider the risk from shared equipment like toasters, blenders, and cake slicers. Train all kitchen staff to Level 2 Food Hygiene standard, which covers cross-contamination in detail. Conduct weekly kitchen inspections checking that colour-coding is being followed, equipment is properly assigned, and surfaces are being cleaned between tasks.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Cross-contamination prevention should be automatic muscle memory for all kitchen staff. Conduct quarterly internal audits that include observing food preparation practices during a normal service period — not just checking paperwork but watching what actually happens. Use ATP (adenosine triphosphate) swab testing on surfaces to objectively measure cleaning effectiveness — a surface that looks clean may still be contaminated. Track audit results over time and identify trends. Review the kitchen layout annually — could better equipment positioning reduce cross-contamination risk? Share best practices from industry sources such as the Chartered Institute of Environmental Health. Celebrate the team when audits show consistently high standards.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Purchase colour-coded chopping boards and a storage rack
Assign dedicated utensils to each preparation area
Inspect fridge storage to ensure raw foods are stored below ready-to-eat items
Establish physical or temporal separation between raw and ready-to-eat preparation
Post a colour-code reference chart in the kitchen
Brief all kitchen staff on the system and the reasons behind it
Conduct a kitchen walk-through identifying every cross-contamination risk point

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft a Cross-Contamination Prevention SOP
Map the kitchen workflow and identify cross-contamination risk points
Implement clean-as-you-go procedures with colour-coded cleaning cloths
Establish allergen-specific preparation areas and dedicated equipment
Conduct weekly kitchen inspections for colour-coding and equipment compliance
Train all kitchen staff to Level 2 Food Hygiene standard
File SOPs, inspection records, and training records in the food safety folder

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly observational audits during normal service
Introduce ATP swab testing to verify surface cleanliness
Review kitchen layout annually for improved workflow separation
Track audit trends and share results with the team
Celebrate consistently high cross-contamination prevention standards

deliverables: Kitchen Workflow Map with risk points identified

tools.description: Laminated A4 chart showing the colour-coded chopping board and utensil system with the food type assigned to each colour, posted in the kitchen for quick reference.
Standard Operating Procedure covering colour-coding, utensil assignment, workflow separation, fridge storage rules, clean-as-you-go procedures, and allergen cross-contact controls.
Weekly inspection checklist verifying colour-coded board use, utensil assignment, fridge storage order, surface cleanliness, and workflow separation compliance.
Visual map of the kitchen layout identifying all cross-contamination risk points with corresponding control measures, used for staff training and layout reviews.
Comprehensive quarterly audit covering observational checks during service, ATP swab test results, equipment condition, and staff knowledge assessment.

failure_modes: Colour-coded boards are purchased but staff revert to using the nearest available board during busy periods
Utensils migrate between preparation areas and are used interchangeably without cleaning
Clean-as-you-go procedures are skipped during peak times, leaving contaminated surfaces in use
Raw and ready-to-eat items are stored at the same level in the fridge, allowing drip contamination
kpis: 100% compliance with colour-coded board usage observed in weekly inspections
Zero instances of raw food stored above ready-to-eat food in any fridge
Weekly kitchen inspections completed on schedule with all issues resolved within 24 hours
All kitchen staff can explain the colour-coding system and cross-contamination risks when asked
constraints: Minimum six colour-coded chopping boards required for full implementation
Kitchen layout must allow at least temporal separation between raw and ready-to-eat preparation
All staff handling food must hold or be working towards Level 2 Food Hygiene certification

time_load.L1: 4 hours (equipment purchase, setup, staff briefing, kitchen walk-through)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (SOP drafting, workflow mapping, training, inspection setup)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (audits, ATP testing, layout reviews)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily observation by kitchen lead during service. Weekly formal kitchen inspection. Monthly SOP compliance review. Quarterly comprehensive cross-contamination audit. Annual kitchen layout review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block prevents harmful bacteria and allergens from transferring between foods, surfaces, and equipment through colour-coded boards, dedicated utensils, workflow separation, and clean-as-you-go procedures. It addresses one of the most common causes of foodborne illness.
implementation_risks: Staff may resist colour-coding as unnecessary during busy periods — emphasise the why, not just the what
Small kitchen spaces make physical separation challenging — temporal separation is the fallback
Equipment investment is relatively low but must be maintained and replaced when worn
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate for all cafes that prepare food on premises. Flag as urgent if signals indicate mixed-use equipment, no colour-coding system, raw/cooked food stored together, or EHO cross-contamination concerns.

blocks.name: Cleaning Schedule Protocol
blocks.purpose: Establishes a comprehensive daily, weekly, and monthly cleaning schedule covering every surface, piece of equipment, and area in the cafe, with clear task assignments, sign-off procedures, and chemical usage guidance. This block ensures the cafe meets the 'Cleaning' pillar of the SFBB 4 Cs framework.

detailed_objectives: Define every cleaning task in the cafe with frequency (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly), method, chemicals, and responsible person
Create a sign-off system so every task has a verified completion record
Ensure cleaning chemicals are food-safe, correctly diluted, and stored safely
Establish clean-as-you-go practices alongside scheduled deep cleaning
Provide an auditable cleaning record that satisfies EHO inspection requirements
secondary_objectives: Reduce pest attraction by maintaining consistently clean premises
Extend equipment lifespan through regular cleaning and maintenance
Create a visibly clean environment that builds customer confidence

blocks.why_this_matters: Cleaning is one of the four pillars of the FSA's Safer Food Better Business framework and is scrutinised at every EHO inspection. Under EU-retained Regulation (EC) 852/2004, food premises must be kept clean and maintained in good repair and condition. The Food Hygiene Rating scheme scores premises on hygienic food handling, structural compliance, and confidence in management — cleaning directly impacts all three. An Environmental Health Officer will check not just that surfaces look clean, but that there is a documented cleaning schedule demonstrating systematic, consistent cleaning practices. A cafe that cleans reactively — only when things look dirty — will inevitably miss hidden contamination. Grease builds up behind equipment, milk residue accumulates in steam wand tips, and bacteria colonise grouting between tiles. These are the areas EHOs specifically target because they reveal whether cleaning is genuine or cosmetic. Poor cleaning creates ideal conditions for pest infestations — mice and cockroaches are attracted to food residue, and their presence triggers immediate enforcement action. In the worst cases, a Hygiene Emergency Prohibition Notice can force immediate closure. Beyond compliance, customers form instant judgements about food safety based on visible cleanliness. A dirty counter, sticky table, or grimy toilet will drive customers away faster than any other single factor. The cleaning schedule is not a bureaucratic exercise — it is the systematic foundation that keeps the cafe safe, compliant, and welcoming.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Walk through every area of the cafe and list every surface, piece of equipment, and zone that requires cleaning. Break this into areas: front-of-house (tables, chairs, counter, display cabinets, floor, windows, toilets), bar area (espresso machine exterior, grinder, steam wands, milk jugs, syrups station, POS area), kitchen (prep surfaces, chopping boards, sinks, oven, microwave, fridge interiors, extractor hood), storage (dry store shelves, walk-in fridge, freezer), and external (signage, outdoor seating, entrance, bins). For each item, define: what is cleaned, how it is cleaned (method and chemicals), how often (after each use, daily, weekly, monthly), and who is responsible. Create a master cleaning schedule document. For daily tasks, print a daily cleaning checklist with space for initials and date — this is what EHOs want to see. Assign cleaning tasks to specific shifts: opening team handles morning setup cleaning, closing team handles end-of-day deep clean, and everyone practises clean-as-you-go during service. Week 2: Review the chemical products used for cleaning. All chemicals must be food-safe where they contact food surfaces. Check that sanitiser is bactericidal and meets BS EN standards. Ensure chemicals are stored separately from food in a locked or designated cupboard. Create a COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) file listing every chemical used, its safety data sheet, and correct dilution rates.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Cleaning SOP document that covers every cleaning task in detail: what, how, with what chemicals, at what dilution, how often, by whom, and how completion is verified. Include a separate section for equipment-specific cleaning — espresso machine group heads, grinder burrs, dishwasher filters, ice machine, and blender components all need specific procedures. Implement a weekly deep-clean schedule covering tasks not done daily: fridge interior wipes, oven cleaning, extractor hood filter washing, drain cleaning, and wall washing behind equipment. Create a monthly schedule for tasks done less frequently: ceiling vents, light fittings, behind-fridge areas, and storage room reorganisation. Assign a cleaning inspection to the closing manager each day — they verify that all daily tasks are completed to standard before anyone leaves. Introduce a weekly cleaning audit where the manager rates each area on a 1-5 scale and records the scores. Track scores over time to identify areas that consistently underperform. By Week 8, the cafe should have a complete cleaning SOP, daily/weekly/monthly schedules with sign-off, a COSHH file, and at least 6 weeks of auditable cleaning records.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Cleaning should be embedded as a shared responsibility, not a chore assigned to the most junior person. Conduct quarterly deep-clean events where the whole team participates — this builds ownership and catches areas that routine cleaning misses. Consider introducing ATP swab testing to objectively measure surface cleanliness and provide staff with immediate feedback. Review cleaning product effectiveness annually — are there better, more environmentally friendly products available? Track cleaning audit scores monthly and set improvement targets. Display cleaning standards visually in the staff area and celebrate teams that consistently achieve high scores. Integrate cleaning protocols into the broader hygiene culture programme (B012).

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Walk through every area and list every surface, equipment, and zone requiring cleaning
Define cleaning method, chemicals, frequency, and responsible person for each item
Create and print daily cleaning checklists with sign-off columns
Assign cleaning tasks to opening, service, and closing shifts
Review all cleaning chemicals for food safety compliance
Create a COSHH file with safety data sheets for every chemical
Ensure chemicals are stored separately from food in a designated cupboard

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft a comprehensive Cleaning SOP with detailed procedures for every task
Implement weekly and monthly deep-clean schedules with sign-off sheets
Create equipment-specific cleaning procedures for espresso machines, grinders, and kitchen equipment
Establish daily closing inspection by the shift manager
Introduce weekly cleaning audits with area-by-area scoring
Track audit scores over time to identify underperforming areas
File all cleaning records, COSHH data, and SOPs in the food safety folder

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly whole-team deep-clean events
Introduce ATP swab testing for objective cleanliness measurement
Review cleaning product effectiveness and environmental impact annually
Display cleaning audit scores and celebrate high-performing teams
Integrate cleaning standards into the hygiene culture programme (B012)

deliverables: Master Cleaning Schedule (daily, weekly, monthly tasks)
Daily Cleaning Checklist with sign-off (printable A4)
COSHH Chemical Register and Safety Data Sheet file

tools.description: Printable A4 daily checklist listing every cleaning task by shift (opening, service, closing) with columns for task, method, initials, time, and manager sign-off.
Comprehensive schedule listing every cleaning task in the cafe with frequency (daily/weekly/monthly/quarterly), method, chemicals, dilution rate, responsible role, and verification method.
Standard Operating Procedure covering cleaning methods, chemical usage, dilution rates, equipment-specific procedures, and safety precautions for every cleaning task.
Area-by-area scoring template (1-5 scale) for weekly cleaning audits covering front-of-house, bar, kitchen, storage, and facilities with trend tracking.
Register listing every cleaning chemical used on premises with product name, hazard classification, correct dilution rate, safety precautions, first aid measures, and storage requirements.

failure_modes: Cleaning checklists are signed off without tasks actually being completed properly
Chemical dilution rates are ignored, resulting in either ineffective cleaning or dangerous concentration levels
Deep-clean tasks on the weekly/monthly schedule are repeatedly postponed during busy periods
New or temporary staff are not shown the cleaning schedule and miss tasks or use wrong products
kpis: 100% of daily cleaning checklist tasks completed and signed off every operating day
Weekly cleaning audit scores averaging 4 or above (out of 5) across all areas
Zero EHO cleaning-related findings at inspection
COSHH register complete and up to date with safety data sheets for every chemical on premises
constraints: All cleaning chemicals must be food-safe where they contact food preparation surfaces
COSHH safety data sheets must be available on-site for every chemical product used
Cleaning records must be retained for a minimum of 12 months for EHO inspection

time_load.L1: 6 hours (area audit, schedule creation, chemical review, COSHH file setup)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (SOP drafting, audit system setup, equipment procedures, training)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/month (audits, deep-clean events, product reviews)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily closing inspection by shift manager. Weekly cleaning audit with scored assessment. Monthly review of audit trends and deep-clean schedule compliance. Quarterly whole-team deep-clean event. Annual review of cleaning products and SOP updates.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a complete cleaning system covering every task, surface, and piece of equipment in the cafe, with daily checklists, proper chemical management, and regular audits. It provides the documented cleaning records EHOs require and keeps the cafe visibly and genuinely clean.
implementation_risks: Cleaning checklists become a box-ticking exercise — manager observation is essential
Chemical costs can be reduced by buying in bulk but storage safety must be maintained
High staff turnover requires continuous training on cleaning procedures
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate for all cafes. Flag as urgent if signals indicate dirty premises, missing cleaning records, EHO cleaning concerns, or pest issues. Foundation block for B009 (Handwashing) and B012 (Hygiene Culture).

blocks.name: Hygiene Culture System
blocks.purpose: Embeds hygiene as a core cultural norm within the cafe team, moving beyond checklists and compliance into a shared identity where cleanliness and food safety are points of pride. This block transforms hygiene from something staff are told to do into something they choose to do because they understand its importance.

detailed_objectives: Create a team culture where hygiene standards are maintained because of shared values, not just compliance pressure
Establish visible leadership commitment to hygiene from the owner and manager downwards
Develop peer accountability systems where team members hold each other to high standards
Make hygiene performance visible, celebrated, and rewarded
Integrate hygiene excellence into the cafe's brand identity and customer promise
secondary_objectives: Reduce the need for constant managerial oversight of hygiene tasks
Lower staff turnover by creating a workplace that staff are proud of
Build customer trust and loyalty through consistently high visible cleanliness

blocks.why_this_matters: The most common reason cafes fail EHO inspections or suffer food safety incidents is not a lack of knowledge or equipment — it is a lack of culture. A cafe can have a perfect cleaning schedule, comprehensive SOPs, and all the right equipment, but if the team does not genuinely care about hygiene, standards will slip the moment the manager's back is turned. The Food Standards Agency's confidence in management scoring (one of three pillars of the Food Hygiene Rating) specifically assesses whether the business has a food safety culture — not just systems, but evidence that those systems are actively followed and continuously improved. Research by the Chartered Institute of Environmental Health shows that businesses with strong food safety cultures achieve consistently higher hygiene ratings and have fewer enforcement actions. Building a hygiene culture is about psychology, not paperwork. It requires visible leadership — if the owner wipes down a table or picks up litter, it signals that no one is above hygiene tasks. It requires positive reinforcement — celebrating good practices rather than only punishing failures. It requires transparency — making hygiene performance visible to the team so everyone can see the standards being met. And it requires peer accountability — creating an environment where team members feel comfortable reminding each other about handwashing, cleaning, or uniform standards without fear of conflict. A strong hygiene culture becomes self-reinforcing: new starters absorb the standards from their colleagues, and those who do not share the team's values self-select out.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: The owner or manager holds a team meeting dedicated entirely to hygiene culture. The message is simple: 'Hygiene is not just a rule here — it is who we are. Every one of us is responsible for keeping this cafe clean and safe, and I expect the same standard from myself as I do from you.' This is not a lecture — it is a conversation. Ask the team: What do you think good hygiene looks like? What gets in the way of keeping standards high? What would help you do better? Listen to the answers. Immediately after the meeting, the owner should visibly model the behaviour: wipe down a counter, pick up litter, check the toilet cleanliness. Actions speak louder than policies. Introduce a daily 'hygiene huddle' — a 60-second check at the start of each shift where the team quickly looks around and identifies one thing that needs attention right now. This creates a habit of proactive cleanliness rather than reactive cleaning. Week 2: Create a simple hygiene scoreboard visible in the staff area showing daily cleaning completion rates. Keep it positive — highlight days where 100% was achieved. Introduce a 'hygiene champion of the week' recognition for the team member who consistently demonstrates the highest standards. This does not need a prize — simple recognition in front of peers is powerful.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Hygiene Culture Charter — a short document (one page maximum) that articulates the cafe's hygiene values, signed by every team member. Frame it positively: 'We take pride in a spotless cafe because our customers trust us with their health.' Post it in the staff area. Integrate hygiene expectations into job descriptions, interview questions, and performance reviews. When hiring, ask candidates about their approach to cleanliness and food safety. During probation reviews, assess hygiene habits alongside other competencies. Create a peer observation programme where team members regularly observe each other's hygiene practices and provide constructive feedback. This must be framed collaboratively, not punitively — 'I noticed you washed your hands before and after that task, great job' is more effective than 'You forgot to wash your hands.' Establish monthly hygiene meetings (15 minutes) where the team reviews cleaning audit scores, discusses any incidents or near-misses, and agrees on improvement actions. Track participation and engagement. By Week 8, hygiene should be a regular topic of team conversation, not something that only comes up when there is a problem.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The test of a strong hygiene culture is what happens when no one is watching. Conduct quarterly anonymous staff surveys asking: Do you feel the cafe maintains high hygiene standards? Do you feel comfortable raising hygiene concerns? Are hygiene expectations clear and consistent? Use the results to identify areas for cultural improvement. Share the cafe's Food Hygiene Rating proudly — display it prominently and celebrate with the team when a 5 rating is maintained or achieved. Consider inviting a local EHO or food safety consultant to visit and provide feedback — external validation reinforces the team's efforts. Review hygiene culture annually: are standards being maintained as the team changes? Are new starters absorbing the culture quickly? Adjust the approach based on evidence and team feedback.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Hold a dedicated team meeting on hygiene culture — conversation, not lecture
Owner visibly models hygiene behaviour immediately after the meeting
Introduce a daily 60-second hygiene huddle at the start of each shift
Create a hygiene scoreboard in the staff area showing daily completion rates
Launch a 'hygiene champion of the week' recognition programme
Ask the team what barriers exist to maintaining high hygiene standards
Address any barrier identified by the team within 48 hours

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft and sign a Hygiene Culture Charter with the entire team
Integrate hygiene expectations into job descriptions and interview questions
Include hygiene habits in probation reviews and performance assessments
Create a peer observation programme for constructive hygiene feedback
Establish monthly 15-minute hygiene meetings to review scores and discuss improvements
Track engagement and participation in hygiene initiatives
Display the Hygiene Culture Charter in the staff area

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly anonymous staff surveys on hygiene culture
Display the Food Hygiene Rating prominently and celebrate the score
Consider external food safety consultant visits for independent feedback
Review hygiene culture annually and adjust approach based on evidence
Ensure new starters absorb the culture through buddy systems and mentoring

tools.description: One-page document articulating the cafe's hygiene values and expectations, written in positive language and signed by every team member. Displayed in the staff area.
60-second protocol for the start-of-shift hygiene check: team visually scans the environment, identifies one thing to address, and commits to maintaining standards through the shift.
Visual display board for the staff area showing daily cleaning completion rates, weekly audit scores, and hygiene champion recognition. Designed to motivate through transparency.
Structured form for peer observation of hygiene practices, focused on positive feedback and constructive suggestions rather than criticism. Used in the peer observation programme.
Anonymous staff survey assessing perceptions of hygiene standards, comfort in raising concerns, clarity of expectations, and management commitment to hygiene culture.

failure_modes: Leadership fails to model hygiene behaviour, undermining the entire programme
Recognition programme feels forced or patronising, reducing engagement rather than building it
Peer observation becomes judgemental or cliquey, creating conflict rather than collaboration
Hygiene culture is treated as a one-off initiative rather than an ongoing commitment, and standards decay over time
kpis: Quarterly staff survey shows 80%+ agreement that hygiene standards are high and well-maintained
Daily hygiene huddle conducted at the start of every shift
Food Hygiene Rating maintained at 5 with EHO confidence in management scored at highest level
New starters demonstrate expected hygiene behaviours within their first week
constraints: Requires genuine leadership commitment — this cannot be delegated to junior staff
Culture change takes time — expect 3-6 months before the culture is self-sustaining
Must avoid creating a blame culture — the approach must be positive and supportive

time_load.L1: 3 hours (team meeting, scoreboard setup, huddle launch)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (charter drafting, peer observation setup, integration into HR processes)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (surveys, culture reviews, recognition updates)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily hygiene huddle at shift start. Weekly review of scoreboard and recognition. Monthly hygiene meeting with team. Quarterly anonymous culture survey. Annual comprehensive culture review and charter refresh.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block moves hygiene beyond checklists into a genuine team culture where cleanliness is a shared value. Through leadership modelling, peer accountability, recognition, and regular conversation, it makes hygiene something the team chooses to uphold rather than something they are forced to do.
implementation_risks: If leadership does not genuinely participate, staff will see the programme as hypocritical
Culture change is slow — resist the temptation to abandon the approach after a few weeks
New starters can disrupt established culture — buddy systems help maintain standards
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B009, B010, and B011 are in place. This block binds hygiene practices into culture. Flag as important if signals indicate staff apathy towards cleaning, inconsistent hygiene standards, or low EHO confidence in management scores.

blocks.name: HACCP Food Safety System
blocks.purpose: Documents the cafe's Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP) system, identifying every food safety hazard in the operation, assessing risk, and defining the critical control points where monitoring prevents unsafe food from reaching customers. This block is a legal requirement and the foundation of the entire food safety management system.

detailed_objectives: Conduct a full hazard analysis covering biological, chemical, and physical hazards at every stage of the food operation
Identify Critical Control Points (CCPs) where control is essential to prevent or reduce hazards to acceptable levels
Define critical limits, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions for each CCP
Create HACCP documentation that satisfies EHO inspection requirements and demonstrates due diligence
Establish a review process to keep the HACCP plan current as the menu, suppliers, or processes change
secondary_objectives: Use the HACCP framework to identify and eliminate food safety risks before they cause harm
Provide a structured approach to food safety that goes beyond the SFBB pack for larger or more complex operations
Build management confidence in the cafe's food safety controls

blocks.why_this_matters: HACCP is a legal requirement for all food businesses in the UK under EU-retained Regulation (EC) 852/2004, Article 5. The regulation requires food business operators to put in place, implement, and maintain a permanent procedure based on HACCP principles. For most small cafes, the Food Standards Agency's Safer Food Better Business (SFBB) pack serves as a simplified HACCP system, and EHOs will accept this for straightforward operations. However, cafes with more complex menus, those that cook from raw, or those aiming for best-in-class food safety should develop a more detailed HACCP plan. HACCP was originally developed by NASA to ensure the safety of food for astronauts — it is a systematic, science-based approach to identifying and controlling food safety hazards. The seven HACCP principles are: conduct a hazard analysis, determine CCPs, establish critical limits, establish monitoring procedures, establish corrective actions, establish verification procedures, and establish documentation. In a cafe context, typical CCPs include cooking temperatures (food must reach 75°C core temperature), chilling (food must cool to 8°C within 90 minutes), and hot holding (food must stay above 63°C). Without a documented HACCP plan, a cafe cannot demonstrate due diligence — the legal defence that shows the business took all reasonable precautions to prevent food safety failures. If a customer becomes ill and the business cannot produce its HACCP documentation, the owner faces personal criminal liability under the Food Safety Act 1990.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: If the cafe currently relies on the SFBB pack, review it to ensure all sections are completed, up to date, and reflect current operations. The SFBB pack is a valid HACCP-based system for simple cafe operations. Verify that the following SFBB sections are filled in: cross-contamination, cleaning, chilling, cooking, management, and the 4-weekly review diary. If the SFBB pack is incomplete, complete it immediately — this is the minimum legal requirement. For cafes with more complex operations (cooking from raw, hot food service, multi-site), begin a formal hazard analysis. List every food process step from delivery through storage, preparation, cooking, cooling, display, and service. At each step, identify potential biological hazards (bacteria, viruses), chemical hazards (cleaning chemicals, allergens), and physical hazards (glass, metal, plastic, hair). Week 2: Assess each hazard for severity and likelihood. Determine which steps are Critical Control Points — points where a control measure is essential to prevent unsafe food. For most cafes, CCPs include: delivery (checking temperatures), chilled storage (maintaining below 8°C), cooking (reaching 75°C core), cooling (reaching 8°C within 90 minutes), hot holding (maintaining above 63°C), and reheating (reaching 82°C in Scotland, 75°C in England). For each CCP, define the critical limit, how it will be monitored, and what corrective action will be taken if the limit is breached.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Document the complete HACCP plan in a formal document separate from (or in addition to) the SFBB pack. The document should include: a description of the food business, a process flow diagram showing every step from delivery to service, a hazard analysis table listing every hazard identified at each step, a CCP determination table, and individual CCP monitoring sheets. For each CCP, create a monitoring record — this is where daily CCP checks are logged. Link these to existing blocks: temperature monitoring (B001) covers the chilled storage CCP, cooking temperature checks cover the cooking CCP. Establish verification procedures: how does the business confirm that the HACCP system is working? This includes reviewing monitoring records, calibrating equipment, and conducting periodic food safety checks. Train the manager and any staff who monitor CCPs on HACCP principles — they must understand why they are checking, not just what number to write down. Create a HACCP review schedule: the plan must be reviewed whenever the menu changes, a new supplier is introduced, equipment is changed, a food safety incident occurs, or at minimum annually. By Week 8, the cafe should have a documented HACCP plan that covers every process, identifies all CCPs, and has monitoring and corrective action procedures in place.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The HACCP plan should be a living document, not a file that sits on a shelf. Conduct an annual comprehensive HACCP review involving the owner, manager, and kitchen lead. Walk through every process step and ask: has anything changed? Are the CCPs still valid? Are the critical limits still correct? Are the monitoring procedures working? Review corrective action records — have there been trends that suggest a systemic issue? Consider engaging a food safety consultant to independently review the HACCP plan every two years. Share HACCP principles with the wider team — even staff who do not monitor CCPs should understand how the system protects customers. Integrate HACCP documentation into the food safety record-keeping system (B016) so all records are centralised and easily accessible for EHO inspection.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Review and complete the SFBB pack if used as the primary HACCP system
List every food process step from delivery through to service
Identify biological, chemical, and physical hazards at each step
Determine which steps are Critical Control Points (CCPs)
Define critical limits, monitoring procedures, and corrective actions for each CCP
Begin recording CCP monitoring checks from Day 1
Ensure existing monitoring (temperature logs, cooking checks) is linked to the HACCP framework

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Document the complete HACCP plan with process flow diagram and hazard analysis tables
Create individual CCP monitoring record sheets
Establish verification procedures to confirm the HACCP system is working
Train the manager and CCP monitoring staff on HACCP principles
Create a HACCP review schedule triggered by menu changes, new suppliers, or incidents
Link HACCP documentation to existing blocks (B001 temperature, B005 allergens)
File the complete HACCP plan in the food safety documentation folder

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct an annual comprehensive HACCP review with owner involvement
Review corrective action trends to identify systemic issues
Consider biennial independent food safety consultant review
Share HACCP principles with the wider team for general awareness
Integrate HACCP into the centralised food safety record-keeping system (B016)

deliverables: HACCP Plan Document (hazard analysis, CCP determination, process flow)
Corrective Action Log linked to CCPs

tools.description: Structured worksheet for analysing biological, chemical, and physical hazards at each process step, including severity, likelihood, and control measures.
Decision matrix for determining whether a control point is a CCP, using the Codex Alimentarius decision tree approach. Documents critical limits and monitoring requirements.
Daily monitoring record for each CCP, capturing the measurement, critical limit, actual result, corrective action if needed, and staff initials.
Template for creating a visual process flow diagram showing every step from ingredient delivery through storage, preparation, cooking, cooling, display, and service.
Comprehensive checklist for the annual HACCP review, covering plan accuracy, CCP validity, monitoring effectiveness, corrective action trends, and documentation completeness.

failure_modes: HACCP plan is created but never reviewed or updated, becoming out of date as the menu and processes change
CCPs are identified but monitoring is inconsistent, leaving gaps in the safety net
Staff monitor CCPs without understanding the significance, treating it as a tick-box exercise
The HACCP plan is overly complex for a simple cafe operation, leading to confusion and non-compliance
kpis: HACCP plan fully documented and available for EHO inspection at all times
100% of CCP monitoring checks completed on schedule every operating day
Zero CCP breaches without documented corrective action
HACCP plan reviewed and updated within 7 days of any menu, supplier, or process change
constraints: HACCP is a legal requirement — all food businesses must have a documented food safety management system
The SFBB pack is acceptable for simple cafe operations but more complex operations need a formal HACCP plan
HACCP documentation must be retained for a minimum of 12 months and be available for EHO inspection

time_load.L1: 8 hours (SFBB review/completion, initial hazard analysis, CCP identification)
time_load.L2: 16 hours (full HACCP plan documentation, process flow, monitoring setup, staff training)
time_load.L3: 4 hours/year (annual review, consultant engagement, trend analysis)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily CCP monitoring record review by shift manager. Monthly HACCP compliance check. Review triggered by any menu change, new supplier, equipment change, or food safety incident. Annual comprehensive HACCP review with owner involvement.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block establishes the cafe's HACCP food safety system — a systematic analysis of every food safety hazard in the operation, identification of critical control points, and documented monitoring procedures. It is a legal requirement and the foundation upon which all other food safety blocks build.
implementation_risks: HACCP can feel academic and overwhelming for small cafe owners — start with the SFBB pack and build up
Over-identifying CCPs creates unnecessary monitoring burden — focus on genuinely critical points
The plan must be proportionate to the complexity of the operation
meta.notes_for_engine: Core foundation block — activate for all cafes. Flag as urgent if no HACCP/SFBB documentation exists, if EHO requests documentation, or if a food safety incident occurs. Links to B001, B005, B009 as prerequisite blocks.

blocks.name: Critical Control Point Monitoring
blocks.purpose: Provides the ongoing monitoring systems, corrective action procedures, and trend analysis for every Critical Control Point identified in the HACCP plan (B013). This block ensures that CCPs are not just identified on paper but actively monitored every day with clear escalation when limits are breached.

detailed_objectives: Implement real-time monitoring procedures for every CCP in the cafe's HACCP plan
Define clear corrective actions for each CCP when critical limits are breached
Create monitoring records that provide auditable evidence of ongoing CCP control
Establish trend analysis to identify CCPs that are drifting towards failure before they breach limits
Assign clear responsibility for CCP monitoring to specific roles on each shift
secondary_objectives: Reduce corrective action frequency by identifying and addressing root causes of CCP deviations
Build staff confidence in responding to CCP breaches with calm, documented procedures
Provide management with visibility of CCP performance across all shifts

blocks.why_this_matters: Identifying Critical Control Points in a HACCP plan is only half the task — the other half is monitoring them consistently and responding correctly when limits are breached. A CCP that is identified but not monitored provides no protection whatsoever. In the UK cafe context, the most common CCPs are cold storage temperatures (below 8°C, best practice below 5°C), cooking temperatures (core temperature of 75°C or above), cooling times (from 63°C to 8°C within 90 minutes), hot holding temperatures (above 63°C), and reheating temperatures (above 75°C, or 82°C in Scotland). Each of these CCPs has a critical limit — a measurable threshold that separates safe from unsafe. When that limit is breached, corrective action must be taken immediately, documented, and reviewed. An EHO inspector assessing confidence in management will look specifically for evidence of CCP monitoring: are checks being done? Are they being done at the right frequency? When a breach occurred, was the corrective action appropriate and timely? Was the affected food disposed of or made safe? Were the records completed? A cafe that can show weeks of consistent CCP monitoring with documented corrective actions demonstrates the highest level of food safety management. Conversely, gaps in monitoring records suggest that the HACCP system exists only on paper. CCP monitoring is the daily discipline that turns a food safety plan into actual food safety.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: For each CCP identified in the HACCP plan (B013), create a simple monitoring record sheet. At minimum, most cafes will have these CCPs: delivery temperature checks (verify chilled goods arrive below 8°C, frozen below -18°C), cold storage monitoring (twice-daily fridge/freezer temperature checks — link to B001), and cooking temperature checks (probe internal temperature of cooked food to confirm 75°C or above). For each CCP, the monitoring sheet should record: date, time, what was checked, the critical limit, the actual reading, whether it passed or failed, corrective action taken if it failed, and the initials of the person who did the check. Assign CCP monitoring responsibilities: delivery checks to whoever receives goods, temperature monitoring to opening and afternoon shift leads, cooking checks to the kitchen team. Week 2: Create a laminated corrective action guide for each CCP and post it in the relevant area. For example, near the fridge: 'If temperature exceeds 8°C: 1. Do not use food from this unit until investigated. 2. Check the door seal. 3. Check if the unit was left open. 4. Inform the manager. 5. If temperature exceeds 15°C, discard all high-risk food. 6. Record the incident on the corrective action log.' Practise one corrective action scenario with the team so they know the procedure is real, not theoretical.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Consolidate all CCP monitoring into a single daily food safety record — a document or digital form that captures every CCP check for the day in one place. This makes it easy for the manager to review all CCP performance at a glance and easy for EHOs to inspect. Develop detailed corrective action SOPs for each CCP breach type: temperature excursion, undercooked food, delayed cooling, hot holding failure, and suspect delivery. Each SOP should include: immediate action, assessment of affected food, decision on disposal or recovery, documentation requirements, root cause investigation, and preventive action. Implement a weekly CCP trend analysis: are any CCPs showing patterns that suggest drift towards failure? For example, if a fridge consistently reads 6-7°C (within limits but trending high), schedule maintenance before it fails. Create a monthly CCP performance summary for the manager showing compliance rates, breach frequency, corrective actions taken, and any recurring issues. Train all staff who monitor CCPs to understand what a critical limit means and why immediate action is essential — not just what number to write down, but what that number signifies.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). CCP monitoring should be automatic and precise, requiring no reminders or prompting. Conduct quarterly CCP verification audits: the manager independently checks a sample of CCP monitoring records against actual conditions. Are the recorded temperatures consistent with what the probe shows right now? Are corrective actions documented appropriately? Consider digital monitoring solutions such as Bluetooth temperature loggers that record continuously and alert staff when limits are approached. Analyse corrective action data annually: what are the most common CCP breaches? Can any be eliminated through equipment upgrades, process changes, or better training? Share CCP compliance rates with the team and celebrate sustained 100% compliance periods.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Create monitoring record sheets for each CCP identified in the HACCP plan
Assign CCP monitoring responsibilities to specific roles on each shift
Begin daily CCP monitoring checks with documented results
Create laminated corrective action guides for each CCP location
Practise one corrective action scenario with the team
Ensure probe thermometers are available and calibrated for CCP checks
Review delivery checking procedures against CCP requirements

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Consolidate all CCP monitoring into a single daily food safety record
Develop detailed corrective action SOPs for each CCP breach type
Implement weekly CCP trend analysis to detect drift towards failure
Create a monthly CCP performance summary report
Train all CCP monitoring staff on the significance of critical limits
Establish root cause investigation procedures for CCP breaches
File all monitoring records and corrective action logs in the food safety folder

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly CCP verification audits
Evaluate digital monitoring solutions for continuous CCP tracking
Analyse annual corrective action data to eliminate recurring breach causes
Share CCP compliance rates with the team and celebrate sustained performance
Review and refine monitoring procedures annually based on operational experience

deliverables: Corrective Action SOP for each CCP breach type

tools.description: Consolidated daily form capturing every CCP check: date, time, CCP name, critical limit, actual reading, pass/fail, corrective action if needed, and initials.
Laminated step-by-step guide posted at each CCP location, detailing immediate actions when a critical limit is breached, including assessment, food disposal decisions, and documentation.
Detailed Standard Operating Procedure for each CCP breach type: temperature excursion, undercooked food, cooling failure, hot holding breach. Covers immediate action through root cause analysis.
Monthly summary showing CCP compliance rates, breach frequency and type, corrective actions taken, trend analysis, and any recurring issues requiring management attention.

failure_modes: CCP checks are recorded but not actually performed — fabricated records provide false assurance
Corrective actions are taken but not documented, leaving no auditable evidence for EHO inspection

kpis: 100% of scheduled CCP checks completed and documented every operating day
Corrective action initiated within 15 minutes of any CCP breach
All CCP breaches documented with root cause analysis completed within 24 hours
Monthly CCP compliance rate sustained above 98%

Probe thermometers must be calibrated and available for all temperature-related CCP checks
Monitoring records must be retained for a minimum of 12 months

time_load.L1: 4 hours (monitoring sheet creation, corrective action guides, staff training)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (consolidated form development, SOP drafting, trend analysis setup)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (verification audits, data analysis, system refinement)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily review of CCP monitoring records by shift manager. Weekly trend analysis of CCP data. Monthly CCP performance summary reviewed by manager. Quarterly verification audit by owner. Immediate review triggered by any CCP breach.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block ensures every Critical Control Point identified in the HACCP plan is actively monitored every day, with clear corrective actions when limits are breached and trend analysis to catch problems before they become failures. It turns the HACCP plan from a document into daily practice.
implementation_risks: Monitoring fatigue — staff may become complacent with repetitive CCP checks over time
Equipment failure (e.g., probe thermometer) can prevent CCP monitoring — always have backups
Over-reliance on paper records — consider digital solutions for real-time CCP visibility
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate immediately after B013 (HACCP plan) is in place. Flag as urgent if signals indicate missed CCP checks, undocumented corrective actions, or EHO concerns about monitoring frequency.

blocks.name: Supplier Due Diligence
blocks.purpose: Establishes a systematic process for verifying that all food and ingredient suppliers meet the cafe's food safety standards, hold appropriate certifications, and provide reliable allergen and traceability information. This block ensures the cafe's food safety chain is only as strong as its weakest supplier.

detailed_objectives: Create an approved supplier list with documented food safety credentials for every supplier
Verify that each supplier holds appropriate food hygiene registration and certification
Obtain and file allergen information, specifications, and safety data for all supplied products
Establish delivery inspection procedures to verify product safety on arrival
Implement a supplier review process to maintain standards over time
secondary_objectives: Enable full traceability from supplier to customer for any food product sold
Reduce the risk of contaminated or mislabelled products entering the cafe's supply chain
Build relationships with suppliers based on shared food safety commitments

blocks.why_this_matters: The Food Safety Act 1990 establishes the 'due diligence' defence — a food business can defend itself against food safety prosecution by demonstrating that it took all reasonable precautions and exercised all due diligence to avoid the offence. Supplier due diligence is a critical component of this defence. If a cafe sells a sandwich containing an undeclared allergen because the bread supplier changed their recipe without notification, the cafe is legally liable — but if it can demonstrate that it had a system for checking supplier credentials and receiving formulation change notifications, this supports a due diligence defence. EU-retained Regulation (EC) 178/2002 (the General Food Law) requires food businesses to be able to identify their suppliers and to implement traceability systems — they must be able to trace any ingredient one step back (to the supplier) and one step forward (to the customer). For a cafe, this means knowing exactly where every ingredient comes from and being able to recall any product if a safety issue is identified. The 2013 horsemeat scandal demonstrated how supply chain failures can damage entire industries. While most cafes source from reputable wholesalers, even established suppliers can have lapses. A cafe that does not check its suppliers' food safety credentials, verify delivery temperatures, or maintain up-to-date product specifications is operating blind to supply chain risks. EHO inspectors increasingly ask to see evidence of supplier due diligence, particularly in relation to allergen information and traceability records.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: List every supplier the cafe uses — this includes food wholesalers, bakery suppliers, milk suppliers, coffee roasters, specialist ingredient suppliers, and any third-party providers of ready-made items like cakes or sandwiches. For each supplier, record: company name, contact details, what they supply, delivery frequency, and current documentation held. Request the following from each supplier: their food hygiene registration number, evidence of their food safety management system, allergen information for every product supplied, and confirmation of their traceability procedures. Most reputable suppliers will have this information readily available. Week 2: Create an Approved Supplier Register — a document listing every approved supplier with their credentials, documentation status, and last review date. Any supplier who cannot provide basic food safety documentation should be flagged for review — consider whether an alternative supplier with better documentation is available. Establish a delivery checking procedure: every delivery must be checked on arrival for temperature compliance (chilled goods below 8°C, frozen below -18°C), packaging integrity, use-by dates, and general condition. Reject any delivery that does not meet these standards and document the rejection.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Supplier Due Diligence SOP covering the complete supplier approval process: initial assessment, documentation requirements, ongoing monitoring, and review criteria. Create a supplier questionnaire that new suppliers must complete before being approved — this should cover their food safety management system, allergen controls, traceability procedures, recall procedures, and insurance. Establish a supplier specification file containing the product specification (ingredients, allergens, storage requirements, shelf life) for every product purchased. This is essential for PPDS labelling (B006) and allergen management (B005). Implement a supplier performance tracking system: record any delivery issues (temperature failures, damaged goods, incorrect products, short shelf life), and review supplier performance quarterly. Set up a formal communication channel with suppliers for formulation change notifications — request that all suppliers inform you in writing before any change to ingredients, allergens, or packaging. File this agreement and hold suppliers accountable. By Week 8, every supplier should be documented, approved, and actively monitored.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Conduct annual supplier reviews where you assess each supplier's ongoing food safety credentials, delivery performance, and responsiveness. Visit key suppliers where practical — seeing their premises gives you confidence in their operations and strengthens the relationship. Review the Approved Supplier Register annually: are all suppliers still meeting standards? Are there better alternatives? Has any supplier had enforcement action taken against them? Monitor food safety news and alerts from the FSA for any issues related to your suppliers or their products. Participate in any supplier food safety audit programmes offered by your wholesalers. Consider requesting third-party audit certificates (BRC, SALSA) from suppliers as a higher standard of assurance.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: List every supplier used by the cafe with contact details and products supplied
Request food safety documentation from each supplier: registration, allergen data, specifications
Create an Approved Supplier Register with documentation status for each supplier
Establish a delivery checking procedure: temperature, packaging, dates, condition
Reject and document any delivery failing to meet food safety standards
Flag suppliers unable to provide basic food safety documentation
Begin filing supplier documentation in a dedicated supplier folder

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft a Supplier Due Diligence SOP covering approval, monitoring, and review
Create a new supplier questionnaire covering food safety systems and allergen controls
Build a supplier specification file for every product purchased
Implement quarterly supplier performance tracking based on delivery issues
Establish formal written agreements for formulation change notifications
Link supplier allergen data to the cafe's allergen matrix (B005) and PPDS system (B006)
File all supplier documentation, specifications, and correspondence

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct annual supplier reviews assessing credentials, performance, and responsiveness
Visit key suppliers to observe their food safety practices
Monitor FSA food alerts and recall notices for supplier-related issues
Review the Approved Supplier Register annually for currency and quality
Consider requesting third-party audit certificates (BRC, SALSA) from key suppliers

tools.description: Central register listing every approved supplier with company details, products supplied, food safety credentials, documentation held, approval date, and next review date.
Checklist for inspecting every delivery on arrival: temperature compliance, packaging integrity, use-by dates, product condition, correct quantities, and delivery documentation.
Assessment questionnaire for evaluating new suppliers before approval: food safety management system, allergen controls, traceability, recall procedures, and insurance details.

failure_modes: Cafe sources products from an unapproved supplier (e.g., a local baker without documentation) without checking credentials

kpis: 100% of suppliers listed on the Approved Supplier Register with current documentation
