Owner focuses exclusively on pay as a retention lever while ignoring culture, management quality, and scheduling fairness
Exit interviews are not conducted or data is not analysed for patterns, so the same problems drive repeated departures
kpis: Annual voluntary turnover rate below 25% (sector average is approximately 30%)
Average team tenure above 12 months
Team pulse survey scores improving or stable quarter on quarter
90% of exit interviews completed for all departing staff
Employer Net Promoter Score above +20
constraints: Recognition programmes must be applied fairly and consistently to avoid perceptions of favouritism
Pulse surveys must be genuinely anonymous to generate honest responses
Retention strategies must complement, not replace, fair compensation per B065

time_load.L1: 4 hours (survey creation, one-to-ones, quick-win fixes)
time_load.L2: 10 hours (recognition programme, stay interviews, dashboard build, social calendar)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/month (survey analysis, culture council, milestone celebrations)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly review of turnover and tenure data. Quarterly pulse survey deployment and analysis. Six-monthly stay interviews for all staff. Annual engagement survey with year-on-year comparison. Immediate review triggered when two or more staff leave within a 30-day period.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block builds a retention-focused culture that goes beyond pay, using recognition, feedback loops, stay interviews, and team-building to create a workplace people actively choose to remain part of.
implementation_risks: Culture initiatives can feel forced if not authentically championed by the owner
Small teams may find anonymous surveys difficult to keep truly anonymous
Social events require budget and time that feel hard to justify when margins are tight
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate high turnover, team morale issues, or multiple departures in quick succession. Strong interdependency with B063 (workforce planning), B064 (recruitment), and B065 (compensation).

blocks.name: New Starter Induction
blocks.purpose: Defines a structured first-week induction programme for all new starters, covering essential orientation, health and safety basics, team introductions, and role-specific fundamentals so that every new team member feels welcome, informed, and safe from day one.

detailed_objectives: Ensure every new starter completes a consistent induction covering venue layout, emergency procedures, and key policies
Provide a welcoming first-day experience that reduces anxiety and builds immediate belonging
Cover all legally required induction elements including health and safety, allergen awareness, and right-to-work documentation
Introduce the new starter to every team member and key operational areas within the first week
Set clear expectations for the first 30 days including learning milestones and support available
secondary_objectives: Reduce early turnover by ensuring new starters feel supported rather than overwhelmed
Shorten the time to productivity by providing structured rather than ad-hoc learning
Create a positive first impression that reinforces the hiring decision

blocks.why_this_matters: The first week in a new job shapes everything that follows. Research from the CIPD shows that up to 20% of staff turnover happens within the first 45 days of employment, and the primary reasons are feeling unsupported, unclear about expectations, or socially isolated. In a busy cafe environment, it is tempting to throw a new starter straight onto the floor with a brief tour and hope they pick things up — but this approach fails consistently. A barista who is not shown where the first aid kit is, how to use the fire extinguisher, or what to do if a customer reports an allergic reaction is both a safety risk and a legal liability. Under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 and the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999, employers have a duty to provide adequate information, instruction, training, and supervision to ensure employee safety. Allergen training is a legal requirement under the Food Information Regulations 2014 and Natasha's Law. Right-to-work checks must be completed before or on the first day of employment to avoid civil penalties of up to £45,000 per illegal worker under the Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006. Beyond compliance, a structured induction dramatically accelerates productivity. A new barista who spends their first three days following a clear induction plan — rather than standing awkwardly waiting to be told what to do — reaches competence two to three weeks faster than one left to figure things out alone.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Create a one-page New Starter First Day Checklist covering: right-to-work document verification, employment contract signed, emergency contact collected, tour of venue (front of house, back of house, storage, fire exits, first aid, staff area), introduction to all on-shift team members, review of key policies (attendance, dress code, mobile phone use, food safety basics), location of SFBB diary and cleaning schedules, and allocation of locker/uniform. Prepare a welcome pack for each new starter: printed rota for their first two weeks, name of their designated buddy, key contact numbers, and a simple map of the venue showing stations, fire exits, and safety equipment. Week 2: For the first five shifts, assign a buddy — an experienced team member who shadows the new starter, answers questions, and provides real-time guidance. The buddy should be chosen for their patience and teaching ability, not just seniority. Each shift should end with a 10-minute debrief between the buddy and the new starter covering what went well, what was confusing, and what to focus on tomorrow. By the end of Week 1, the new starter should be able to independently navigate the venue, follow basic hygiene protocols, and handle simple customer interactions under supervision.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a comprehensive Induction Pack document covering all first-week content in a structured format that can be reused for every new hire. Include a signed acknowledgement page for health and safety induction, allergen awareness, fire evacuation procedure, and key policies — this creates the audit trail required for due diligence. Build an induction training schedule template that maps out learning objectives for each of the first five shifts, allocating time for observation, practice, and assessment. Create a buddy guide — a short document for designated buddies explaining their role, how to give constructive feedback, common new-starter anxieties, and when to escalate concerns to management. Establish a Day 5 check-in meeting between the new starter and their manager to review progress against the induction checklist, address any concerns, and confirm the training plan for weeks two to four. Integrate induction records into the staff file so that completion of each element is documented and retrievable for inspection or audit purposes.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Refine the induction programme based on feedback from recent new starters — conduct a short survey at the one-month mark asking what was most helpful, what was missing, and how welcomed they felt. Use this feedback to continuously improve the programme. Develop a pre-start engagement process: send new starters a welcome message, a brief introduction to the team, and practical information (parking, dress code, what to bring) before their first day so they arrive feeling prepared. Create video walkthroughs of key processes (how to open the till, how to use the espresso machine, allergen checking procedure) that new starters can review on their phone before or after shifts. Track time to competence as a metric — how many shifts until a new starter can work independently at each station? Use this data to identify whether induction improvements are accelerating readiness. Benchmark your induction process against best practice from organisations like the SCA or hospitality industry bodies.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Create a New Starter First Day Checklist covering all essential induction elements
Prepare a welcome pack template with rota, buddy name, contacts, and venue map
Designate a buddy pool of experienced staff willing and able to mentor new starters
Ensure right-to-work document checking process is clear and compliant
Set up end-of-shift debrief routine between buddy and new starter for first five shifts
Verify that fire exit, first aid, and emergency information is current and visible
Schedule a Day 5 check-in meeting between manager and new starter

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a comprehensive Induction Pack document with signed acknowledgements
Build an induction training schedule template mapping learning objectives per shift
Create a buddy guide for designated mentors
Integrate induction records into the staff file system
Establish a one-month feedback survey for new starters on the induction experience
Review and update the induction programme after every three new hires
Link induction completion to probation review milestones in B069

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Implement pre-start engagement with welcome messages and practical information
Develop video walkthroughs of key processes for self-directed learning
Track time to competence as a metric and use data to refine induction
Benchmark induction against SCA and hospitality industry best practice
Conduct annual review of entire induction programme with buddy and new-starter feedback

deliverables: New Starter First Day Checklist (printable A4)
Welcome Pack template with venue map and key information

tools.description: Comprehensive first-day checklist covering right-to-work verification, contract signing, venue tour, safety briefing, team introductions, policy review, and equipment allocation. Signed by new starter and manager.
Five-shift training plan mapping specific learning objectives, observation tasks, practice activities, and buddy debriefs for each of the new starter's first five shifts.
Guide for designated buddies covering their role and responsibilities, how to give constructive feedback, common new-starter anxieties, and escalation triggers for management involvement.
Formal record of all induction elements completed with signed acknowledgements for health and safety, allergen awareness, fire procedures, and key policies. Filed in the staff record.
Short survey deployed at the one-month mark asking new starters to rate and comment on their induction experience, buddy support, information quality, and sense of welcome.

failure_modes: Induction is skipped or rushed because the cafe is busy, leaving new starters unsafe and overwhelmed
Right-to-work checks are not completed on or before the first day, creating significant legal exposure
Buddy is assigned but given no guidance or time, resulting in inconsistent mentoring quality
Health and safety induction is not documented, removing the employer's defence of due diligence in the event of an incident
kpis: 100% of new starters complete the full induction checklist within their first five shifts
Right-to-work verification completed on or before Day 1 for every new hire
New starter turnover within first 45 days below 10%
New starter feedback survey scores averaging 4 out of 5 or above
Time to independent competence reducing quarter on quarter
constraints: Right-to-work checks are a legal requirement before or on the first day of employment
Health and safety induction must comply with the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
Allergen awareness training is mandatory under the Food Information Regulations 2014
Induction records must be retained for the duration of employment plus a reasonable period

time_load.L1: 3 hours (checklist creation, welcome pack, buddy briefing)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (induction pack, training schedule, buddy guide, record system)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (feedback analysis, programme refinement, video creation)

blocks.review_protocol: Review induction checklist after every new hire for completeness. Monthly review of buddy feedback and new-starter debrief notes. Quarterly analysis of new-starter feedback surveys. Six-monthly update of induction content to reflect operational changes. Annual full programme review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a structured first-week induction for every new starter covering safety, orientation, team introductions, and role basics, ensuring people feel welcome, informed, and safe from their very first shift.
implementation_risks: Small cafes may struggle to allocate buddy time during busy periods
Induction quality varies if multiple managers run the process without a standard template
Documentation requirements can feel burdensome but are essential for legal compliance
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate immediately for any new hire. Cross-reference with B068 for barista-specific training, B069 for probation framework, and B070 for onboarding excellence assessment.

blocks.name: Barista Training & Certification
blocks.purpose: Establishes a structured barista training programme covering coffee craft fundamentals through to advanced skills, aligned to SCA certification standards where appropriate, ensuring every team member can consistently produce excellent coffee and confidently explain the product to customers.

detailed_objectives: Define a tiered barista training curriculum from foundation (espresso basics, milk steaming) through to advanced (latte art, brewing methods, sensory skills)
Establish internal competency assessments so that progression is based on demonstrated skill, not just time served
Align training content with SCA Coffee Skills Programme modules where the venue aspires to specialty standards
Ensure all baristas can confidently discuss coffee origin, roast profile, and flavour notes with customers
Create a training schedule that delivers skill development without disrupting service operations
secondary_objectives: Build a culture of coffee excellence that attracts passionate baristas to the venue
Reduce coffee waste by improving extraction consistency and reducing shots pulled incorrectly
Enable the venue to justify premium pricing through demonstrably skilled preparation

blocks.why_this_matters: Coffee is the core product of any cafe, and the barista's skill is the single biggest variable in whether a customer receives an excellent drink or a mediocre one. The difference between a properly extracted espresso and a poorly pulled shot is enormous — yet many cafes offer no structured training beyond 'watch someone else and have a go.' In the specialty coffee sector, customer expectations are high and rising. A flat white made with badly steamed milk or an over-extracted espresso served without comment will drive discerning customers to competitors. The Specialty Coffee Association's Coffee Skills Programme provides an internationally recognised framework covering barista skills, brewing, sensory skills, green coffee, and roasting — and even if a cafe does not pursue formal SCA certification, the curriculum provides an excellent structure for internal training. Training also has direct financial impact. A barista who consistently dials in the grinder correctly wastes less coffee. One who understands milk texturing uses less milk per drink. One who can explain the single-origin filter offering upsells a higher-margin product. Across a year, these small improvements compound into thousands of pounds of saved cost and increased revenue. Beyond the product, training is one of the strongest retention tools available. Baristas who feel they are developing their craft stay longer. A cafe that invests in SCA certifications or internal skill badges signals that it takes both its coffee and its people seriously. In a sector where the best baristas can choose where they work, the venues that invest in development win the talent war.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Assess the current skill level of every barista using a simple practical test: pull three espresso shots, steam milk for a flat white, and make a pour-over or batch brew if applicable. Rate each person on extraction quality, milk texture, speed, cleanliness, and customer interaction. This gives a baseline. Create a Barista Skills Checklist covering the fundamentals every team member must master: espresso machine operation, grinder adjustment, dose and yield targets, milk steaming to appropriate temperature and texture, basic drink recipes for the full menu, till operation for coffee orders, and cleaning routines for the coffee station. Week 2: Pair each less-experienced barista with the strongest barista on shift for at least two supervised training sessions. Focus on the basics that have the biggest quality impact: consistent dose weight, appropriate extraction time (typically 25-32 seconds for espresso), and milk steamed to 60-65°C with a microfoam texture. Post the venue's espresso recipe card (dose, yield, time) next to the grinder so every barista pulls to the same standard. Ensure all baristas understand and can articulate the current coffee offering — what roaster, what blend or single origin, what flavour notes, and what brewing methods are available.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a three-tier internal barista training programme: Foundation (espresso basics, milk steaming, menu execution, hygiene), Intermediate (grinder calibration, latte art basics, filter brewing methods, sensory vocabulary), and Advanced (advanced latte art, cupping and sensory skills, guest coffee preparation, training others). Create a Barista Training Log that tracks each person's progress through the tiers with sign-off from a senior barista or manager upon completion of each module. Establish a weekly 30-minute training session — this could be a cupping of the current coffee, a latte art practice session, or a deep dive into a brewing variable. Protect this time in the rota. Investigate SCA certification options: the SCA Foundation Barista Skills course is typically a one-day course costing £150-250 and provides an internationally recognised credential. Consider certifying at least one senior barista who can then train others internally. Document standard operating procedures for all coffee preparation — espresso recipe, milk steaming protocol, filter brew ratios, cleaning and maintenance schedules for grinder and machine. These SOPs ensure consistency regardless of who is on shift.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Pursue SCA certification systematically — aim for at least two team members holding SCA Barista Skills Foundation, with one progressing to Intermediate. Use this as a recruitment differentiator and a quality benchmark. Introduce internal barista competitions — monthly latte art throwdowns or blind cupping challenges that make skill development fun and social. Establish a coffee education programme for the whole team, including non-barista staff, so that everyone can talk confidently about the coffee when customers ask. Bring in guest trainers — roasters, Q-graders, or experienced baristas from other venues — for quarterly masterclass sessions. Track coffee quality metrics alongside training: extraction yield, customer complaints about coffee, waste from remakes, and average time per drink. Correlate improvements in these metrics with training investment. Create a mentoring pathway where Advanced-tier baristas are responsible for training Foundation-tier starters, building leadership skills and succession planning simultaneously.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Assess current barista skill levels using a practical espresso and milk test
Create a Barista Skills Checklist covering all foundation competencies
Post the venue's espresso recipe card at the grinder station
Pair less-experienced baristas with senior baristas for supervised training
Ensure all baristas can articulate the current coffee offering to customers
Review and clean all coffee equipment to ensure it supports consistent extraction
Establish baseline waste data for coffee and milk to measure future improvements

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a three-tier barista training programme (Foundation, Intermediate, Advanced)
Create a Barista Training Log tracking individual progress and sign-offs
Establish weekly 30-minute protected training sessions in the rota
Document coffee preparation SOPs for espresso, milk, filter, and cleaning
Investigate and budget for SCA Foundation Barista Skills certification
Set up a cupping station for regular sensory training
Link training tier progression to pay bands in B065

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Pursue SCA certification for senior baristas
Launch monthly internal barista competitions (latte art, cupping)
Establish quarterly guest trainer masterclass sessions
Track coffee quality metrics and correlate with training investment
Build a mentoring pathway from Advanced to Foundation tier
Extend coffee education to all front-of-house staff

deliverables: Barista Training Log for individual progress tracking
Coffee Preparation SOP set (espresso, milk, filter, cleaning)
Espresso Recipe Card for grinder station

tools.description: Practical assessment form scoring espresso extraction, milk steaming, drink presentation, speed, cleanliness, and customer communication. Used for baseline assessment and tier progression sign-off.
Individual training record tracking progress through Foundation, Intermediate, and Advanced tiers with module completion dates, assessor sign-offs, and notes on areas for development.
Standard operating procedures covering espresso recipe (dose, yield, time), milk steaming protocol (temperature, texture), filter brew ratios, equipment cleaning, and grinder maintenance schedules.
Template for planning weekly 30-minute training sessions including topic, learning objective, equipment needed, and follow-up practice tasks for participants.
Monthly tracker capturing extraction consistency, coffee waste volume, milk waste, customer coffee complaints, average time per drink, and correlation with training activity.

failure_modes: Training is promised during recruitment but never delivered, causing disillusionment and turnover
Only the head barista receives training, creating a single point of failure for coffee quality
Training sessions are consistently cancelled due to being too busy, signalling that development is not a priority
SOPs exist but are not followed, resulting in drink quality varying wildly between baristas and shifts
kpis: 100% of baristas achieve Foundation tier competency within their first 30 days
At least one team member holding SCA Foundation Barista Skills certification
Weekly training sessions completed at least 80% of scheduled weeks per quarter
Coffee waste (remade drinks and discarded shots) below 5% of total coffee used
Customer satisfaction scores for coffee quality at 4.5 out of 5 or above
constraints: SCA certification courses require external providers and have associated costs (£150-250 for Foundation)
Training sessions require protected time in the rota which impacts labour scheduling
Coffee equipment must be properly maintained to support training — poorly maintained machines make consistent extraction impossible

time_load.L1: 4 hours (skills assessment, checklist creation, recipe card, initial training pairs)
time_load.L2: 12 hours (curriculum development, SOP writing, training log setup, SCA research)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/week (training sessions, competitions, quality tracking)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily check of espresso recipe compliance by shift lead. Weekly training session delivery tracked by manager. Monthly review of training log progress for all baristas. Quarterly assessment of coffee quality metrics. Six-monthly review of training programme content against SCA standards and customer feedback.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a structured barista training programme from foundation skills through to advanced craft, aligned with SCA standards, ensuring consistent coffee quality and providing meaningful development that retains talented baristas.
implementation_risks: Head barista may resist structured training if they learned informally and see formal programmes as unnecessary
SCA certification costs may be difficult to justify for very small venues
Training quality depends on the trainer — investing in train-the-trainer skills is essential
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate for any venue where coffee quality signals are weak, barista turnover is high, or the venue aspires to specialty positioning. Links to B067 for general induction context and feeds into B071 career progression.

blocks.name: Probation Review Framework
blocks.purpose: Establishes a structured probation review process with formal check-ins at 30, 60, and 90 days, ensuring new starters receive clear feedback, documented performance assessment, and a fair decision on confirmation of employment — while protecting the business from retaining poor fits.

detailed_objectives: Define a probation period length and structure that is clearly communicated in the employment contract
Create a 30/60/90-day review schedule with specific assessment criteria for each milestone
Ensure probation reviews are documented, creating a fair and defensible record of performance assessment
Provide new starters with structured feedback so they understand expectations and areas for improvement
Establish a clear decision-making process for confirming, extending, or ending employment at probation conclusion
secondary_objectives: Identify training needs early so they can be addressed before probation ends
Reduce the cost of bad hires by making timely decisions rather than letting poor performers drift
Demonstrate fair employment practices that comply with ACAS guidance on probation periods

blocks.why_this_matters: Probation periods are one of the most underused management tools in small cafes. Many owners either skip probation entirely or set a three-month period in the contract and then forget about it until month four, at which point the employee is confirmed by default. This is a missed opportunity on both sides. A well-run probation process protects the business by creating a structured window to assess whether a new hire genuinely fits the role, the team, and the culture — with a lower-risk exit route if they do not. It also protects the employee by guaranteeing they receive regular feedback, clear expectations, and genuine support to succeed. Under UK employment law, employees generally cannot claim ordinary unfair dismissal until they have two years' continuous service (Employment Rights Act 1996, s.108), which gives employers flexibility during the probation period. However, ACAS guidance strongly recommends that probation dismissals follow a fair process including clear expectations, documented feedback, and an opportunity to improve. Discrimination claims under the Equality Act 2010 have no qualifying service period, so probation processes must be applied consistently and without bias. A structured 30/60/90-day framework transforms probation from a forgotten contractual clause into an active management process. The 30-day review catches early issues — is the person turning up on time, learning the basics, fitting into the team? The 60-day review assesses developing competence — can they work a station independently, handle customer interactions, follow SOPs? The 90-day review is the confirmation decision — has this person earned their place on the team?

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Review all current employment contracts to confirm that probation periods are clearly stated, including length (typically three or six months for cafe roles), notice period during probation (usually one week), and the process for extension or termination. If contracts do not include probation terms, draft an updated template with legal guidance. Create a simple Probation Review Form with three sections: what is going well, what needs improvement, and agreed actions for the next period. This single form is used at 30, 60, and 90 days with progressive expectations at each stage. Week 2: For any staff currently in their probation period, schedule their next review meeting immediately. Prepare by reviewing their induction checklist completion (B067), any training assessments (B068), attendance record, and feedback from their buddy or shift leads. Conduct the review as a supportive two-way conversation — not an interrogation. Document the outcome, have both parties sign, and file in the staff record. Set calendar reminders for every new hire's 30, 60, and 90-day review dates at the point of hiring so that reviews are never missed.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Probation Assessment Framework with specific, measurable criteria for each review stage. At 30 days: attendance and punctuality, basic hygiene compliance, venue orientation complete, team integration, attitude and willingness to learn. At 60 days: independent operation of at least one station, consistent menu execution, customer interaction confidence, adherence to SOPs, proactive communication. At 90 days: reliable independent performance across required stations, contribution to team culture, problem-solving and initiative, readiness for confirmation. Create a probation tracker that shows all current probationers, their review dates, and status — this prevents anyone falling through the cracks. Draft a Probation Extension Letter template for cases where performance is borderline — an extension (typically one to three months) with specific targets gives the employee a fair chance to improve while protecting the business. Develop a Probation Termination Process aligned with ACAS guidance: a meeting with the employee, clear explanation of the reasons, written confirmation, and the contractual notice period honoured. Train all managers on how to conduct probation reviews fairly, consistently, and with proper documentation.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Analyse probation data to identify patterns — are certain roles, shifts, or managers associated with higher probation failure rates? This data feeds back into recruitment (B064) and induction (B067) improvements. Introduce a probation success celebration — when someone passes their 90-day review, recognise it in the team briefing and on the Wall of Wins. This signals that completing probation is an achievement worth marking. Survey staff who have recently completed probation on the quality of the process — was feedback useful, were expectations clear, did they feel supported? Use this to refine the framework. Benchmark probation outcomes against sector norms: a probation pass rate of 80-90% suggests good recruitment and induction; below 70% suggests upstream problems; above 95% suggests the probation process may not be rigorous enough. Integrate probation review outcomes with the career progression framework in B071, so that passing probation is the first step on a visible development pathway.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Review all employment contracts for clear probation terms and update if necessary
Create a Probation Review Form covering strengths, areas for improvement, and agreed actions
Schedule review meetings for any staff currently in their probation period
Set calendar reminders for 30/60/90-day reviews at the point of every new hire
Conduct first probation reviews as supportive two-way conversations with documentation
File all probation review records in individual staff files
Brief existing managers on the expectation to conduct and document all probation reviews

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Probation Assessment Framework with specific criteria for 30/60/90-day stages
Create a probation tracker showing all current probationers with dates and status
Draft Probation Extension Letter and Probation Termination Letter templates
Develop a Probation Termination Process aligned with ACAS guidance
Train all managers on fair, consistent, and documented probation review delivery
Link probation assessment criteria to induction milestones from B067
Establish a sign-off process requiring both manager and employee signatures

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Analyse probation data for patterns in pass rates, failure reasons, and role-specific trends
Introduce a probation success celebration as part of team recognition
Survey recently confirmed staff on the quality and fairness of the probation process
Benchmark probation outcomes against hospitality sector norms
Integrate probation completion into the career progression pathway in B071

deliverables: Probation Review Form (used at 30, 60, and 90 days)
Probation Assessment Framework with stage-specific criteria
Probation Tracker (all current probationers, dates, status)

tools.description: Structured review form used at 30, 60, and 90 days covering strengths, areas for improvement, agreed actions, and progressive assessment criteria. Signed by both manager and employee.
Detailed matrix defining specific, measurable assessment criteria for each probation stage — attendance, skill development, team integration, SOP compliance, and customer interaction standards.
Live tracker showing all staff currently in probation, their start date, next review date, review status, and any flags or concerns. Prevents anyone from being overlooked.

Letter templates for extending probation (with specific improvement targets and timeline) and for ending employment during probation (with reasons, notice period, and ACAS-compliant process documentation).
Quarterly analysis of probation outcomes including pass rates, extension rates, termination rates, common development areas, and correlation with recruitment source and induction quality.

failure_modes: Probation reviews are never scheduled or are consistently postponed, resulting in confirmation by default without genuine assessment
Reviews are conducted informally with no documentation, leaving the business exposed if a dismissal is challenged
Assessment criteria are vague or subjective, making it difficult to justify extension or termination decisions
Manager avoids difficult conversations about underperformance, allowing poor fits to become confirmed employees
kpis: 100% of probation reviews conducted within five days of the scheduled 30/60/90-day date
All probation reviews documented with signed forms filed in staff records
Probation pass rate between 80% and 90% (indicating balanced rigour and recruitment quality)
Zero probation-related tribunal claims or ACAS early conciliation cases
New starters reporting clear expectations and fair feedback in post-probation surveys
constraints: Probation terms must be clearly stated in the employment contract to be enforceable
Probation dismissals should follow ACAS guidance including a fair process and written confirmation
Discrimination protections under the Equality Act 2010 apply from day one regardless of probation status
Probation reviews must be consistent across all new starters to avoid claims of unfair treatment

time_load.L1: 2 hours (form creation, contract review, scheduling)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (assessment framework, tracker, templates, manager training)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/quarter (data analysis, process refinement, benchmarking)

blocks.review_protocol: Probation reviews conducted at 30, 60, and 90 days for every new starter without exception. Monthly check of probation tracker for upcoming reviews. Quarterly review of probation outcomes and trend analysis. Annual review of probation process effectiveness and ACAS compliance.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a structured 30/60/90-day probation review process with clear assessment criteria, proper documentation, and ACAS-aligned procedures for confirmation, extension, or termination of new starters.
implementation_risks: Managers may find difficult conversations uncomfortable and avoid raising performance concerns
In very small teams, formal probation reviews can feel overly bureaucratic — frame them as supportive check-ins
Probation extension or termination without proper documentation creates legal risk
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate immediately for every new hire. Cross-reference with B067 for induction completion status, B068 for barista skills assessment, and B071 for career progression gateway.

blocks.name: Onboarding Excellence
blocks.purpose: Provides a holistic assessment and continuous improvement framework for the entire onboarding journey — from pre-start engagement through induction, training, and probation — ensuring all elements work together to deliver a best-in-class new starter experience that accelerates competence and maximises retention.

detailed_objectives: Integrate B067 (Induction), B068 (Barista Training), and B069 (Probation) into a seamless, cohesive onboarding journey
Establish metrics that measure onboarding effectiveness across speed to competence, new starter satisfaction, and early retention
Create a continuous improvement loop where feedback from each cohort of new starters improves the process for the next
Ensure the onboarding experience reflects and reinforces the venue's culture and values from the very first touchpoint
Benchmark the onboarding process against best practice in specialty coffee and wider hospitality
secondary_objectives: Reduce new starter anxiety and build confidence through a predictable, well-communicated onboarding timeline
Create an onboarding experience that new starters talk about positively, aiding future recruitment through word of mouth
Identify systemic gaps in onboarding that contribute to early turnover or slow skill development

blocks.why_this_matters: Most cafes treat induction, training, and probation as separate activities managed by different people at different times. The result is a fragmented experience for new starters: a rushed first day, inconsistent training depending on who is on shift, probation reviews that reference expectations never clearly communicated, and a general sense of being thrown in at the deep end. This fragmentation is the primary reason that early turnover in hospitality remains stubbornly high. Onboarding excellence means designing the entire journey — from the moment a job offer is accepted to the moment probation is confirmed — as a single, integrated experience with clear milestones, consistent support, and measurable outcomes. Research from the Brandon Hall Group shows that organisations with a strong onboarding process improve new hire retention by 82% and productivity by over 70%. In a cafe context, this translates directly to financial performance: a barista who reaches full competence in four weeks rather than eight generates an additional four weeks of productive output per hire. Multiply that across three to five new starters per year and the impact on revenue and service quality is substantial. Onboarding excellence also serves as a diagnostic tool. If new starters consistently struggle with the same skills, the training programme needs updating. If they report feeling unwelcome, the team culture needs attention. If they leave before probation ends, either recruitment or induction — or both — are failing. By treating onboarding as a system and measuring its outputs, the cafe owner gains insight into the health of their entire people operation.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Map the current onboarding journey from job offer acceptance to probation confirmation. Document every touchpoint: pre-start communication (or lack thereof), first-day experience, who trains the new starter on what, when feedback is given, and how probation is managed. Identify gaps and inconsistencies — where does the process break down? Which steps are skipped when the cafe is busy? Create a simple Onboarding Journey Map on a single A3 sheet showing the timeline from Day minus 7 (pre-start) to Day 90 (probation confirmation) with key milestones, responsible persons, and deliverables at each stage. Week 2: Cross-reference the onboarding map against B067 (Induction Checklist), B068 (Barista Training Programme), and B069 (Probation Review Schedule) to ensure alignment. Verify that what is promised in each block actually happens in practice. Interview the last two or three new starters about their experience — what worked, what was confusing, what was missing. Document the findings and create an immediate action list for the top three improvements.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Build an Onboarding Tracker that follows each new starter through the entire journey, linking induction completion, training tier assessments, buddy feedback, and probation review outcomes in a single view. This prevents any element falling through the cracks and provides a real-time picture of each new starter's progress. Develop an Onboarding Quality Scorecard with three key metrics: time to independent competence (measured in shifts), new starter satisfaction score (from a Day 30 survey), and 90-day retention rate. Set targets for each metric and review monthly. Create an Onboarding Playbook — a single document or folder that contains everything a manager needs to run onboarding for a new hire: pre-start checklist, Day 1 agenda, induction pack, training schedule, buddy assignment sheet, probation review forms, and all associated templates. This ensures consistency regardless of which manager is handling the hire. Establish a post-onboarding debrief with each new starter at Day 90, specifically asking about the onboarding experience rather than job performance. Feed findings into a rolling improvement log.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Conduct a six-monthly Onboarding Audit reviewing all data from the tracker, scorecard, and debrief surveys. Identify trends: is time to competence improving? Are satisfaction scores rising? Is 90-day retention increasing? Present findings to the team and celebrate improvements. Benchmark against other specialty coffee venues and hospitality best practice — attend industry events, read case studies, and connect with SCA-affiliated trainers for ideas. Consider creating a new starter welcome video featuring current team members talking about what they love about working at the venue — this can be sent pre-start and sets a positive tone. Introduce a 'buddy of the year' award recognising the team member who has been the most effective mentor for new starters. Track the long-term impact of onboarding quality on overall retention — do cohorts with higher onboarding satisfaction scores have longer average tenure? Use this data to justify continued investment in the programme.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Map the current onboarding journey from pre-start to probation confirmation
Identify gaps and inconsistencies in the existing process
Cross-reference the journey map against B067, B068, and B069 deliverables
Interview recent new starters about their onboarding experience
Create an A3 Onboarding Journey Map with milestones and responsibilities
Document the top three immediate improvements and action them
Assign ownership for each stage of the onboarding journey

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build an Onboarding Tracker linking induction, training, and probation data
Develop an Onboarding Quality Scorecard with three key metrics and targets
Create a comprehensive Onboarding Playbook with all templates and checklists
Establish a Day 90 post-onboarding debrief process
Build a rolling improvement log capturing feedback from each cohort
Train all managers on using the Onboarding Playbook consistently
Link onboarding completion to the career progression pathway in B071

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct six-monthly Onboarding Audits reviewing all metrics and trends
Benchmark onboarding against industry best practice and SCA standards
Create a new starter welcome video featuring current team members
Introduce a buddy of the year recognition award
Analyse long-term correlation between onboarding quality and staff retention
Present onboarding improvement data to the team regularly

deliverables: Onboarding Journey Map (A3 visual timeline from Day -7 to Day 90)
Onboarding Tracker linking induction, training, and probation milestones
Onboarding Quality Scorecard with three key metrics
Onboarding Playbook containing all templates and checklists

tools.description: Visual A3 timeline showing every touchpoint from Day -7 (pre-start) to Day 90 (probation confirmation) with milestones, responsible persons, deliverables, and cross-references to B067, B068, and B069.
Individual tracker for each new starter linking induction checklist completion, barista training tier progress, buddy feedback, and probation review outcomes in a single integrated view.
Monthly scorecard tracking time to independent competence, new starter satisfaction score, and 90-day retention rate with targets and trend analysis.
Comprehensive manager guide containing all onboarding templates, checklists, and procedures in a single reference document, ensuring consistent delivery regardless of which manager handles the hire.
Day 90 survey focused specifically on the onboarding experience — welcome, information quality, training effectiveness, buddy support, and suggestions for improvement.

failure_modes: Onboarding is treated as a collection of separate tasks rather than an integrated journey, resulting in gaps and inconsistencies
The Onboarding Playbook is created but not used, with each manager reverting to their own informal approach
Metrics are collected but never reviewed, so the continuous improvement loop never activates
Feedback from new starters is gathered but not acted upon, eroding trust in the process
kpis: 90-day new starter retention rate above 85%
Time to independent competence reducing quarter on quarter
New starter satisfaction score averaging 4 out of 5 or above at Day 30
100% of onboarding milestones completed on schedule for every new hire
Onboarding Quality Scorecard reviewed monthly with actions documented
constraints: Onboarding excellence depends on B067, B068, and B069 being implemented — this block integrates rather than replaces them
Consistency requires all managers to follow the Onboarding Playbook without shortcuts
Metric collection requires basic tracking systems even if just spreadsheet-based

time_load.L1: 4 hours (journey mapping, gap analysis, new-starter interviews)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (tracker build, scorecard design, playbook creation, manager training)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/quarter (audit, benchmarking, improvement implementation)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly review of Onboarding Quality Scorecard metrics. Post-onboarding debrief with every new starter at Day 90. Quarterly review of improvement log and implementation of top priorities. Six-monthly full Onboarding Audit with trend analysis. Annual benchmarking against industry best practice.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block integrates induction, training, and probation into a single cohesive onboarding journey, measuring its effectiveness and continuously improving it to deliver a best-in-class new starter experience.
implementation_risks: Integration across three blocks requires coordination that may be difficult in owner-operator venues
Measuring onboarding quality requires consistent data collection which can feel like overhead
The playbook must be kept current — outdated content is worse than no playbook
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a synthesis block. Activate when B067, B068, and B069 are all at least L1-implemented. Signals that suggest onboarding weakness include high early turnover, slow skill development, and new-starter complaints.

blocks.name: Career Progression Framework
blocks.purpose: Defines clear, visible career pathways from entry-level barista through to senior roles, so every team member understands what is required to progress, what each level brings in terms of pay, responsibility, and development, and that their growth is genuinely possible within the venue.

detailed_objectives: Design a multi-level career ladder appropriate to the venue's size, from barista to senior barista, shift lead, supervisor, and manager
Define the skills, experience, qualifications, and behaviours required to move between each level
Link career levels to pay bands established in B065, creating a transparent relationship between development and reward
Create visible progression opportunities that staff can self-assess against and work towards
Establish a promotion process that is fair, consistent, and based on demonstrated competence rather than favouritism or tenure alone
secondary_objectives: Reduce turnover by showing ambitious team members a future at the venue rather than elsewhere
Build internal succession planning so the venue is not dependent on external recruitment for senior roles
Attract higher-calibre candidates by advertising career development as part of the employment offer

blocks.why_this_matters: One of the most common reasons talented baristas leave independent cafes is the perception that there is nowhere to go. Without a visible career pathway, a skilled barista earning £11.50 per hour looks across the street and sees the same £11.50 but with a different view. There is no compelling reason to stay because staying and leaving look identical from a career perspective. A career progression framework changes this equation fundamentally. When a barista can see that reaching Senior Barista brings a pay increase to £12.50, access to SCA certification, and responsibility for training new starters — and that Shift Lead brings £13.50, rota management responsibility, and a pathway to Supervisor — the calculation shifts. They are no longer comparing hourly rates; they are comparing futures. For micro and small cafes, this does not require creating fictitious corporate ladders. Even a three-level structure — Barista, Senior Barista, Shift Lead — provides meaningful progression. What matters is that the criteria are transparent, the process is fair, and the rewards are real. The framework should be linked directly to the skills matrix (B073), performance reviews (B072), and pay bands (B065) so that progression is integrated into the broader people management system rather than existing in isolation. Career progression also solves the succession problem. When the only person who can open the cafe, manage suppliers, and handle complaints is the owner, the business has no resilience. A career framework that develops shift leads and supervisors creates the bench strength needed for the owner to take a day off, go on holiday, or eventually step back from daily operations.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Define the career levels that make sense for your venue. For most independent cafes, three to four levels work well: Barista (entry), Senior Barista, Shift Lead, and potentially Supervisor or Assistant Manager. For sole-trader kiosks or vans, even two levels (Barista, Senior Barista) provide meaningful progression. For each level, write a one-paragraph role description covering the key responsibilities, expectations, and what makes this level different from the one below. Week 2: Map current team members to the framework. Where does each person sit? Are there people already performing at a higher level but without the title or pay to match? Identify any immediate misalignments — a long-serving team member doing shift lead duties on barista pay is a retention risk that should be addressed promptly. Share the draft framework with the team for feedback. This is not a top-down decree but a tool for their development. Ask: does this make sense, is anything missing, what would motivate you to work towards the next level? Refine based on input.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). For each career level, define specific progression criteria across four dimensions: technical skills (coffee craft, food preparation, till operation), operational skills (opening/closing, stock management, problem-solving), people skills (customer interaction, team communication, conflict handling), and qualifications or training (SCA certifications, food safety levels, first aid). These criteria should be objective and assessable — not 'shows initiative' but 'has independently resolved at least three customer complaints without manager involvement.' Link each level to the corresponding pay band from B065 so that progression has immediate financial reward. Create a Career Progression Guide — a document or poster for the staff area showing all levels, criteria for each, and the process for requesting a progression review. Establish the progression review process: the team member self-assesses against the criteria, discusses with their manager in a dedicated meeting (not a routine performance review), provides evidence of meeting the criteria, and receives a decision within two weeks. Where criteria are not yet met, the outcome should be a development plan with specific actions and a timeline for re-assessment.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Track progression data: how many team members have moved up a level in the past year, average time at each level, and whether progression rates differ by demographic (to guard against bias). Introduce a mentoring element — Senior Baristas mentor Baristas, Shift Leads mentor Senior Baristas — creating a development culture that reinforces the framework. Celebrate progressions publicly: announce level-ups in team briefings, update the Wall of Wins, and consider a small celebration. Connect the career framework to external opportunities where appropriate — a barista aspiring to roasting, cafe management, or opening their own venue should feel that the skills they develop here are transferable. Conduct an annual review of the framework itself: are the levels still appropriate as the business evolves, are the criteria achievable but stretching, and does the pay differential between levels remain motivating? Benchmark against competitor cafes — what progression do they offer, and is your framework competitive?

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Define three to four career levels appropriate to the venue's size and structure
Write a one-paragraph role description for each level
Map all current team members to the framework and identify misalignments
Share the draft framework with the team for feedback and refine accordingly
Address any immediate pay or title misalignments for existing staff
Display the career framework visually in the staff area
Communicate the framework in a team meeting as a development tool, not a hierarchy

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Define specific progression criteria for each level across four dimensions
Link career levels to pay bands from B065
Create a Career Progression Guide document for staff reference
Establish a fair, consistent progression review process with self-assessment
Develop individual development plans for team members not yet meeting next-level criteria
Integrate progression criteria with the skills matrix in B073
Train managers on conducting progression review conversations

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track progression data including rates, timing, and demographic analysis
Introduce a mentoring structure linked to career levels
Celebrate progressions publicly in briefings and on the Wall of Wins
Conduct annual framework review to ensure levels and criteria remain appropriate
Benchmark the career framework against competitor and sector offerings
Connect internal progression to broader career development in coffee and hospitality

deliverables: Career Level Definitions document (role descriptions for each tier)
Progression Criteria Matrix (skills, behaviours, qualifications per level)
Career Progression Guide (staff-facing reference document or poster)
Progression Review Process and Self-Assessment Form

tools.description: Defines each career level (Barista, Senior Barista, Shift Lead, Supervisor/Manager) with role descriptions, key responsibilities, and what differentiates each level from the one below.
Detailed matrix specifying objective, assessable criteria for progression across four dimensions: technical skills, operational skills, people skills, and qualifications/training.
Self-assessment form allowing team members to evaluate themselves against the progression criteria for their target level, providing evidence and identifying development gaps.
Structured plan documenting specific actions, training, and experiences needed to meet progression criteria, with timelines, support required, and review dates.
Annual tracker monitoring progression rates, average time at each level, demographic analysis for bias detection, and correlation between progression and retention.

failure_modes: Career framework exists on paper but promotions still happen based on favouritism or availability, destroying credibility
Criteria are set too high, making progression feel unachievable and demotivating rather than aspirational
Pay bands are not linked to levels, so progression brings responsibility without financial reward
Framework is created but never communicated to the team, so it has no motivational impact
kpis: At least one team member progresses to a higher level per year (in venues with 5+ staff)
100% of team members can describe the career framework and their current level
Progression-related turnover (leaving because no progression available) below 10% of voluntary leavers
All progression decisions documented with evidence-based assessment against published criteria
Average tenure of staff above Barista level exceeding 18 months
constraints: Career levels must be realistic for the venue's size — a two-person kiosk cannot have five management tiers
Pay differentials between levels must be financially sustainable within the venue's wage budget
Progression criteria must be objective and consistently applied to avoid discrimination claims

time_load.L1: 4 hours (level definition, team mapping, initial communication)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (criteria development, pay band linking, guide creation, process design)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (data tracking, framework review, benchmarking)

blocks.review_protocol: Progression review meetings conducted on request with a maximum two-week response time. Quarterly review of development plans for active progression candidates. Six-monthly review of framework relevance and criteria appropriateness. Annual full framework review including pay band alignment and team feedback.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates visible career pathways within the cafe, defining levels from barista to senior roles with clear criteria, pay links, and a fair progression process that motivates ambitious team members to stay and grow.
implementation_risks: Sole-trader venues may struggle to create meaningful progression in a very flat structure
Existing team dynamics can be disrupted when titles and levels are formalised for the first time
Progression expectations must be managed — not everyone can or will reach every level
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate retention issues linked to lack of development, ambitious staff leaving for competitors, or absence of succession planning. Strong interdependency with B072 (performance reviews) and B065 (pay bands).

blocks.name: Performance Review System
blocks.purpose: Establishes a structured system of regular performance conversations — distinct from probation reviews — that provide ongoing feedback, recognise achievement, identify development needs, and create the evidence base for career progression and pay decisions.

detailed_objectives: Implement a regular performance review cycle with formal reviews at least every six months
Create a performance review format that is constructive, two-way, and focused on development rather than judgement
Establish clear performance standards linked to role descriptions and career level criteria from B071
Document performance conversations to create a fair, consistent record that supports progression and capability decisions
Train managers to deliver effective performance feedback that motivates rather than demoralises
secondary_objectives: Identify high performers early and fast-track their development to reduce flight risk
Surface underperformance before it becomes entrenched, enabling timely support or intervention
Create a feedback-rich culture where performance conversations are normal, not feared

blocks.why_this_matters: In most independent cafes, performance feedback happens in one of two ways: never, or in a crisis. Staff either receive no formal feedback at all — drifting along with no idea whether their work is valued — or they receive feedback only when something goes wrong, making every conversation feel like a disciplinary. Both approaches are damaging. The absence of feedback leads to disengagement. The CIPD's annual Good Work Index consistently shows that employees who receive regular feedback report significantly higher job satisfaction, motivation, and intention to stay. In a cafe context, a barista who never hears that their latte art is improving, their customer interactions are excellent, or their stock rotation is noticed will eventually conclude that nobody cares about their contribution — and start looking elsewhere. Conversely, when underperformance is not addressed, it spreads. Other team members notice when a colleague consistently arrives late, cuts corners, or delivers poor drinks without consequence. They either lower their own standards to match or resent picking up the slack. Without documented performance conversations, the manager has no foundation for capability processes if the situation deteriorates, leaving the business exposed under ACAS disciplinary and grievance guidelines. A structured performance review system provides the discipline to have regular, balanced conversations about what is going well, what could improve, and what the team member needs to reach their goals. It connects directly to the career progression framework (B071) by providing the evidence base for promotion decisions and to the skills matrix (B073) by identifying specific development areas.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Decide on the review cycle — for most cafes, a formal review every six months supplemented by informal monthly check-ins works well. Create a simple Performance Review Form with four sections: achievements since last review, areas for development, goals for the next period, and any support or training needed. Keep it to one to two pages — this is a conversation framework, not a bureaucratic exercise. Week 2: Schedule the first round of reviews for all team members who are past their probation period. Give each person at least one week's notice and ask them to prepare by thinking about what they are proud of, what they find challenging, and what they want to achieve in the next six months. Conduct reviews in a quiet space away from the shop floor, allocating at least 30 minutes per person. Begin each review by asking the team member for their self-assessment — let them speak first. This makes the conversation collaborative rather than top-down. Document the key points, agreed actions, and goals. Both parties sign the form and a copy goes in the staff file.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Performance Standards document that defines what good performance looks like at each career level for each key responsibility — these standards provide objective reference points for review conversations. Create a self-assessment template that team members complete before their review, encouraging reflection on achievements, challenges, and aspirations. Establish a monthly informal check-in protocol — a five-to-ten-minute conversation between manager and each team member covering workload, wellbeing, and any immediate concerns. These check-ins are not documented formally but ensure that the six-monthly review contains no surprises. Build a performance tracker showing all team members, their last review date, next review date, key development goals, and progress status. Train managers on giving balanced feedback using the SBI (Situation-Behaviour-Impact) model: describe the situation, the specific behaviour observed, and the impact it had. This prevents feedback from feeling personal or vague. Create a process for addressing underperformance identified through reviews — aligned with ACAS guidance on capability procedures — including additional support, closer supervision, and a clear improvement plan with timeline.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Introduce 360-degree feedback for senior roles — input from peers, direct reports, and customers (via feedback analysis) provides a richer picture of performance than manager assessment alone. Calibrate performance assessments across the team to ensure consistency — are different managers applying the same standards? Conduct a calibration meeting where all reviewers discuss their assessments before finalising. Link performance review outcomes directly to pay review decisions and career progression discussions, creating a transparent connection between contribution and reward. Analyse performance data for patterns: are certain skills consistently identified as development areas across the team, suggesting a training gap? Are high performers clustered under certain managers, suggesting leadership quality variation? Conduct an annual review of the performance review process itself — survey participants on whether reviews feel fair, useful, and motivating. Adjust the format based on feedback.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Decide on the performance review cycle (recommended: six-monthly formal, monthly informal)
Create a Performance Review Form with achievements, development areas, goals, and support needed
Schedule first-round reviews for all staff past probation with at least one week's notice
Conduct reviews in a quiet space allowing at least 30 minutes per person
Begin each review with the team member's self-assessment
Document key points, agreed actions, and goals with dual sign-off
File all review records in individual staff files

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Performance Standards document defining good performance at each career level
Create a self-assessment template for team members to complete before reviews
Establish a monthly informal check-in protocol
Build a performance tracker showing review dates, goals, and progress for all staff
Train managers on SBI feedback model and constructive review delivery
Create an underperformance management process aligned with ACAS capability guidance
Link performance review records to career progression evidence in B071

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce 360-degree feedback for senior roles and shift leads
Conduct calibration meetings to ensure consistency across reviewers
Link performance outcomes to pay review and progression decisions
Analyse performance data for team-wide training gaps and leadership quality patterns
Survey participants annually on the fairness and usefulness of the review process
Refine review format and frequency based on team feedback

deliverables: Performance Review Form (one to two pages, four sections)
Performance Standards Document by career level
Performance Tracker (dates, goals, progress for all staff)

tools.description: Structured review form covering achievements, development areas, goals for the next period, and support or training needs. Includes dual sign-off and is used for six-monthly formal reviews.
Pre-review questionnaire for team members to reflect on their achievements, challenges, skill development, and aspirations before the formal performance conversation.
Document defining observable performance standards for each key responsibility at each career level, providing objective benchmarks for review conversations and progression assessments.
Five-question guide for informal monthly check-ins covering workload, wellbeing, concerns, support needs, and any quick wins or blockers. Takes five to ten minutes per person.

Structured improvement plan for use when performance reviews identify capability concerns, defining specific targets, support measures, review dates, and consequences — aligned with ACAS capability guidance.

failure_modes: Reviews are scheduled but consistently cancelled or postponed, signalling that people development is not a priority
Feedback is exclusively negative, turning reviews into dreaded events rather than developmental conversations
Reviews are conducted but actions are never followed up, making the process feel pointless
Different managers apply wildly different standards, creating perceived unfairness across the team
kpis: 100% of post-probation staff receive a formal performance review every six months
Monthly informal check-ins completed for at least 80% of team members
All reviews documented with signed forms filed within one week of the conversation
At least 70% of development goals set in reviews achieved by the next review period
Staff survey scores on feedback quality and fairness at 4 out of 5 or above
constraints: Performance reviews must be fair, consistent, and free from discriminatory bias under the Equality Act 2010
Underperformance processes must follow ACAS guidance on capability procedures
Review documentation must be stored securely in compliance with GDPR data protection requirements
Reviews require protected time away from the shop floor, impacting labour scheduling

time_load.L1: 3 hours (form creation, scheduling, first round of reviews for a small team)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (standards document, tracker, manager training, process design)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (check-ins, data analysis, calibration)

blocks.review_protocol: Formal performance reviews conducted every six months for all post-probation staff. Monthly informal check-ins between manager and each team member. Quarterly review of performance tracker for overdue reviews or stalled development plans. Annual calibration session to ensure consistency across reviewers. Annual review of the performance review process itself.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a structured performance review system with six-monthly formal reviews and monthly informal check-ins, providing balanced feedback, documenting development, and connecting performance to career progression and pay decisions.
implementation_risks: Owner-managers in very small cafes may feel performance reviews are too formal for their setting
Poorly trained reviewers can do more harm than good — invest in feedback skills first
Linking reviews to pay creates pressure to inflate assessments, undermining the system's integrity
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate absent feedback, staff complaints about unclear expectations, underperformance going unaddressed, or readiness for career progression framework activation. Bidirectional dependency with B071.

blocks.name: Skills Matrix & Development
blocks.purpose: Creates a visual skills matrix mapping every team member's current competency across all operational areas, identifying strengths, gaps, and development priorities to ensure balanced capability across the team and targeted investment in training.

detailed_objectives: Map all operational skills required across the venue — coffee, food, till, cleaning, opening, closing, stock management
Assess each team member's competency level in each skill area using a consistent rating scale
Identify critical skill gaps where only one or two people hold a key competency
Create individual development plans driven by skills matrix insights
Track skill development over time to demonstrate team capability growth
secondary_objectives: Inform cross-training priorities by highlighting single-person dependencies
Support rota planning by showing which staff can cover which stations
Provide objective evidence for career progression decisions linked to B071

blocks.why_this_matters: Most cafe owners carry a mental map of who can do what — they know that Sarah makes the best flat whites, Tom is the fastest on till, and only Maria knows how to do the end-of-day cash-up. The problem with mental maps is that they are invisible, untested, and fragile. When the owner is not on shift, the shift lead may not know that the new starter has never been shown how to operate the dishwasher or that nobody scheduled today can handle a dairy-free milk complaint. A skills matrix makes the invisible visible. By mapping every team member against every operational skill on a simple grid — using ratings such as 'not trained,' 'learning,' 'competent,' and 'can train others' — the cafe immediately sees where its strengths and vulnerabilities lie. If only one person is rated 'competent' at grinder calibration, that is a key person risk (B077). If three out of eight staff have never been trained on allergen checking, that is a food safety risk (B001-B006). If nobody on the weekend rota is competent at food preparation, that is a service quality risk. The skills matrix is not a judgement tool — it is a planning tool. It drives training investment towards the areas of greatest need and greatest impact. Combined with the career progression framework (B071) and performance reviews (B072), it gives each team member a clear picture of where they are strong, where they need development, and what steps will help them progress. The matrix also makes scheduling smarter: the rota builder can ensure that every shift has the right mix of skills to cover all stations safely and effectively.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: List all operational skills required in the venue. For most cafes this includes: espresso preparation, milk steaming, filter brewing, latte art, food preparation, food plating, allergen management, till operation, card payment handling, opening procedure, closing procedure, stock rotation, goods receiving, cleaning stations, dishwasher operation, grinder calibration, equipment troubleshooting, customer complaint handling, and first aid. Adjust this list to match your specific operation. Create a grid with team member names across the top and skills down the side. Week 2: Rate each team member against each skill using a four-point scale: 0 = Not Trained, 1 = Learning (can do with supervision), 2 = Competent (can do independently), 3 = Expert (can train others). Be honest — this is a diagnostic tool, not a performance review. Involve shift leads in the assessment for accuracy. Highlight any skills where only one person is rated at competent or above — these are immediate cross-training priorities. Share the completed matrix with the team, framing it as a development tool that shows where training investment will go.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Create a digital version of the skills matrix (spreadsheet or simple app) that can be updated as training progresses. Use colour coding: red for skills with single-person dependency, amber for two-person coverage, green for three or more competent team members. Build Individual Development Plans (IDPs) for each team member, driven by the matrix — each person should have two to three specific skills they are working to develop over the next quarter. These IDPs should align with the career progression criteria in B071 and be reviewed in performance conversations per B072. Establish a training calendar that allocates protected time for skill development based on matrix priorities. Link the skills matrix to rota planning — when building the weekly rota, cross-reference against the matrix to ensure each shift has the minimum required skill coverage for all operational areas. Create a quarterly skills review process where the matrix is updated based on recent training, assessments, and operational observation.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Track skills development velocity — how many skill-level increases are achieved across the team per quarter? Set a target and celebrate when it is exceeded. Use the matrix to identify potential trainers and mentors: team members rated at level 3 in a skill area should be supported to teach that skill to others, building their leadership capabilities. Analyse the relationship between skills depth and operational performance — do shifts with higher average skill ratings produce fewer errors, less waste, and better customer satisfaction scores? Integrate the skills matrix with SCA certification planning from B068 — which SCA modules align with which matrix skills, and who is the next candidate for external certification? Conduct an annual matrix review that also assesses whether the skill categories themselves need updating as the venue's offering evolves — new menu items, new equipment, new service formats.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: List all operational skills required across the venue
Create a skills matrix grid with team members and skills on a four-point scale
Rate each team member honestly with input from shift leads
Identify single-person skill dependencies and flag as immediate priorities
Share the completed matrix with the team as a development tool
Identify the top three cross-training priorities from the matrix
Begin addressing the highest-risk skill gap within the first fortnight

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a digital skills matrix with colour-coded dependency warnings
Build Individual Development Plans driven by matrix gaps for each team member
Establish a training calendar allocating protected time for skill development
Link the skills matrix to rota planning for minimum skill coverage per shift
Create a quarterly skills review and matrix update process
Integrate skills data with career progression criteria from B071
Align development plans with performance review goals from B072

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track skills development velocity as a team metric
Identify and support level-3 team members to become trainers and mentors
Analyse correlation between shift skill ratings and operational performance
Integrate the matrix with SCA certification planning from B068
Conduct annual matrix review updating skill categories for business evolution
Celebrate skill development achievements in team briefings and Wall of Wins

deliverables: Skills Matrix Grid (team members vs operational skills, four-point scale)
Individual Development Plan template driven by matrix gaps
Skills Matrix Update Process (quarterly review guide)
Training Calendar template aligned to matrix priorities
Skills Coverage Report for rota planning

tools.description: Grid mapping all team members against all operational skills using a four-point rating scale (Not Trained, Learning, Competent, Expert). Colour-coded to highlight single-person dependencies.
Per-person development plan identifying two to three priority skills from the matrix, specific learning actions, timelines, and support needed. Reviewed quarterly and linked to performance reviews.
Monthly calendar allocating protected training time for skill development based on matrix priorities, with assigned trainers, topics, and expected outcomes.
