time_load.L1: 2 hours (walkthrough, checklist creation, staff briefing)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (SOP drafting, role assignments, quality score setup)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (audits, dashboard review, checklist updates)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily review of completed checklist by opening manager. Weekly review of opening quality scores by manager. Monthly closing compliance review in team meeting. Quarterly checklist content review and update.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a standardised closing checklist so every end-of-day task — cleaning, cash, food safety, security — is completed consistently regardless of who is closing. It links directly to morning readiness and prevents the costly drift that occurs when closing is left to memory.
implementation_risks: Staff may resist the perceived 'micromanagement' of a formal checklist — frame it as a support tool, not surveillance
Paper checklists can be lost or damaged — laminate and consider early digital migration
Seasonal variations (outdoor seating, extended summer hours) require checklist updates that may be overlooked
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B092 (Opening Checklist) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate morning operational issues traceable to poor closing, equipment damage from improper shutdown, or security concerns.

blocks.name: Shift Handover System
blocks.purpose: Creates a structured shift handover process that ensures critical information — outstanding tasks, equipment issues, customer complaints, stock shortages, and operational notes — transfers reliably between outgoing and incoming teams. This block prevents the information black holes that form at every shift change.

detailed_objectives: Establish a written handover log that captures all essential information at every shift change
Define a face-to-face handover briefing protocol between outgoing and incoming shift leads
Ensure equipment issues, customer complaints, and stock shortages are communicated without fail
Create accountability so problems are owned across shifts rather than falling between the cracks
Standardise handover timing to prevent service gaps during transition periods
secondary_objectives: Build team cohesion by connecting shifts that might otherwise operate in isolation
Reduce repeated customer complaints caused by information not being passed between teams
Provide managers with a daily operational narrative they can review remotely

blocks.why_this_matters: In a typical UK cafe, there are at least two distinct shift teams each day — an opening crew and an afternoon/closing crew — and often a third covering the lunch rush. Each shift change represents a potential rupture in operational continuity. Without a formal handover system, critical information evaporates: the morning team discovers a blocked drain but leaves before the plumber arrives, and the afternoon team has no idea the plumber is coming or what the issue was. A customer complains about their sandwich at 11am, the opening team promises a replacement on their next visit, but the afternoon team knows nothing about it. The espresso machine starts pulling shots slowly at 2pm, but nobody recorded that the morning team already descaled it, so the afternoon team wastes thirty minutes repeating the process. These failures are individually small but cumulatively devastating. They erode customer trust, waste labour hours, create friction between shift teams ('they never tell us anything'), and generate the kind of low-level operational chaos that owners struggle to diagnose because the root cause is invisible — it lives in the gap between shifts. A structured handover system takes five minutes per shift change but saves hours of confusion, prevents repeated work, and creates an auditable daily log that managers can review to spot emerging patterns. For cafes with absent owners or multi-site operators, the shift handover log becomes the single most valuable document for understanding what is actually happening on the floor.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Create a simple A4 Shift Handover Log with sections for: Date and Shift (AM/PM), Outgoing Shift Lead name, Key Issues (anything the next team needs to know), Equipment Status (any faults or quirks), Stock Alerts (items running low or out), Customer Notes (complaints, promises, VIP visits), Outstanding Tasks (anything started but not finished), and Incoming Shift Lead signature confirming they have read and understood. Place the log in a dedicated folder or clipboard at the till point. From Day 1, the outgoing shift lead completes the handover log in the final 10 minutes of their shift. When possible, the outgoing and incoming leads should overlap by 10-15 minutes to walk through the log together. If overlap is not possible (common in small cafes with tight labour budgets), the incoming lead reads the log immediately upon arrival and signs it. The manager reviews all handover logs daily, either in person or via a photo sent to a WhatsApp group. Week 2: Refine the log format based on what information is actually useful versus what is being left blank. Add a 'Priorities for Next Shift' section so the outgoing team can flag the single most important thing the incoming team should focus on.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Shift Handover SOP that formalises the process: who writes it, when it must be completed, what must be included, and how the incoming team acknowledges receipt. Create a digital version using a shared Google Doc or simple app (e.g., a WhatsApp Business channel dedicated to handovers) so all staff can access handover history from their phones. Introduce a handover quality score — the incoming shift lead rates the handover they received on a 1-5 scale, and these scores are reviewed weekly. Begin tracking recurring themes in handover logs (e.g., if the same equipment issue appears three days running, it triggers a maintenance request). Link handover logs to the closing checklist (B093) so that closing tasks feed directly into the next morning's handover. Establish that any customer complaint or promise must be recorded in the handover log as a mandatory field — this prevents the most damaging type of information loss.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Shift handovers should feel like a professional briefing, not paperwork. Train shift leads to deliver a 2-minute verbal summary covering the three most important points, then hand over the written log for detail. Introduce cross-shift recognition — if the afternoon team finds the cafe in excellent condition, they can leave a positive note for the morning team, building mutual respect. Conduct monthly handover audits where the manager reviews a week's worth of logs and provides feedback. Integrate handover data into weekly management meetings to identify operational trends. Consider a digital handover board visible to all staff that shows the last three handovers at a glance. Review and update the handover template quarterly to keep it relevant and concise.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Design and print a simple A4 Shift Handover Log template
Place the handover log at a consistent, accessible location near the till
Brief all shift leads on the handover process and sign-off requirement
Schedule 10-15 minute shift overlaps where labour budget allows
Manager reviews all handover logs daily and flags any gaps
Add a 'Priorities for Next Shift' section after first week of use
Establish a photo-to-WhatsApp backup for days when manager is offsite

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Draft a formal Shift Handover SOP with mandatory fields and timing
Create a digital handover channel (shared doc or messaging group)
Implement incoming shift lead quality scoring (1-5) for handovers received
Track recurring handover themes weekly to identify systemic issues
Link handover logs to closing checklist (B093) for continuity
Make customer complaint recording a mandatory handover field
File all handover logs for a minimum of 4 weeks for review purposes

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Train shift leads to deliver 2-minute verbal handover summaries
Introduce cross-shift recognition notes for positive team feedback
Conduct monthly handover audits reviewing a full week of logs
Integrate handover data into weekly management meeting agendas
Review and update the handover template quarterly

tools.description: A4 template with sections for key issues, equipment status, stock alerts, customer notes, outstanding tasks, and priorities for the next shift. Signed by both outgoing and incoming shift leads.
Standard Operating Procedure defining handover timing, mandatory content fields, verbal briefing protocol, quality scoring, and escalation procedures for critical issues.
Weekly tracker recording incoming shift lead quality scores for handovers received, identifying patterns in poor handovers and linking them to specific teams or individuals.
Monthly report analysing recurring themes from handover logs — equipment issues, stock shortages, customer complaints — to identify systemic problems requiring management intervention.

failure_modes: Outgoing shift leaves without completing the handover log, creating a complete information gap for the incoming team
Handover log is filled with vague or unhelpful entries ('all fine') that provide no actionable information
Incoming shift lead does not read the handover log and repeats work or misses critical issues

kpis: 100% of shift changes have a completed and signed handover log
Average handover quality score of 4.0 or above out of 5.0
Zero customer complaints caused by information lost between shifts per month
All equipment issues recorded in handover logs resolved within 48 hours

time_load.L1: 1 hour (template design, staff briefing)
time_load.L2: 3 hours (SOP drafting, digital channel setup, quality score implementation)
time_load.L3: 30 minutes/month (audits, theme analysis, template updates)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily review of handover logs by manager. Weekly review of handover quality scores. Monthly handover audit reviewing one full week of logs. Quarterly template review and update.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a formal shift handover system so critical information — equipment issues, customer complaints, stock shortages, outstanding tasks — transfers reliably between teams. It prevents the information black holes that form at every shift change and gives managers a daily operational narrative.
implementation_risks: Shift leads may view handover logs as unnecessary paperwork if the value is not clearly communicated
Labour budget constraints may prevent adequate shift overlap for face-to-face briefings
Digital handover channels can become cluttered with non-essential messages if not managed
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B092 and B093 as part of the shift management trio. Flag as urgent if signals indicate recurring information gaps between shifts, repeated customer complaints, or equipment issues that persist across multiple days without resolution.

blocks.name: Par-Level Stock Management
blocks.purpose: Defines minimum stock levels (par levels) for every key ingredient and consumable, ensuring the cafe never runs out of critical items during service while avoiding over-ordering that ties up cash and increases waste. Par levels are the foundation of intelligent stock management.

detailed_objectives: Identify every stock item that requires a defined minimum holding level
Calculate par levels based on actual daily usage, lead times, and delivery schedules
Create a visual par-level reference sheet accessible to all staff at the point of stock storage
Establish a simple reorder trigger system — when stock hits par, the order goes in automatically
Adjust par levels seasonally and in response to menu changes or trading pattern shifts
secondary_objectives: Reduce emergency orders from cash-and-carry runs that cost more and disrupt the shift
Minimise waste from over-ordering perishable items like milk, pastries, and fresh produce
Free up working capital by holding only what is needed rather than over-stocking

blocks.why_this_matters: Running out of oat milk at 10am on a Saturday morning is not a minor inconvenience — it is a revenue disaster. In a typical UK specialty cafe, alternative milks now account for 30-40% of all drinks sold, and a customer who cannot get their oat flat white will leave, not switch to dairy. The same principle applies across the menu: no sourdough for the avocado toast, no bananas for smoothies, no takeaway cups for the morning rush. Each stockout costs not just the immediate sale but the customer's confidence that the cafe is well-run. On the other side of the equation, over-ordering is equally destructive. A cafe that buys too much fresh milk dumps litres down the drain every week. Pastries ordered in excess of demand either get sold at a loss through end-of-day discounting or thrown away entirely. Every item sitting on a shelf represents cash that could be earning returns elsewhere. Par-level stock management solves both problems by establishing a scientifically derived minimum holding for each item. The par level is calculated from average daily usage multiplied by lead time (how long it takes to get a delivery), plus a safety buffer for demand spikes. When stock drops to the par level, an order is triggered. This simple system — used universally in professional hospitality — prevents both stockouts and over-ordering. For a small UK cafe spending £2,000-£4,000 per week on supplies, getting par levels right typically reduces waste by 10-15% and eliminates 90% of emergency purchasing, saving £200-£500 per month.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: List every stock item the cafe uses, grouped by category: Coffee & Tea (beans, tea bags, syrups, chocolate powder), Dairy & Alternatives (whole milk, semi-skimmed, oat milk, soy milk, cream), Food Ingredients (bread, butter, eggs, avocados, salad items, sandwich fillings), Pastries & Baked Goods (croissants, muffins, cakes — whether baked in-house or bought in), Packaging (takeaway cups in all sizes, lids, paper bags, napkins, deli boxes), and Cleaning Supplies (sanitiser, cloths, bin bags). For each item, record the current delivery schedule (e.g., milk delivered Monday/Wednesday/Friday, coffee beans fortnightly). Over the first week, track actual daily usage by counting what you use each day — this does not need to be precise, rough counts are fine to start. Week 2: For each item, calculate a simple par level using the formula: Par = (Average Daily Usage x Days Between Deliveries) + 1 Day Safety Buffer. Write the par level on a label and stick it on the shelf where the item is stored. Train all staff: 'If you notice stock is at or below the label level, tell the shift lead immediately.' Create a simple reorder sheet listing all items, their par levels, current stock, and order quantity needed.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Refine par levels using 4-6 weeks of actual usage data rather than estimates. Differentiate par levels by day of the week — a Saturday par level for milk will be significantly higher than a Tuesday. Build a Weekly Order Template that pre-populates expected order quantities based on par levels, allowing the person ordering to simply adjust for any unusual circumstances (events, holidays, weather forecasts). Create a Par Level Master Sheet in a spreadsheet that records each item's par level, supplier, unit cost, lead time, and last review date. Introduce a monthly par level review where the manager compares actual usage against par levels and adjusts any items that are consistently over- or under-ordered. Link par levels to the mid-service stock check (B096) so that par-level items are specifically monitored during busy periods. Establish a supplier communication protocol so orders triggered by par levels are placed by a consistent deadline (e.g., all orders for next-day delivery placed by 2pm).
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Par levels should now be second nature to the team. Introduce seasonal par-level profiles — summer levels for iced drinks, cold brew, and salad items; winter levels for hot chocolate, soup, and heavier bakes. Implement a digital stock management system (even a shared Google Sheet works) where par levels are tracked in real time and orders can be generated semi-automatically. Conduct quarterly par-level audits comparing waste data, stockout frequency, and ordering costs against the par-level framework. Adjust par levels immediately when menu items are added, removed, or changed. Share par-level performance data with the team so they understand the financial impact of good stock management. Explore just-in-time delivery arrangements with key suppliers to reduce the safety buffer needed and further tighten working capital.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: List every stock item the cafe uses, grouped by category
Record current delivery schedules for all suppliers
Track actual daily usage for each item over one full week
Calculate par levels using the formula: (Daily Usage x Days Between Deliveries) + 1 Day Buffer
Label each storage location with the par level for the item stored there
Train all staff to report when stock reaches the par-level label
Create a simple reorder sheet listing items, par levels, and order quantities

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Refine par levels using 4-6 weeks of actual usage data
Create day-of-week par level variations for high-volume items
Build a Weekly Order Template pre-populated with par-level quantities
Create a Par Level Master Sheet in a spreadsheet with full item details
Introduce monthly par level review meetings to adjust levels
Link par levels to mid-service stock checks (B096)
Establish supplier order deadlines aligned with delivery schedules

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Create seasonal par-level profiles for summer and winter trading
Implement a digital stock management system for real-time tracking
Conduct quarterly par-level audits against waste and stockout data
Share par-level performance data with the team monthly
Explore just-in-time delivery to reduce safety buffer requirements

deliverables: Stock Item Master List with par levels

tools.description: Spreadsheet template for calculating par levels for every stock item based on average daily usage, delivery frequency, and safety buffer. Includes columns for item name, category, daily usage, lead time, calculated par, and review date.
Pre-populated order form listing all stock items with par levels, current stock, and calculated order quantities. Allows quick adjustment for unusual demand and generates orders by supplier.
Printable labels showing the par level for each stock item, designed to be affixed to shelves, fridges, and storage areas so any team member can instantly see whether stock needs reordering.

Monthly analysis comparing par levels against actual usage, waste, and stockout incidents. Flags items where par levels need adjustment and tracks the financial impact of stock management improvements.

failure_modes: Par levels are set based on guesswork rather than actual usage data, leading to persistent over- or under-ordering
Par levels are never reviewed after initial setup, becoming increasingly inaccurate as demand patterns change
Staff do not check stock against par levels or fail to report when items hit the trigger point
Seasonal demand shifts are not reflected in par levels, causing summer stockouts of cold items or winter waste of salad ingredients
kpis: Zero critical stockouts (milk, coffee beans, takeaway cups) during trading hours per week
Food waste reduction of 10-15% within 8 weeks of par level implementation
Emergency cash-and-carry runs reduced to fewer than 2 per month
Par levels reviewed and adjusted monthly with documented evidence
constraints: Par levels must be recalculated whenever supplier delivery schedules change
Perishable items require tighter par levels than ambient goods due to shelf life limitations
Seasonal par-level adjustments must be made at least 2 weeks before demand shifts

time_load.L1: 4 hours (item listing, usage tracking, initial par calculation, labelling)
time_load.L2: 3 hours (data refinement, order template creation, monthly review setup)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (audits, seasonal adjustments, digital system maintenance)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily stock-to-par check by shift lead. Weekly order review against par levels by manager. Monthly par level accuracy review in management meeting. Quarterly full par level audit with seasonal adjustments.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block defines minimum stock levels for every key item so the cafe never runs out during service and never over-orders. Par levels are calculated from actual usage data and delivery schedules, creating a simple reorder trigger that prevents both stockouts and waste.
implementation_risks: Staff may resist the discipline of checking stock against par levels during busy periods
Initial par level calculations based on limited data may be inaccurate — plan to refine after 4-6 weeks
Supplier delivery reliability directly impacts par level effectiveness — unreliable suppliers need higher buffers
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B096 (Mid-Service Stock Check) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate frequent stockouts, excessive waste, or emergency purchasing patterns.

blocks.name: Mid-Service Stock Check
blocks.purpose: Implements a structured mid-service stock check that catches low-stock situations before they become stockouts, giving the team time to restock from back-of-house, adjust menu availability, or place emergency orders. This block is the early warning system that prevents the worst moment in hospitality: telling a customer you have run out.

detailed_objectives: Establish a scheduled mid-service stock check at a fixed time each day (typically 11am or just before the lunch rush)
Define a quick-check list of the 15-20 items most likely to run out during service
Create a rapid restocking protocol so back-of-house stock is moved to service areas before demand peaks
Integrate the mid-service check with par levels (B095) so staff know exactly what 'low' means for each item
Enable real-time menu adjustments when items are running low to avoid customer-facing stockouts
secondary_objectives: Build staff awareness of consumption patterns so they anticipate shortages rather than react to them
Reduce the stress of discovering empty shelves during the busiest period of the day
Provide daily usage data that feeds back into par-level accuracy (B095)

blocks.why_this_matters: Most cafe stockouts do not happen because the item was never ordered — they happen because the item ran out during service and nobody noticed until a customer asked for it. The gap between back-of-house storage and the service counter is where stock visibility dies. A full box of oat milk sits in the walk-in while the barista tells the fifth customer in a row that they have run out, simply because nobody checked. A tray of croissants waits in the kitchen while the display cabinet sits empty and customers assume there are none left. These are not supply chain failures; they are information failures. The mid-service stock check solves this by creating a deliberate pause — typically at 11am, before the lunch rush — where a designated team member walks the entire service area and checks stock levels against a short, focused list. This takes 5-7 minutes and prevents hours of lost revenue and customer frustration. In a cafe turning over £1,500-£3,000 per day, a single stockout of a popular item during the lunch rush can cost £50-£150 in lost sales. More importantly, it costs customer confidence: regulars who find their usual order unavailable twice in a month start looking elsewhere. The mid-service check is a tiny time investment with outsized returns in revenue protection and customer satisfaction.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Identify the 15-20 items most likely to run out during a typical trading day. These will almost always include: milk (all types), takeaway cups (most popular sizes), coffee beans in the hopper, sandwich fillings or daily specials, pastries in the display, napkins and packaging. Create a simple Quick Check Card — a single laminated card listing these items with a tick box and space to note 'OK', 'Low', or 'Out'. Assign the mid-service check to the shift lead or a designated team member, scheduled for 11:00am daily (or 30 minutes before the busiest period if the cafe's peak is different). The check should take no more than 7 minutes: walk the bar, check the display, check the counter supplies, glance at the till roll, and note anything that needs restocking. If items are low, immediately restock from back-of-house. If items are out and cannot be restocked, update the team verbally and remove the item from any menu boards. Week 2: Review the first week's Quick Check Cards to identify which items consistently show as 'Low' — these may indicate par levels need adjusting (feed back to B095).
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Expand the mid-service check into a two-check system: a primary check at 11am and a secondary check at 2pm (or appropriate times for the cafe's trading pattern). Create a digital version of the Quick Check Card accessible on a tablet or phone so results are instantly visible to the manager. Build a Restock Protocol that defines where each item is stored in back-of-house and how much should be brought forward during a restock — avoid the common mistake of bringing too much to the counter and creating clutter. Introduce a 'menu flex' system: when the check reveals a key item is running low and cannot be restocked, the shift lead has authority to remove it from the menu board and suggest alternatives to the team, preventing the awkward customer-facing 'sorry, we've run out' moment. Track mid-service check data weekly to spot patterns — if oat milk consistently runs low by 11am, either the par level is wrong or the morning demand is higher than estimated.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Mid-service stock awareness should become instinctive for the whole team, not dependent on a formal check. Train all staff to adopt a 'stock radar' mindset: whenever they pass a storage area, they glance at levels. Introduce a traffic-light stock display system for key items (green = adequate, amber = approaching par, red = below par) visible in the prep area. Use mid-service check data to create demand forecasting models — over time, the cafe should be able to predict exactly when items will run low on any given day of the week. Integrate mid-service checks into the digital stock management system so data flows automatically to par levels, ordering, and waste tracking. Celebrate weeks with zero stockouts as team achievements.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Identify the 15-20 items most likely to run out during service
Create a laminated Quick Check Card with tick boxes for each item
Assign the mid-service check to the shift lead at 11:00am daily
Define the check route: Bar, Display, Counter Supplies, Packaging
Establish immediate restocking from back-of-house when items are low
Train team to remove unavailable items from menu boards immediately
Review Quick Check Cards weekly to identify recurring shortages

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Expand to a two-check system at 11am and 2pm
Create a digital Quick Check accessible on tablet or phone
Build a Restock Protocol defining storage locations and restock quantities
Introduce a menu flex system for real-time availability adjustments
Track mid-service check data weekly to identify demand patterns
Feed mid-service data back to par-level calculations (B095)
Train shift leads on proactive menu management during shortages

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Train all staff to adopt a continuous 'stock radar' mindset
Implement a traffic-light stock display system for key items
Use check data to build demand forecasting models by day of week
Integrate mid-service checks into digital stock management system
Celebrate zero-stockout weeks as team achievements

deliverables: Quick Check Card (laminated A5 reference card)
Restock Protocol with storage locations and quantities

tools.description: Laminated A5 card listing the 15-20 items most likely to run out during service, with tick boxes for OK/Low/Out status. Designed for a 5-7 minute walkthrough of all service areas.
Reference document showing where each key item is stored in back-of-house, the standard restock quantity, and the maximum front-of-house holding to prevent counter clutter.
Weekly tracker compiling mid-service check results to identify items that consistently run low, feeding data back to par-level adjustments and ordering decisions.
Quick-reference guide giving the shift lead authority and scripts for removing items from the menu when stock runs low, including suggested alternative recommendations for customers.

failure_modes: The mid-service check is skipped during busy periods — precisely when it is most needed
Check is performed superficially (glancing rather than counting) and misses low-stock items hidden behind fuller stock
Results of the check are not acted upon — items are noted as 'Low' but nobody restocks
The check list becomes outdated as menu items change but the card is not updated
kpis: Mid-service stock check completed on time 100% of trading days
Customer-facing stockouts (items ordered but unavailable) reduced to fewer than 3 per week
Time from low-stock detection to restocking under 10 minutes
Menu flex decisions made proactively (before customer requests) 90% of the time
constraints: Mid-service check timing must be adjusted to suit the cafe's specific peak trading pattern
The Quick Check Card must be updated whenever menu items are added or removed
Back-of-house stock must be organised so restocking can happen within minutes, not requiring a search

time_load.L1: 1 hour (item identification, card creation, staff briefing)
time_load.L2: 2 hours (protocol development, digital setup, flex guidelines)
time_load.L3: 30 minutes/month (data analysis, forecasting updates)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily completion verification by manager. Weekly stockout pattern review. Monthly analysis of mid-service check data feeding back to par levels. Quarterly Quick Check Card content review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block implements a quick mid-service stock check — typically at 11am before the lunch rush — that catches low-stock items before they become stockouts. It takes 5-7 minutes and prevents the revenue loss and customer frustration of running out during the busiest part of the day.
implementation_risks: The check will be the first thing skipped when the cafe gets busy, which is precisely when it matters most — build it into the daily rhythm
Staff may not know where back-of-house stock is stored, making restocking slow — label everything
If the shift lead is also making drinks during peak, they need cover for the 7 minutes the check takes
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B095 (Par-Level Stock Management) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate frequent mid-service stockouts, customer complaints about item availability, or high waste from over-restocking.

blocks.name: Stock Management Excellence
blocks.purpose: Elevates stock management from a reactive chore to a strategic capability by integrating par levels (B095), mid-service checks (B096), waste tracking, demand forecasting, and supplier coordination into a unified system. This is the capstone block that transforms stock management from 'ordering when we run out' to 'never running out and never over-ordering'.

detailed_objectives: Integrate par-level management and mid-service checks into a single cohesive stock management framework
Implement waste tracking that identifies the root causes of stock loss (over-ordering, spoilage, portioning, theft)
Build demand forecasting capability using historical sales data, day-of-week patterns, and seasonal trends
Establish a continuous improvement cycle where stock management performance is measured, reviewed, and optimised monthly
Create a stock management culture where every team member understands the financial impact of waste and stockouts
secondary_objectives: Reduce total stock holding value by 15-25% without increasing stockout risk
Achieve food cost percentage targets consistently (25-35% for food, 20-28% for coffee)
Position the cafe to negotiate better supplier terms through accurate, predictable ordering patterns

blocks.why_this_matters: Stock management is where operational discipline and financial performance intersect most visibly. A UK cafe spending £3,000 per week on supplies with a 5% waste rate is losing £150 weekly — £7,800 annually — to poor stock management alone. That figure does not include the hidden costs of stockouts (lost sales, customer churn) or over-ordering (tied-up capital, storage space, rushed end-of-day discounting). Most independent cafe owners know they waste too much but cannot quantify it or identify the root causes. Is the waste coming from over-ordering milk that expires? From portioning sandwiches too generously? From pastries that do not sell on quiet days? Without a systematic approach, waste becomes an accepted cost of doing business rather than a solvable problem. Stock Management Excellence brings together par levels, mid-service checks, waste logs, and demand data into a single system that answers these questions. It moves the cafe from reactive ('we ran out of oat milk again') to predictive ('we will need 20% more oat milk next week because of the school holidays') to strategic ('we can reduce our milk order by 10 litres per week by switching to a 3-day delivery cycle'). For cafes approaching multi-site expansion or seeking investment, demonstrating disciplined stock management is a credibility signal that sophisticated operators and investors look for immediately.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). This block assumes B095 (Par-Level Stock Management) and B096 (Mid-Service Stock Check) are already operational. Week 1: Introduce a Daily Waste Log — a simple sheet where staff record every item discarded during the day, including the reason (expired, damaged, over-produced, dropped, returned by customer). Place the waste log next to the waste bin so recording happens at the point of disposal. Categorise waste into three types: Avoidable (over-production, poor rotation), Unavoidable (coffee grounds, vegetable trimmings), and Unknown (the concerning category that needs investigation). At the end of each day, the closing team totals the waste log. Week 2: The manager reviews one week of waste logs and identifies the top 3 waste items by value. For each, determine the root cause: if croissants are consistently wasted, is the par level too high or is the cafe ordering the same quantity regardless of day of the week? If milk is being dumped, is it a rotation issue or an over-ordering issue? Create a simple action plan for each of the top 3 waste items.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Build a Stock Management Dashboard — a weekly spreadsheet or digital tool that brings together data from par levels, mid-service checks, waste logs, and ordering records. Track three core metrics weekly: Waste Percentage (waste cost divided by total stock cost), Stockout Incidents (number of times items were unavailable during service), and Order Accuracy (how closely actual orders match what was actually needed). Begin building demand forecasting by day of the week: record daily sales of key items (coffees by type, food items, pastries) and after 4 weeks, calculate the average and standard deviation for each day. Use this data to adjust par levels and ordering with day-specific quantities. Implement a weekly stock management review meeting (15 minutes) where the manager reviews dashboard data, discusses waste trends, and agrees actions. Introduce team waste awareness training — show staff the financial impact of waste using real numbers from the cafe ('last month we threw away £600 of food — that's more than one of your monthly wages').
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Stock management should now be data-driven and proactive. Implement a monthly Stock Management Scorecard covering waste percentage, stockout rate, order accuracy, and cost trends. Set targets: food waste below 3% of food cost, zero critical stockouts, order accuracy above 90%. Use 12 weeks of demand data to build simple forecasting models that predict next week's needs based on day-of-week patterns, weather, local events, and school term dates. Explore supplier consolidation — can fewer, better suppliers improve quality, reduce delivery frequency, and lower costs? Introduce quarterly stock management reviews with the owner, linking stock performance directly to GP% and profitability. Consider investing in a basic inventory management system (e.g., MarketMan, Lightspeed) if the cafe's scale justifies the cost. Share stock management excellence as a team achievement — display the waste reduction journey visually so staff see their collective impact.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Introduce a Daily Waste Log at the point of waste disposal
Categorise waste as Avoidable, Unavoidable, or Unknown
Train all staff to record waste at the moment of disposal
Manager reviews waste logs weekly and identifies top 3 waste items by value
Determine root cause for each major waste item and create action plans
Ensure B095 (Par Levels) and B096 (Mid-Service Checks) are operational
Set up a simple stock performance tracking sheet

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build a Stock Management Dashboard integrating waste, stockout, and ordering data
Track Waste %, Stockout Incidents, and Order Accuracy weekly
Record daily sales by item for 4+ weeks to build day-of-week demand profiles
Adjust par levels and ordering with day-specific quantities
Implement a weekly 15-minute stock management review meeting
Deliver team waste awareness training using real cafe financial data
Create a waste reduction action plan targeting the top 5 waste categories

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Implement a monthly Stock Management Scorecard with targets
Build simple demand forecasting models using 12+ weeks of data
Explore supplier consolidation for improved terms and delivery efficiency
Conduct quarterly stock management reviews linking to GP% and profitability
Display waste reduction journey visually for team motivation

deliverables: Demand Forecasting Worksheet by day of week

tools.description: Simple recording sheet placed next to the waste bin for staff to log every discarded item with quantity, estimated value, and reason (expired, over-produced, damaged, returned). Totalled daily by the closing team.
Weekly spreadsheet integrating data from par levels, mid-service checks, waste logs, and ordering records. Tracks Waste %, Stockout Incidents, and Order Accuracy with trend analysis.
Day-of-week demand profiles for key items built from 4-12 weeks of sales data, enabling predictive ordering that adjusts quantities based on expected trading patterns, weather, and events.

Monthly performance report covering waste percentage against target, stockout frequency, order accuracy, cost trends, and GP% impact. Used in management review meetings and quarterly business reviews.

failure_modes: Waste log is not completed consistently, making waste data unreliable and root cause analysis impossible
Stock management is treated as the manager's problem rather than a team responsibility, limiting improvement potential
Dashboard data is collected but never reviewed or acted upon, creating a system that consumes time without delivering value
Demand forecasting relies on insufficient data or ignores external factors (weather, events, school holidays)
kpis: Food waste percentage below 3% of total food cost
Zero critical stockouts (top 10 items) per week
Order accuracy above 90% (ordered quantity within 10% of actual need)
GP% improvement of at least 2 percentage points within 12 weeks of implementation
constraints: Requires B095 (Par Levels) and B096 (Mid-Service Checks) to be operational before this block can deliver full value
Demand forecasting requires a minimum of 4 weeks of sales data before producing reliable predictions
Waste tracking must be honest and complete — a culture of blame will cause staff to hide waste rather than record it

time_load.L1: 2 hours (waste log setup, staff training, initial review)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (dashboard build, demand profiling, review meeting setup)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (scorecard review, forecasting updates, supplier analysis)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily waste log review by closing team. Weekly stock management dashboard review by manager. Monthly Stock Management Scorecard review in management meeting. Quarterly comprehensive review linking stock performance to financial results.

meta.summary_for_humans: This capstone block brings together par levels, mid-service checks, waste tracking, and demand forecasting into a unified stock management system. It transforms stock management from reactive ordering into a strategic capability that directly improves GP% and reduces waste.
implementation_risks: Attempting to implement this block before B095 and B096 are stable will overwhelm the team with too many new processes simultaneously
Waste logging requires a blame-free culture — if staff fear punishment for recording waste, they will hide it instead
Small cafes may find the full dashboard and forecasting system disproportionate to their scale — scale the tools to fit the operation
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a capstone block — only activate once B095 and B096 are showing stable performance. Flag as high-value if signals indicate high waste percentages, poor GP%, or inconsistent ordering patterns.

blocks.name: Seating Turnover Strategy
blocks.purpose: Develops strategies to optimise table turnover during peak periods without making customers feel rushed, balancing the need to maximise covers with the cafe's hospitality values. This block addresses the delicate art of moving people through without pushing them out.

detailed_objectives: Analyse current seating turnover rates by time period and day of the week
Identify bottlenecks that slow table turnover: slow clearing, lingering customers, inefficient table layouts
Implement subtle turnover techniques that maintain a welcoming atmosphere
Design service flow so that table clearing and resetting happens within 2 minutes of a customer leaving
Create peak-period seating management protocols that maximise revenue per seat per hour
secondary_objectives: Reduce walkaways during peak periods caused by no available seating
Improve the perceived speed of service by eliminating waits for a clean table
Balance dine-in turnover with the takeaway channel to manage capacity overall

blocks.why_this_matters: A cafe with 30 seats and an average dwell time of 45 minutes can serve a maximum of 40 covers per hour. But if table clearing takes 5 minutes and resetting takes another 3, effective capacity drops to 30 covers — a 25% loss. During peak hours (typically 8-10am and 12-2pm in a UK cafe), every minute a table sits dirty or unoccupied represents lost revenue. At an average spend of £6-8 per customer, a single seat sitting empty for 15 unnecessary minutes during the Saturday morning rush costs the cafe £1.50-£2.00. Across 30 seats over a 4-hour peak window, this inefficiency can cost £50-£100 per day — over £1,000 per month. But turnover optimisation is not simply about speed; it is about subtlety. Customers who feel rushed leave bad reviews, stop returning, and tell their friends. The best cafes manage turnover through environmental and service design rather than direct pressure: natural service cues like presenting the bill with the second coffee, clearing plates promptly, adjusting music tempo during peak periods, and designing furniture that is comfortable for 30-45 minutes but not for hours. In the UK coffee culture, where many customers treat cafes as remote offices, the tension between dwell time and turnover is particularly acute. This block provides a structured approach to maximising revenue without sacrificing the welcoming atmosphere that defines a great cafe.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Measure current table turnover by observing and recording, for three representative days, how long each table is occupied (dwell time), how long tables sit dirty after customers leave (clearing time), and how long it takes to reset a table for the next customer (reset time). Calculate average dwell time, average clearing time, and average reset time. Most UK cafes will find dwell times of 30-60 minutes, clearing times of 3-8 minutes, and reset times of 1-3 minutes. The immediate win is almost always in clearing time — dirty tables are both lost revenue and an unpleasant sight for arriving customers. Implement a 'clear within 2 minutes' rule: when a customer leaves, the nearest available team member clears the table within 2 minutes and resets it within 1 further minute. This requires tables to be visible from the counter or service area. Week 2: Designate a team member during peak periods whose primary role is floor management — clearing, resetting, and greeting customers to available tables. Even in a small cafe, having one person focused on floor flow during the rush transforms efficiency. Train the team on natural service cues: offering the bill alongside the last drink rather than waiting to be asked, asking 'Can I get you anything else?' as a gentle signal, and ensuring WiFi and power sockets are positioned to support quick visits rather than extended stays.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Introduce a revenue-per-seat-per-hour metric and track it across different time slots and days. This metric reveals when turnover is strong and when it is weak, guiding where to focus improvement efforts. Design a peak-period service flow that differs from off-peak: during quiet periods, longer dwell times are actively welcome (they create atmosphere and potential add-on sales), but during peak periods the team shifts into a higher-tempo mode with faster clearing, proactive bill delivery, and queue management. Create a table management system — for cafes with more than 20 seats, this can be as simple as a whiteboard showing table status (available, occupied, needs clearing). Implement environmental turnover cues: slightly brighter lighting during peak, up-tempo background music, and table settings that encourage eating over working (removing power strips during lunch rush, for example). Analyse the mix of seating types — high stools and bar seating naturally encourage shorter dwell times than deep sofas, so position quick-turnover seating near the entrance for peak-period arrivals.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Seating turnover should be managed as a strategic capability linked directly to revenue optimisation. Develop a seating heat map showing which tables turn fastest and which have the longest dwell times — this data informs layout decisions and furniture investments. Experiment with reservation or time-slot systems during peak periods (increasingly common in busy UK brunch cafes). Test different table configurations seasonally — more two-tops during busy periods, community tables during quieter times. Link turnover data to the peak capacity management system (B099) so that seating and capacity are managed holistically. Review turnover strategy quarterly, adjusting for seasonal demand patterns, new menu introductions, and any changes to the local competitive landscape.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Measure current dwell time, clearing time, and reset time over 3 representative days
Implement a 'clear within 2 minutes, reset within 1 minute' rule for all tables
Designate a floor management role during peak periods for clearing and resetting
Train team on natural service cues for encouraging turnover without pressure
Ensure all tables are visible from the counter or service area for quick clearing
Position cleaning supplies within easy reach of the floor area
Observe and record how many potential customers walk away due to no available seats

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Introduce and track revenue-per-seat-per-hour metric across time slots
Design differentiated peak and off-peak service flow protocols
Create a simple table management system (whiteboard or digital)
Implement environmental turnover cues: lighting, music tempo, power access
Analyse seating mix and position quick-turnover furniture for peak arrivals
Train team on peak-period service tempo shifts
Track walkaway rates during peak periods as a capacity indicator

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Develop a seating heat map showing turnover rates by table position
Experiment with reservation or time-slot systems during peak brunch periods
Test seasonal table configurations to optimise for demand patterns
Link turnover data to peak capacity management (B099)
Review turnover strategy quarterly with revenue impact analysis

deliverables: Table Turnover Baseline Report (dwell, clearing, reset times)
Revenue Per Seat Per Hour tracking sheet

tools.description: Data collection sheet for recording dwell time, clearing time, and reset time for each table over a representative trading day. Used to establish baseline turnover metrics.
Written protocol defining the differentiated service approach during peak periods: clearing speed targets, bill delivery timing, floor management role, and environmental cue adjustments.

tools.tool_name: Revenue Per Seat Per Hour Tracker
tools.description: Weekly spreadsheet calculating revenue per available seat per hour across different time slots and days, identifying peak and underperforming periods for targeted improvement.
Visual floor plan overlay showing average turnover rate, dwell time, and revenue contribution for each table position, informing layout decisions and furniture investments.

failure_modes: Staff make customers feel rushed by clearing too aggressively, generating negative reviews and reduced return visits
The 'clear within 2 minutes' rule is ignored during peak periods when staff are focused on making drinks
Environmental cues are applied too bluntly (e.g., turning lights up so much that the cafe loses its atmosphere)
Turnover strategy focuses only on speed without considering the customer experience and lifetime value
kpis: Average table clearing time under 2 minutes during peak periods
Revenue per seat per hour improved by 15% within 8 weeks
Walkaway rate during peak periods reduced by 50%
Customer satisfaction scores maintained at or above pre-implementation levels
constraints: Turnover tactics must never make customers feel unwelcome or rushed — hospitality comes first
Environmental cues must be subtle enough that most customers do not consciously notice them
Any seating layout changes must comply with fire safety occupancy limits and accessibility requirements

time_load.L1: 3 hours (baseline measurement, rule implementation, staff training)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (metric setup, service flow design, environmental cue planning)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (heat map analysis, strategy review, layout testing)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily observation of clearing times during peak periods by shift lead. Weekly revenue-per-seat-per-hour review by manager. Monthly walkaway rate analysis. Quarterly full turnover strategy review with layout and furniture assessment.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block develops strategies to improve table turnover during peak periods without making customers feel rushed. It focuses on the practical mechanics — clearing speed, service cues, environmental design — and the strategic metrics like revenue per seat per hour that quantify the financial impact.
implementation_risks: Overly aggressive turnover tactics will damage the cafe's reputation — always err on the side of hospitality
Staff may feel uncomfortable with the shift to more active floor management if the cafe culture has been passive
Laptop workers and remote workers present a particular challenge — they stay for hours and spend minimally, requiring a separate policy
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B099 (Peak Capacity Management) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate high walkaway rates during peak periods, customer complaints about waiting for tables, or revenue-per-seat-per-hour significantly below benchmarks.

blocks.name: Peak Capacity Management
blocks.purpose: Creates a systematic approach to managing the cafe at maximum capacity — coordinating queue management, seating allocation, service speed, kitchen throughput, and team deployment to ensure that peak periods generate maximum revenue without collapsing into chaos.

detailed_objectives: Identify the cafe's true maximum capacity across all service channels (dine-in, takeaway, delivery)
Design a peak-period operating model with defined roles, positions, and service sequences
Implement queue management that keeps customers engaged and reduces perceived wait times
Coordinate kitchen and bar throughput so that neither bottleneck constrains the other
Deploy staff optimally during peak periods based on demand data rather than guesswork
secondary_objectives: Reduce customer walkaway rates during the busiest trading hours
Maintain service quality and drink/food standards under peak pressure
Build team confidence and resilience through structured peak-period routines

blocks.why_this_matters: Peak periods define a cafe's financial viability. In a typical UK high-street cafe, 60-70% of daily revenue is generated in just 4-5 hours — the morning rush (7:30-10:00am) and the lunch window (12:00-2:00pm). How well a cafe performs during these compressed windows determines whether it is profitable or struggling. The challenge is that peak periods are inherently stressful: orders stack up, the queue grows, customers become impatient, staff rush and make mistakes, drink quality drops, food takes longer, and the atmosphere shifts from welcoming to frantic. Without a structured peak management system, each busy period feels like a crisis rather than an expected and manageable event. The difference between a cafe that handles 80 customers per hour smoothly and one that descends into visible chaos at 50 customers per hour is not talent or luck — it is preparation, positioning, and protocol. Peak capacity management treats the rush as a planned operational mode with its own rules, roles, and rhythms. Staff know their positions, the service sequence is optimised for speed, the queue is managed rather than ignored, and the kitchen operates to a throughput plan rather than reacting to individual orders. Cafes that master peak management consistently capture 15-25% more revenue during their busiest hours than those that do not, simply by serving more customers without losing quality or turning people away.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Define the cafe's peak periods precisely by reviewing till data or, if no data exists, observing footfall patterns over a full week. Record the time each peak starts and ends, the approximate number of customers per 15-minute interval, and the current service speed (time from order to delivery). Identify the primary bottleneck during peak: is it the queue (too many people waiting to order), the espresso machine (drinks taking too long), the kitchen (food orders backing up), or the till (payment processing too slow)? Address the bottleneck first. Assign clear peak-period positions: during the rush, Person A is on espresso, Person B is on till, Person C is on food prep, Person D is on floor/clearing. Nobody moves from their position during peak. Create a 'peak mode' signal — a verbal call or visible sign — that tells the team the rush is starting and everyone should be in position. Week 2: Implement queue management basics: ensure the queue has a clear path that does not block the door, place a menu board visible from the queue so customers can decide before they reach the till, and train the till operator to acknowledge arriving customers even when serving someone else ('I'll be right with you'). If the queue regularly extends outside, consider a queue barrier or an A-board directing flow.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Peak Period Operating Manual — a one-page document that defines: peak period times, team positions, service sequence, quality non-negotiables (e.g., 'we never skip latte art, even in the rush'), and escalation triggers (e.g., 'if the queue reaches 10 people, call the manager to open a second till'). Implement a peak-period staffing model based on demand data: schedule your strongest barista and fastest food preparer for peak shifts, and ensure minimum staffing levels are met (never run a peak with fewer than X people). Introduce a peak-period drinks menu that streamlines preparation without reducing customer choice — pre-batch filter coffee, pre-portion milk for common sizes, and keep the most popular syrups within arm's reach. Track peak-period performance metrics weekly: customers served per hour, average transaction time, walkaway count, and quality checks (spot-checking drink quality during the rush). Link seating turnover strategy (B098) to peak management so that table availability and service speed are coordinated.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Peak periods should feel like a well-rehearsed performance, not a scramble. Introduce pre-peak briefings: a 2-minute team huddle before each rush covering expected volume, any specials or shortages, and position assignments. Implement real-time capacity monitoring — track how many customers are in the cafe and how many are in the queue, using this data to make in-the-moment decisions about tempo and staffing. Develop contingency plans for extreme peaks (bank holidays, local events, competitor closures): what happens when demand exceeds normal capacity by 30%? Create a peak performance scorecard that tracks key metrics over time and sets improvement targets each quarter. Consider physical improvements that increase peak throughput: a second till point, a dedicated takeaway collection area, or a prep station positioned for peak-period efficiency. Review and update the peak operating model seasonally as demand patterns shift.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Map peak periods precisely using till data or direct observation
Identify the primary service bottleneck during each peak window
Assign clear fixed positions for each team member during peak periods
Create a 'peak mode' signal to trigger the team into peak positions
Implement basic queue management: clear path, visible menu, verbal acknowledgement
Ensure no team member moves from their peak position during the rush
Record customer counts and walkaway observations during peak for one week

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a one-page Peak Period Operating Manual
Build a peak-period staffing model based on demand data
Streamline the peak-period menu preparation (batch coffee, pre-portion milk)
Track weekly peak metrics: customers/hour, transaction time, walkaways, quality
Implement escalation triggers for extreme queue lengths
Link seating turnover (B098) to peak management coordination
Schedule strongest performers for peak shifts consistently

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce 2-minute pre-peak team briefings before each rush
Implement real-time capacity monitoring during peak periods
Develop contingency plans for extreme peak events
Create a peak performance scorecard with quarterly improvement targets
Review and update the peak operating model seasonally

deliverables: Peak Period Map (times, volumes, bottleneck analysis)
Peak Staffing Model with minimum levels

tools.description: Data collection sheet for recording customer volumes by 15-minute intervals, queue lengths, service times, and walkaway counts during peak periods over a representative week.
One-page reference document defining peak period times, team positions, service sequence, quality non-negotiables, and escalation triggers. Laminated and posted in the staff area.
Staffing model that maps required team members and their positions to expected demand levels during each peak period, ensuring minimum coverage is always met.
Monthly scorecard tracking peak-period customers served per hour, average transaction time, walkaway rate, quality check scores, and revenue-per-peak-hour trends.

failure_modes: Staff abandon their assigned peak positions to help elsewhere, creating gaps in the service chain
Queue management is neglected because the team is focused on making drinks, leading to walkaways and frustration
Quality standards drop during peak as staff rush to clear the queue — poorly made drinks cost more in returns and reputation than slightly longer waits
Peak staffing is not consistently maintained due to absence, holiday, or labour budget constraints
kpis: Customers served per peak hour increased by 20% within 8 weeks
Average order-to-delivery time under 4 minutes during peak periods
Walkaway rate during peak periods below 5%
Drink and food quality spot-checks passing at 95% during peak service
constraints: Peak capacity must not exceed fire safety occupancy limits at any time
Quality standards must be maintained during peak — speed cannot compromise food safety or drink quality
Peak staffing models must account for staff breaks, ensuring nobody works a full peak period without a rest

time_load.L1: 3 hours (observation, position assignment, queue setup, staff briefing)
time_load.L2: 5 hours (operating manual, staffing model, metric tracking, menu streamlining)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (scorecard review, contingency updates, seasonal adjustments)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily peak period debrief between shift lead and manager (2 minutes). Weekly peak metrics review by manager. Monthly peak performance scorecard in management meeting. Quarterly peak operating model review and seasonal adjustment.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a structured approach to managing the cafe at maximum capacity — assigning positions, managing queues, coordinating kitchen and bar throughput, and deploying staff optimally. It transforms peak periods from stressful chaos into a planned, repeatable operational mode.
implementation_risks: Staff may resist fixed positions during peak if they are used to a more flexible, informal approach
Over-optimising for speed can erode the cafe's welcoming atmosphere — balance efficiency with hospitality
Peak management is only as strong as the weakest link — if one team member is absent or underperforming, the system breaks down
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B098 (Seating Turnover Strategy) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate long queue times, high walkaway rates, customer complaints about speed, or revenue-per-hour significantly below potential during peak periods.

blocks.name: Seating Experience Optimisation
blocks.purpose: Balances the tension between maximising seating turnover for revenue and providing a comfortable, welcoming experience that encourages return visits. This capstone block integrates turnover strategy (B098) and capacity management (B099) into a holistic seating philosophy that serves both the business and the customer.

detailed_objectives: Define the cafe's seating philosophy — where it sits on the spectrum from fast-casual to lounge
Design seating zones that cater to different customer needs: quick coffee, working lunch, social gathering, remote working
Optimise furniture selection for both comfort and turnover — different seat types drive different dwell times
Create a seating layout that maximises covers while maintaining comfortable spacing and flow
Develop policies for peak versus off-peak seating management that flex with demand
secondary_objectives: Improve customer satisfaction scores related to seating comfort and availability
Reduce conflict with laptop workers and long-stay customers through proactive policy design
Increase average spend per visit by creating seating environments that encourage food ordering alongside drinks

blocks.why_this_matters: Seating is the most tangible expression of a cafe's brand and values. A cafe filled with hard wooden stools and narrow ledges is saying 'drink and go.' A cafe with deep sofas and power sockets everywhere is saying 'stay all day.' Neither extreme is ideal for a business trying to balance revenue, atmosphere, and customer satisfaction. The most successful UK cafes design their seating intentionally, creating zones that serve different customer missions: a quick two-top near the door for the commuter grabbing a flat white, a comfortable booth for the mum meeting a friend for an hour, a communal table for the remote worker who needs space, and a cosy corner for the couple on a weekend brunch date. Each zone has different furniture, different proximity to power, and subtly different service expectations. This is not interior design for its own sake — it is revenue engineering. A cafe that converts even 10% of its takeaway-only customers to dine-in customers who also order food has increased average transaction value by 40-60%. A cafe that resolves the laptop worker problem — people occupying a four-top for three hours while buying a single Americano — without alienating those customers has recaptured significant peak-period revenue. Seating Experience Optimisation brings together the operational work from B098 and B099 with customer experience design to create a seating strategy that is commercially smart, operationally efficient, and genuinely hospitable.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit every seat in the cafe. For each table, record: table size (2-top, 4-top, communal), furniture type (stool, chair, bench, sofa), proximity to power sockets, visibility from the counter, natural light, and current average dwell time (use data from B098). Create a simple floor plan marking each table position. Observe customer behaviour for a full week: which tables are chosen first? Which are avoided? Where do laptop workers sit? Where do groups cluster? Where do single customers go? This observation reveals the cafe's natural seating zones — areas that customers have organically designated for different uses. Week 2: Based on observations, sketch a rough zoning plan: Quick Zone (near the door, higher stools or counter seating, no power sockets — for takeaway-to-dine-in converts and quick visits), Social Zone (comfortable chairs around standard tables — for friends, meetings, and meals), and Focus Zone (if space permits, quieter area with power access — for remote workers and students). Assess whether the current furniture arrangement supports these zones or works against them. Make any quick-win changes: move power strips away from high-turnover areas, swap a low sofa out of a prime window spot for a two-top that will turn over faster.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a formal Seating Strategy Document that defines the cafe's zoning plan, furniture specifications for each zone, and management policies for peak versus off-peak. Create a laptop and remote working policy — many successful UK cafes now implement a 'one drink per hour' minimum, limit WiFi to 90 minutes during peak, or designate specific tables as laptop-friendly only during off-peak hours. Communicate this policy with signage that is friendly and clear, not hostile. Analyse revenue data by seating zone to understand which areas generate the most revenue per square foot — this data should inform any future furniture purchases or layout changes. Implement seasonal seating adjustments: outdoor seating for summer (council licence required in most UK local authorities), festive table decorations in December, lighter tablecloths in spring. Design the service flow so staff naturally move through all seating zones during peak, ensuring no area is neglected. Consider accessibility requirements under the Equality Act 2010 — wheelchair access to tables, space for pushchairs, hearing loop awareness.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Seating experience should be continuously refined based on customer feedback and revenue data. Conduct a bi-annual customer survey specifically about seating — comfort, availability, atmosphere, noise levels. Test different furniture arrangements for 2-4 weeks and measure the impact on revenue per seat per hour, customer satisfaction, and dwell time. Explore innovative seating concepts: a standing coffee bar for ultra-quick service during morning peak, a bookable private area for small group meetings, or a seasonal outdoor terrace with its own service flow. Integrate seating data into the broader business planning: when considering menu changes, promotions, or events, assess the seating implications. Build the seating experience into the cafe's brand narrative — 'a seat for every mood' — so that regular customers know exactly where they will sit depending on what they need that day. Review the entire seating strategy annually alongside any planned refurbishment or equipment investment.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit every seat: table size, furniture type, power access, light, average dwell time
Create a floor plan marking all table positions and current characteristics
Observe customer seating behaviour for one full week to identify natural zones
Sketch a preliminary zoning plan: Quick Zone, Social Zone, Focus Zone
Make quick-win furniture and power socket adjustments to support zones
Move high-turnover seating near the entrance and door
Position comfortable seating away from the highest-traffic areas

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a formal Seating Strategy Document with zoning and furniture specifications
Create and communicate a laptop and remote working policy
Analyse revenue by seating zone to identify highest-value areas
Implement seasonal seating adjustments (outdoor, festive, etc.)
Ensure service flow covers all seating zones during peak periods
Review accessibility compliance under the Equality Act 2010
Design signage that communicates seating policies warmly and clearly

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct bi-annual customer surveys specifically about seating experience
Test different furniture arrangements and measure revenue impact over 2-4 weeks
Explore innovative seating concepts (standing bar, bookable areas, terraces)
Integrate seating data into broader business planning and menu decisions
Review the entire seating strategy annually alongside refurbishment plans

deliverables: Seating Audit Report with floor plan
Seating Strategy Document with zoning plan

tools.description: Table-by-table audit template recording table number, size, furniture type, power access, natural light, average dwell time, and observed customer preference patterns.
Formal document defining the cafe's seating zones, furniture specifications, peak/off-peak policies, laptop worker guidelines, and accessibility provisions.
Monthly analysis of revenue generated per seating zone, revenue per square foot, and dwell time by zone. Identifies highest-performing areas and underperforming sections.
Bi-annual customer survey template covering seating comfort, availability during peak, atmosphere, noise levels, and overall satisfaction with the cafe's seating experience.

failure_modes: Zoning is designed on paper but never communicated to staff, so they seat customers randomly without regard to zone purpose
Laptop worker policy is implemented too aggressively, alienating a loyal customer segment and generating negative reviews
Furniture is chosen for aesthetics rather than function, resulting in seats that look great but discourage the desired dwell time
Accessibility requirements are overlooked, creating legal risk and excluding customers with disabilities or mobility needs
kpis: Revenue per seat per hour improved by 10% within 12 weeks of zoning implementation
Customer seating satisfaction score of 4.0 or above out of 5.0 in bi-annual surveys
Peak-period table availability improved — walkaway rate reduced by 30%
Average dine-in spend per customer increased by 15% through zoning and experience design
constraints: Seating layout must comply with fire safety occupancy limits and emergency exit access
All seating must meet accessibility requirements under the Equality Act 2010
Outdoor seating requires a pavement licence from the local authority (Licensing Act or Business and Planning Act provisions)
Furniture changes must be budgeted and phased — avoid trying to redesign the entire cafe at once

time_load.L1: 3 hours (audit, observation, quick-win adjustments)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (strategy document, policy creation, zone implementation, signage)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/half-year (surveys, furniture testing, strategy review)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly observation of zone effectiveness during peak by manager. Monthly revenue-per-zone review. Bi-annual customer seating satisfaction survey. Annual full seating strategy review aligned with business planning.

meta.summary_for_humans: This capstone block balances turnover and comfort by designing seating zones, furniture choices, and management policies that serve different customer needs. It integrates the work from B098 and B099 into a cohesive seating philosophy that maximises revenue while maintaining a genuinely welcoming atmosphere.
implementation_risks: Seating redesigns can be expensive — prioritise changes that deliver the highest revenue impact per pound spent
Staff may struggle to enforce seating policies (e.g., laptop limits) if the rationale is not clearly explained
Customer habits take time to shift — a new zoning plan may take 4-6 weeks to become established behaviour
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a capstone block — activate after B098 and B099 are operational. Flag as high-value if signals indicate customer complaints about seating, revenue per square foot significantly below benchmarks, or conflict with laptop workers.

blocks.name: Delivery Workflow Integration
blocks.purpose: Integrates delivery order fulfilment into the cafe's existing operational workflow so that Deliveroo, Uber Eats, and Just Eat orders are processed smoothly alongside dine-in and takeaway service without cannibalising in-store capacity or creating chaos during peak periods.

detailed_objectives: Design a delivery order workflow that slots into existing kitchen and bar operations without disruption
Establish a dedicated delivery staging area where orders are assembled and collected by riders
Create clear order prioritisation rules balancing delivery, dine-in, and takeaway during peak periods
Manage delivery tablet alerts and order acceptance so the team is never overwhelmed by simultaneous orders
Ensure delivery orders maintain the same quality standards as dine-in orders despite the packaging and transit requirements
secondary_objectives: Reduce delivery order errors (wrong items, missing items) to below 2% of total delivery orders
Minimise rider wait time to maintain strong platform ratings and avoid penalties
Protect dine-in customer experience from being degraded by delivery order volume

blocks.why_this_matters: Delivery has become a significant revenue channel for UK cafes, with Deliveroo, Uber Eats, and Just Eat collectively processing millions of orders daily. For many independent cafes, delivery now represents 15-30% of total revenue. But adding delivery to a cafe that was designed for dine-in and takeaway creates operational friction that, if unmanaged, damages both channels. The kitchen receives three order streams — dine-in tickets, till-based takeaways, and tablet-based deliveries — with no single system coordinating them. During peak periods, delivery orders stack up while the team prioritises the visible queue of in-store customers, causing delivery times to spike, platform ratings to drop, and commission charges to feel increasingly unjustifiable. Meanwhile, delivery riders cluster near the counter, blocking flow and creating congestion. Food sits waiting for collection, losing quality by the minute. Staff feel overwhelmed because they have no clear protocol for how to balance the competing demands. The solution is not to limit delivery volume but to integrate it properly. This means a designated staging area, a clear priority sequence, controlled order acceptance during peak, and team members who understand their role in the delivery workflow. A well-integrated delivery operation adds revenue without adding chaos. A poorly integrated one drains margin, stresses staff, and degrades the experience for every customer — in-store and delivery alike.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Designate a Delivery Staging Area — a specific counter, shelf, or table where all delivery orders are placed once prepared, clearly separated from the dine-in and takeaway collection points. This area should be near the door or a secondary entrance to minimise rider traffic through the cafe. Label the area clearly and brief all riders on where to collect. Place the delivery platform tablets (Deliveroo, Uber Eats, Just Eat) in a single location visible to the person managing orders — not scattered around the cafe. Assign responsibility for delivery order management to a specific team member during each shift (this can be the same person doing other tasks during quiet periods, but they own the delivery flow). Establish a simple priority rule for peak periods: dine-in orders first, then takeaway, then delivery. This can be adjusted based on the cafe's revenue mix, but having a default prevents arguments and confusion. Week 2: Time the delivery process from order acceptance to rider collection. Identify where delays occur: is it in preparation (kitchen or bar), assembly (putting the order together with correct packaging), or staging (order sitting waiting for the rider)? Address the largest delay first. Set a target: all delivery orders prepared and staged within 12 minutes of acceptance.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Create a Delivery Order SOP covering: how to accept orders on each platform, how to communicate delivery orders to the kitchen/bar (printed ticket vs verbal), how to assemble and package orders (linking to B102 Delivery Packaging Standards), how to manage rider collection, and how to handle order issues (missing items, customer complaints through the platform). Implement order throttling during extreme peak periods — all major delivery platforms allow cafes to increase prep time or pause ordering temporarily. Define the triggers for throttling: 'If the in-store queue exceeds 8 people, increase delivery prep time to 25 minutes. If it exceeds 12, pause delivery for 15 minutes.' Train the team to use these tools proactively rather than drowning under orders. Introduce a delivery order tracking system — even a simple whiteboard listing current delivery orders, their status, and expected collection time — so the whole team has visibility. Track delivery performance metrics weekly: order accuracy, average prep time, rider wait time, platform ratings, and delivery revenue as a percentage of total.
