application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Delivery should be a fully integrated revenue channel, not an afterthought. Optimise the delivery menu separately from the dine-in menu — not all items travel well, and the delivery menu should feature items that maintain quality through packaging and transit (see B102). Negotiate platform commission rates based on volume data — cafes doing 50+ orders per week on a single platform have leverage to negotiate reduced commission. Explore direct ordering (via the cafe's own website or app) to capture delivery revenue without platform commissions. Analyse delivery profitability per item — some items may generate revenue but zero profit after commission and packaging costs. Use delivery data to identify new customer acquisition: how many delivery customers have never visited the physical cafe, and what would convert them? Review the delivery workflow quarterly, adjusting for seasonal demand, menu changes, and platform policy updates.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Designate a delivery staging area near the door, clearly labelled and separate from dine-in collection
Consolidate all delivery platform tablets in a single visible location
Assign delivery order ownership to a specific team member per shift
Establish a default priority sequence: dine-in, takeaway, delivery during peak
Brief riders on the designated staging area and collection process
Time the delivery process end-to-end and identify the primary delay point
Set a target of 12 minutes from order acceptance to staged and ready

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a Delivery Order SOP covering acceptance, communication, assembly, and issue handling
Implement order throttling triggers for extreme peak periods
Create a delivery order tracking system (whiteboard or digital)
Train team on platform tools for adjusting prep time and pausing orders
Track weekly delivery metrics: accuracy, prep time, rider wait, platform ratings
Link delivery workflow to packaging standards (B102)
Develop a process for handling delivery order complaints and refunds

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Optimise the delivery menu separately from the dine-in menu for travel suitability
Negotiate platform commission rates based on volume data
Explore direct ordering channels to reduce commission dependency
Analyse delivery profitability per item including commission and packaging costs
Review the delivery workflow quarterly for seasonal and platform changes

deliverables: Delivery Staging Area setup and signage

tools.description: Standard Operating Procedure covering the full delivery order lifecycle: tablet management, order acceptance, kitchen/bar communication, packaging, staging, rider collection, and complaint handling.
Simple whiteboard or digital display listing all active delivery orders with order number, platform, items, status (preparing/ready/collected), and expected rider arrival time.
Quick-reference card defining when and how to adjust delivery prep times or pause delivery platforms based on in-store queue length and kitchen capacity.
Weekly dashboard tracking delivery order accuracy, average prep time, rider wait time, platform ratings, commission costs, and delivery revenue as a percentage of total turnover.

failure_modes: Delivery orders are accepted during peak without throttling, overwhelming the kitchen and degrading dine-in service quality
No designated staging area means delivery orders sit on the counter alongside dine-in orders, causing mix-ups and congestion
Riders are not briefed on the collection process and wander through the cafe looking for their order, disrupting customers
Platform ratings drop due to slow prep times, triggering reduced visibility and lower order volumes in a downward spiral
kpis: Delivery order accuracy above 98% (correct items, correct quantities)
Average delivery order prep time under 12 minutes from acceptance to staged
Rider wait time at staging area under 3 minutes on average
Platform ratings maintained at 4.5 or above across all delivery partners
constraints: Platform commission rates (typically 25-35%) must be factored into delivery menu pricing to maintain margins
Delivery staging area must not obstruct fire exits, customer flow, or accessibility routes
Delivery order throttling during peak must be agreed with platform account managers to avoid penalties

time_load.L1: 2 hours (staging area setup, tablet consolidation, staff briefing)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (SOP creation, throttling protocol, tracking system, team training)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (performance review, menu optimisation, commission negotiation)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily delivery order accuracy check by shift lead. Weekly delivery performance metrics review by manager. Monthly delivery revenue and profitability review. Quarterly delivery workflow and platform relationship review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block integrates delivery order fulfilment (Deliveroo, Uber Eats, Just Eat) into the cafe's workflow with a dedicated staging area, clear priority rules, throttling protocols for peak periods, and performance tracking. It prevents delivery from cannibalising dine-in quality while capturing significant incremental revenue.
implementation_risks: Platform commission rates can erode margins if delivery menu pricing does not account for them
Staff may resent delivery orders as 'extra work' if the workflow is not properly integrated and delivery is not positioned as a valuable revenue stream
Pausing deliveries during peak may harm platform rankings — negotiate with platform managers before implementing
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B102 (Delivery Packaging Standards) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate poor platform ratings, high delivery complaint rates, or delivery orders disrupting in-store service quality.

blocks.name: Delivery Packaging Standards
blocks.purpose: Defines packaging standards that ensure food and drink quality is maintained from cafe to customer's door, covering material selection, assembly techniques, labelling, and presentation. This block recognises that the delivery packaging is the cafe's brand ambassador when the customer cannot experience the physical space.

detailed_objectives: Select packaging materials that maintain food temperature, prevent leakage, and present professionally
Create assembly standards for each delivery menu category (hot drinks, cold drinks, sandwiches, salads, pastries, hot food)
Ensure all packaging meets food safety labelling requirements (allergens, use-by dates, business name and address)
Balance packaging quality with cost — every penny of packaging comes directly off the margin
Reduce environmental impact through sustainable packaging choices that align with customer expectations
secondary_objectives: Build brand recognition through consistent, branded packaging that stands out on the doorstep
Reduce delivery complaint rates caused by spills, temperature loss, or damaged items
Ensure compliance with UK food labelling regulations for distance selling (Food Information Regulations 2014)

blocks.why_this_matters: When a customer orders from a cafe through Deliveroo, Uber Eats, or Just Eat, the packaging is the cafe. There is no beautifully designed interior, no friendly barista, no aroma of fresh coffee. There is only a bag, a box, and whatever is inside. If the sandwich arrives crushed, the soup has leaked, or the coffee is lukewarm, the customer's entire impression of the cafe is shaped by that single experience. Delivery platforms display star ratings prominently, and packaging failures drive negative reviews that suppress future order volumes. In practical terms, a poorly packaged delivery order costs the cafe twice: once in the immediate refund or credit the platform issues to the unhappy customer (often without even consulting the cafe), and again in the reduced visibility and order volume that follows declining ratings. UK food labelling regulations for distance selling add a compliance dimension — all food delivered to a customer must include the business name and address, a full list of ingredients with allergens emphasised, use-by or best-before dates where applicable, and storage instructions. Non-compliance can result in enforcement action from Trading Standards. Packaging cost is a critical consideration: the average UK cafe spends 3-6% of delivery revenue on packaging alone, and this comes directly off the already commission-squeezed margin. Every packaging decision must balance quality, cost, sustainability, and regulatory compliance simultaneously.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit current delivery packaging by ordering from the cafe's own delivery platforms (or asking a friend to) and assessing the customer experience: Does the food arrive at the correct temperature? Is there any leakage? Are items separated appropriately? Is the packaging clean and professional? Is allergen information included? Compile a list of every packaging item currently used: cups, lids, bags, boxes, wraps, napkins, cutlery, stickers, labels. For each, record the supplier, unit cost, and whether it meets the need. Identify the top 3 packaging failures from delivery reviews and platform feedback — these are the problems to solve first. Week 2: Source improved packaging for the identified problem areas. Key priorities: double-wall or insulated cups for hot drinks (the standard single-wall cup loses 10°C in 15 minutes), leak-proof containers for soups and sauces, and rigid boxes for items that crush easily (pastries, cakes, salads). Ensure all packaging can carry a printed or stickered label with the cafe name, allergen information, and use-by date. Create a simple packaging assembly guide — a laminated card showing which container to use for each menu item and how to assemble the order (e.g., 'place salad in large deli box, dressing in a separate pot, cutlery in the bag').
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Delivery Packaging SOP that covers every menu item available on delivery: which container, which lid, how to seal, how to label, and how to bag. Include photos for visual reference so any team member can pack an order correctly. Introduce branded packaging elements — even a simple branded sticker on a plain kraft bag significantly improves brand recognition and customer perception. Calculate the true packaging cost per delivery order and factor this into delivery menu pricing (see B111 and B114). Source sustainable packaging where financially viable — UK customers increasingly expect recyclable or compostable packaging, and platforms like Deliveroo highlight eco-friendly packaging in their app. Test packaging performance: prepare a delivery order, package it, leave it for 20 minutes (simulating delivery time), then assess temperature, presentation, and structural integrity. This simple test reveals problems before customers do. Establish a packaging stock check as part of the daily mid-service check (B096) to ensure the cafe never runs out of delivery packaging during a busy service.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Packaging should be a brand-building investment, not just a cost to minimise. Develop seasonal packaging variations — a festive sticker in December, a summer design in July — that create social media-worthy unboxing moments. Negotiate packaging supply agreements with bulk pricing, reviewing at least quarterly. Track delivery packaging complaints separately from food quality complaints to isolate packaging-specific issues. Explore branded insulated bags that riders can use (some platforms facilitate this). Investigate compostable packaging certifications (PAS 9017, EN 13432) and communicate sustainability commitments to customers via packaging inserts or stickers. Review packaging standards quarterly, incorporating customer feedback, cost data, and sustainability developments.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit current delivery packaging by ordering from the cafe and assessing the experience
Inventory all current packaging items with suppliers and unit costs
Identify top 3 packaging failures from delivery reviews and platform feedback
Source improved packaging for the identified problem areas (insulated cups, leak-proof containers)
Create a laminated packaging assembly guide for each delivery menu item
Ensure all packaging carries cafe name, allergen info, and use-by date as required
Place packaging supplies in a logical, accessible order near the delivery staging area

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Delivery Packaging SOP with photos for every menu item
Introduce branded packaging elements (stickers, stamped bags, printed cups)
Calculate true packaging cost per delivery order and adjust menu pricing accordingly
Source sustainable packaging where financially viable
Conduct packaging performance tests simulating 20-minute delivery times
Add delivery packaging to daily mid-service stock check (B096)
Train all team members on correct packaging assembly using the SOP

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Develop seasonal packaging variations for brand-building moments
Negotiate bulk packaging supply agreements with quarterly review
Track delivery packaging complaints separately from food quality complaints
Explore compostable packaging certifications and sustainability communication
Review packaging standards quarterly incorporating feedback and cost data

deliverables: Packaging Audit Report with cost and quality assessment

tools.description: Laminated visual guide showing which container, lid, label, and bag to use for each delivery menu item, with assembly instructions and photos for consistent order packaging.
Detailed SOP covering packaging materials, assembly procedures, allergen labelling requirements, quality checks before handoff, and sustainability standards.
Spreadsheet calculating the total packaging cost per delivery order by menu item, including all components (container, lid, bag, label, cutlery, napkin), feeding into delivery menu pricing.
Test log for evaluating packaging performance under simulated delivery conditions: temperature retention, structural integrity, leak prevention, and visual presentation after 20 minutes.

failure_modes: Packaging is chosen on cost alone, resulting in poor temperature retention, leaks, and crushed items that drive negative reviews
Allergen labelling is incomplete or missing, creating legal liability under Food Information Regulations 2014
Team members assemble delivery orders inconsistently because no packaging SOP exists, leading to variable quality
Packaging stock runs out during a busy delivery period because it was not included in routine stock checks
kpis: Delivery packaging complaint rate below 2% of total delivery orders
100% of delivery orders include correct allergen labelling and business information
Packaging cost maintained between 3-6% of delivery order revenue
Packaging performance test pass rate above 95% (temperature, integrity, presentation)
constraints: All delivery food must comply with Food Information Regulations 2014 for distance selling (allergen info, business name and address)
Packaging costs must be factored into delivery menu pricing to protect margins after platform commission
Sustainable packaging choices must not compromise food safety or temperature maintenance

time_load.L1: 3 hours (audit, sourcing, assembly guide creation)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (SOP development, cost calculation, performance testing, staff training)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/quarter (review, sustainability updates, supplier negotiation)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily visual check of packaging assembly by shift lead. Weekly delivery complaint review with packaging analysis. Monthly packaging cost review. Quarterly full packaging standards review with performance testing.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block sets packaging standards for delivery orders so food and drink arrive in excellent condition. It covers material selection, assembly procedures, allergen labelling compliance, cost management, and sustainability — recognising that delivery packaging is the cafe's brand ambassador when the customer never visits the physical space.
implementation_risks: Premium packaging significantly increases per-order cost — ensure delivery menu pricing accounts for this
Sustainable packaging is often more expensive and may not perform as well for hot items — test before committing
Allergen labelling for delivery is a legal requirement that many small cafes overlook — non-compliance risk is real
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B101 (Delivery Workflow Integration) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate high delivery complaint rates related to packaging, missing allergen information, or delivery platform ratings below 4.0.

blocks.name: Delivery Channel Strategy
blocks.purpose: Develops a strategic approach to delivery platform partnerships — determining which platforms to use, how to negotiate terms, when to invest in direct ordering, and how to optimise the delivery channel as a profitable revenue stream rather than a margin-eroding necessity.

detailed_objectives: Evaluate each delivery platform (Deliveroo, Uber Eats, Just Eat) for suitability based on the cafe's location, customer base, and menu
Negotiate commission rates and promotional terms that protect margin
Develop a delivery-specific menu optimised for items that travel well and generate acceptable profit after commission
Explore direct ordering channels (own website, app, phone) to capture delivery revenue without platform commission
Build a delivery channel P&L so the true profitability of delivery is understood and managed
secondary_objectives: Use delivery as a customer acquisition channel — converting delivery customers into dine-in regulars
Leverage platform marketing tools (promoted listings, offers) strategically rather than reactively
Build a delivery brand presence that complements rather than competes with the dine-in brand

blocks.why_this_matters: Delivery platform commissions in the UK typically range from 25-35% of the order value — a charge that can erase profit entirely if the delivery menu is not priced and managed strategically. Many independent cafe owners view delivery as 'free extra revenue' without recognising that after commission, packaging, and the operational cost of fulfilling orders, some delivery items generate negative margin. A £4.50 flat white delivered via Deliveroo at 30% commission nets the cafe £3.15 before packaging (say 30p for an insulated cup and lid), leaving £2.85 — against a coffee cost of approximately £0.90 and labour allocation of at least £0.50, the gross margin is around £1.45 or 32%. Compare this to the same flat white sold in-store at £4.50 with a GP of over 75%. Delivery is profitable only when managed strategically: pricing delivery items higher than dine-in (platforms allow independent pricing), curating a delivery menu that excludes low-margin items, negotiating commission rates based on volume, and investing in direct ordering channels that bypass commissions entirely. For a cafe doing £2,000 per week in delivery revenue at 30% average commission, the annual commission bill is over £31,000. Reducing commission by 5 percentage points — achievable through negotiation and direct ordering — saves over £5,000 per year. The delivery channel strategy determines whether delivery is a profit centre or a public charity that subsidises platform shareholders at the cafe owner's expense.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit current delivery platform performance. For each platform the cafe is listed on, gather: total orders per week, average order value, commission rate, current platform rating, and any promotional spend. If the cafe is not yet on delivery platforms, research which platforms are most active in the local area — this varies significantly by UK region and town size. Deliveroo tends to dominate in urban areas, Just Eat has wider UK coverage including smaller towns, and Uber Eats sits between the two. Contact each platform's local sales representative and request current commission terms — do not accept the first offer. Calculate the current gross margin on the top 10 delivery items after commission and packaging costs. If any items generate a gross margin below 40%, they are candidates for price adjustment or removal from the delivery menu. Week 2: Adjust delivery menu pricing if the current prices match dine-in prices. Most platforms allow independent pricing, and delivery customers expect to pay a premium of 10-20% over dine-in prices. Remove any items that generate unacceptable margins after commission — not everything on the dine-in menu belongs on the delivery menu.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Build a Delivery Channel P&L that tracks delivery revenue, commission costs, packaging costs, estimated labour allocation, and resulting net margin by platform. Review this P&L monthly to identify which platform is most profitable. Develop relationships with platform account managers — regular contact (monthly calls) positions the cafe for promotional support, preferential placement, and commission negotiation when volume warrants it. Create a delivery-specific marketing calendar using platform promotional tools: featured listings, percentage-off offers during quiet periods, and new item launches. Calculate the return on promotional spend — a 20% discount offer must generate enough incremental volume to be worthwhile after the increased commission on the higher order count. Explore direct ordering: set up a simple ordering page on the cafe's website using a free or low-cost tool (e.g., Square Online, Shopify), and promote it with in-store signage ('Order for delivery directly and save') and social media. Even capturing 10% of current delivery orders directly saves significant commission.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Delivery strategy should be managed as a distinct business channel with its own targets, metrics, and optimisation cycle. Conduct quarterly platform reviews comparing performance, commission rates, and customer acquisition across all channels. Negotiate commission reductions based on demonstrated volume — platforms regularly offer reduced rates to retain high-performing partners. Build a loyalty mechanism for direct ordering customers (e.g., 10th delivery free) to shift volume away from platforms over time. Analyse delivery customer data to understand demographics, order patterns, and conversion potential — who are these customers and can they be encouraged to visit in-store? Evaluate emerging delivery models: dark kitchen partnerships, collection-only delivery listings, and micro-delivery zones. Review the entire delivery channel strategy bi-annually, aligning with the cafe's broader business plan and growth objectives.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit current delivery platform performance: orders, revenue, commission, ratings
Research platform activity in the local area if not yet listed
Contact platform sales representatives and negotiate commission terms
Calculate gross margin on top 10 delivery items after commission and packaging
Adjust delivery menu pricing to 10-20% above dine-in prices where platforms allow
Remove low-margin items from the delivery menu
Ensure delivery menu descriptions and photos are accurate and appealing

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build a Delivery Channel P&L tracking revenue, commission, packaging, and net margin by platform
Establish monthly contact with platform account managers
Create a delivery marketing calendar using platform promotional tools
Calculate ROI on each promotional campaign before committing spend
Set up a direct ordering channel on the cafe's website
Promote direct ordering with in-store signage and social media
Review delivery P&L monthly and adjust strategy accordingly

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly platform performance reviews with commission renegotiation
Build a loyalty mechanism for direct ordering customers
Analyse delivery customer data for demographics and conversion potential
Evaluate emerging delivery models (dark kitchen, collection-only, micro-zones)
Review full delivery channel strategy bi-annually aligned with business plan

deliverables: Delivery Menu with optimised pricing and item selection

tools.description: Audit template for evaluating each delivery platform's performance: weekly orders, average order value, commission rate, platform rating, promotional spend, and estimated net margin.
Monthly P&L tracking delivery revenue by platform, commission costs, packaging costs, labour allocation, promotional spend, and resulting net margin with trend analysis.
Item-by-item margin calculator for the delivery menu, showing dine-in price, delivery price, ingredient cost, packaging cost, commission at various rates, and resulting GP% for each item.
Preparation template for platform commission negotiations: current volume data, competitor commission intelligence, negotiation targets, and fallback positions.

failure_modes: Delivery menu pricing mirrors dine-in pricing, resulting in unacceptable margins after 25-35% commission
Commission rates are accepted without negotiation, leaving significant money on the table
Promotional spend on platforms is deployed reactively without ROI calculation, burning margin without measurable return
Direct ordering is not explored, leaving the cafe permanently dependent on high-commission platforms
kpis: Delivery channel net margin above 15% after all costs (commission, packaging, labour)
Average commission rate across platforms reduced by at least 3 percentage points within 12 months
Direct ordering capturing at least 10% of total delivery volume within 6 months of launch
Platform ratings maintained at 4.5 or above across all delivery partners
constraints: Platform exclusivity clauses may restrict listing on competing platforms — review all contracts carefully
Delivery menu pricing must remain competitive with similar local cafes on the same platforms
Direct ordering requires a reliable delivery fulfilment method (self-delivery or courier partnership)

time_load.L1: 3 hours (platform audit, margin calculation, pricing adjustments)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (P&L build, direct ordering setup, marketing calendar, account manager meetings)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (strategy review, commission negotiation, channel performance analysis)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly delivery P&L review by owner. Quarterly platform performance and commission review. Bi-annual full delivery channel strategy review. Annual delivery channel alignment with business plan.

meta.summary_for_humans: This capstone block develops a strategic approach to delivery platforms — which to use, how to negotiate terms, what to charge, and when to invest in direct ordering. It transforms delivery from a margin-eroding afterthought into a managed, profitable revenue channel.
implementation_risks: Raising delivery prices too aggressively can reduce order volumes — test increases incrementally
Direct ordering requires delivery fulfilment infrastructure that the cafe may not have
Platform algorithms favour cafes that accept all orders quickly — throttling or pausing can reduce future visibility
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a capstone block — activate after B101 and B102 are operational. Flag as high-value if signals indicate delivery revenue exceeding 15% of total but with unknown or poor profitability.

blocks.name: Coffee Roaster Relationship
blocks.purpose: Establishes and manages the strategic relationship with the cafe's coffee roaster — the single most important supplier partnership in any specialty or independent cafe. This block covers roaster selection, contract negotiation, quality alignment, training support, and the ongoing partnership that determines the quality of every coffee served.

detailed_objectives: Define the cafe's coffee identity (origin preferences, roast profile, flavour priorities) as the foundation for roaster selection
Evaluate roaster partners on quality, consistency, support, price, and alignment with the cafe's values
Negotiate supply terms that balance cost control with quality access (pricing, delivery frequency, minimum orders)
Establish a quality feedback loop between the cafe and the roaster for continuous improvement
Secure roaster-provided training, equipment support, and marketing collaboration
secondary_objectives: Build a partnership that provides early access to seasonal and limited-edition coffees
Negotiate equipment loan or leasing arrangements where available (common with UK roasters)
Use the roaster relationship as a marketing asset — customers care about where their coffee comes from

blocks.why_this_matters: Coffee is the defining product of a cafe, and the roaster is the defining supplier. Unlike most food and beverage suppliers who provide commodity inputs, the coffee roaster shapes the cafe's identity, quality ceiling, and competitive positioning. Choosing the wrong roaster — or managing the relationship poorly — is like a restaurant hiring the wrong head chef. Every cup suffers. In the UK specialty coffee market, the roaster relationship typically extends far beyond simple bean supply. Good roasters provide barista training, dial-in support for new coffees, equipment maintenance advice, marketing materials, and sometimes equipment on loan or lease. These value-added services are worth thousands of pounds annually and should be factored into the total value of the relationship, not just the per-kilogram price. The typical UK cafe uses 15-30kg of coffee per week, spending £200-£500 weekly or £10,000-£26,000 annually on beans alone. At coffee cost percentages of 20-28%, this is the second-largest cost line after labour. Yet many cafe owners choose their roaster based on a single cupping session and never revisit the decision, even as their needs evolve, prices increase, or quality drifts. A strategic roaster relationship includes regular quality reviews, transparent pricing conversations, supply security planning, and mutual commitment to the cafe's success. The best roaster relationships feel like partnerships, not transactions.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: If the cafe already has a roaster, document the current relationship: who is the roaster, what coffees are supplied (house espresso blend, single origins, filter, decaf), current price per kilogram, delivery schedule, minimum order quantity, payment terms, and any additional services provided (training, equipment, marketing). Calculate the current coffee cost percentage: (total coffee bean spend / total coffee revenue) x 100. UK cafe benchmark is 20-28% — if the cafe is outside this range, investigate why. If the cafe does not have a roaster or is considering switching, create a Roaster Evaluation Criteria list covering: roast quality and consistency, price per kilogram, delivery reliability, minimum orders, training provision, equipment support, sustainability credentials, and cultural fit. Request samples from 3-5 roasters and conduct a blind tasting with the barista team. Week 2: For existing roasters, schedule an annual review meeting (even if the relationship is long-standing) to discuss: current pricing, upcoming coffee releases, quality feedback, training needs, equipment support, and any issues from either side. For new roaster selection, narrow the shortlist to 2 roasters and run a 2-week trial with each, tracking customer feedback, barista satisfaction, and waste rates (poorly roasted coffee generates more waste).
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Establish a formal roaster partnership framework. Create a Coffee Supply Agreement covering: pricing (fixed or variable, review frequency), delivery schedule and lead times, minimum order quantities, quality standards (roast date freshness, defect tolerance), training commitment (how many barista training sessions per year), equipment support terms, and termination notice period. Implement a monthly coffee quality check: cup every delivery, record scores, and share feedback with the roaster. Track coffee cost percentage weekly alongside sales data to identify drift. Build a relationship calendar: quarterly catch-ups with the roaster's account manager, an annual visit to the roastery (many UK roasters encourage this), and bi-annual menu development sessions where the roaster recommends new coffees aligned with seasonal trends. Negotiate a pricing structure that rewards volume: if the cafe commits to a minimum monthly order, the per-kilogram rate should reflect this. Discuss equipment loan or lease options — many UK specialty roasters offer grinder loans or espresso machine placement deals in exchange for exclusivity.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The roaster relationship should be a genuine strategic partnership that drives the cafe's coffee programme forward. Collaborate on exclusive blends or single-origin selections tailored to the cafe's customer base. Co-host customer events: cuppings, meet-the-roaster evenings, origin story talks. Explore joint marketing: the roaster promotes the cafe on their social media and website, and the cafe prominently credits the roaster in-store and online. Benchmark the roaster's pricing against market rates annually — transparency strengthens the relationship rather than undermining it. Plan for succession: what happens if the roaster has supply issues, discontinues a key coffee, or goes out of business? This feeds into B105 (Backup Supplier Contingency). Review the entire roaster relationship annually against the original evaluation criteria, ensuring it still delivers the quality, value, and partnership the cafe needs.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Document current roaster relationship: coffees, pricing, delivery, services
Calculate current coffee cost percentage and benchmark against 20-28% target
Create a Roaster Evaluation Criteria list if selecting or reviewing roasters
Request samples from 3-5 potential roasters and conduct blind tastings
Schedule an annual review meeting with the current roaster
Record roast dates on every delivery to monitor freshness
Identify any gaps in the current relationship (training, equipment, marketing support)

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a formal Coffee Supply Agreement covering all key terms
Implement monthly coffee quality checks with scored cupping and roaster feedback
Track coffee cost percentage weekly alongside sales data
Build a relationship calendar: quarterly catch-ups, annual roastery visit, menu development sessions
Negotiate volume-based pricing and explore equipment loan options
Establish a coffee menu development process with roaster input
Document all roaster communications and agreements in a supplier file

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Collaborate on exclusive blends or single-origin selections for the cafe
Co-host customer events: cuppings, roaster visits, origin story evenings
Explore joint marketing opportunities with the roaster
Benchmark roaster pricing annually against market rates
Review the full roaster relationship annually against evaluation criteria

tools.description: Scoring template for evaluating roaster partners across quality, consistency, price, delivery reliability, training support, equipment, sustainability, and cultural fit. Used during selection and annual review.
Formal agreement template covering pricing, delivery, quality standards, training commitment, equipment terms, exclusivity (if any), and termination provisions.
Monthly quality tracking log where each coffee delivery is cupped and scored, with notes on consistency, freshness (roast date), and any deviations from expected flavour profile.
Annual calendar of roaster touchpoints: quarterly account meetings, annual roastery visit, bi-annual menu development sessions, training sessions, and collaborative events.

failure_modes: Roaster is chosen on price alone without assessing quality, consistency, or support — leading to poor coffee and no partnership value
The roaster relationship is never reviewed, and pricing creeps upward or quality drifts downward without anyone noticing
Over-reliance on a single roaster without backup planning creates critical supply risk (see B105)
Roaster training and support opportunities are not utilised, leaving free value on the table
kpis: Coffee cost percentage maintained within 20-28% consistently
Monthly cupping scores stable or improving (no downward quality drift)
All coffee deliveries received within 7 days of roast date
At least 2 barista training sessions provided by the roaster per year
constraints: Exclusivity agreements with roasters must be carefully reviewed — they limit backup options
Coffee cost percentage targets must account for the cafe's positioning (specialty cafes may accept higher costs for premium quality)
Roaster relationships require personal investment — this is an owner-level responsibility, not delegable to junior staff

time_load.L1: 4 hours (audit, tasting, evaluation, initial meeting)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (agreement drafting, quality system setup, calendar planning)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (reviews, events, pricing benchmarking)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly coffee cost percentage check by manager. Monthly quality cupping and roaster feedback. Quarterly account review meeting with roaster. Annual comprehensive relationship review and market benchmarking.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block manages the cafe's most important supplier relationship — the coffee roaster. It covers selection, contract terms, quality monitoring, training access, and the strategic partnership that determines the quality of every coffee served. The roaster relationship is an owner-level responsibility with significant financial and quality implications.
implementation_risks: Switching roasters disrupts barista routines and customer expectations — plan transitions carefully with a 4-week overlap
Exclusivity deals may offer better pricing but create dangerous supply dependency
Small cafes may lack the volume to negotiate aggressively — focus on the total value of the relationship, not just price
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate as a priority for any new cafe onboarding. Flag as urgent if signals indicate coffee quality complaints, rising coffee cost percentage, or supply disruptions from the current roaster.

blocks.name: Backup Supplier Contingency
blocks.purpose: Ensures the cafe has identified, vetted, and pre-approved backup suppliers for every critical stock category so that a primary supplier failure — delivery miss, quality issue, business closure, or supply chain disruption — never results in the cafe being unable to trade.

detailed_objectives: Identify all critical stock categories where a supply failure would force the cafe to close or significantly reduce its offering
Source and vet at least one backup supplier for each critical category
Establish pre-approved accounts with backup suppliers so ordering can happen immediately in an emergency
Create a Supplier Contingency Plan document accessible to the manager on duty
Test backup suppliers periodically to ensure quality and reliability before they are needed in a crisis
secondary_objectives: Use backup supplier pricing as leverage in negotiations with primary suppliers
Build a broader supplier network that provides options for menu development and innovation
Reduce the cafe's vulnerability to regional supply chain disruptions (severe weather, transport strikes, supplier insolvency)

blocks.why_this_matters: Supply chain disruptions are not rare events — they are inevitable. In the UK hospitality sector, supplier failures occur regularly: delivery lorries break down, dairy suppliers short-deliver during heat waves, bakery suppliers close for staff holidays, coffee roasters have equipment failures that delay roasting. The COVID-19 pandemic, the 2021 lorry driver shortage, and ongoing post-Brexit supply complications have demonstrated that even 'reliable' supply chains can fail without warning. For a cafe, the consequences of a supply failure are immediate and visible. If the milk delivery does not arrive by 7am, the cafe cannot make lattes, flat whites, cappuccinos, or hot chocolates — roughly 70% of its drink revenue is gone for the day. If the bread supplier fails, the entire lunch menu collapses. If the coffee roaster misses a delivery, the cafe serves stale beans or shuts entirely. A Supplier Contingency Plan removes the panic from these situations. When every critical category has a pre-approved backup, the manager on duty does not need to call the owner, search Google, or negotiate with an unknown supplier under pressure. They open the contingency folder, find the backup supplier contact and account details, and place an emergency order. The best contingency plans are never used — but when they are needed, they prevent a crisis from becoming a catastrophe. The cost of maintaining backup supplier relationships is negligible compared to the cost of a single day of lost trading.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: List every supplier the cafe currently uses and categorise them by criticality: Critical (failure means the cafe cannot trade normally — coffee, milk, bread, packaging), Important (failure reduces the menu significantly — sandwich fillings, pastries, syrups), and Convenient (failure is inconvenient but manageable — speciality ingredients, seasonal items). For each Critical supplier, answer: if this supplier failed to deliver tomorrow, what would we do? If the answer is 'I don't know' or 'we'd have to close,' a backup is needed immediately. Identify at least one backup supplier for each Critical category. For coffee beans, this might be a local roaster who can supply at short notice, or emergency stock from a specialty wholesaler. For milk, identify the nearest alternative dairy delivery service or a cash-and-carry with early morning opening. For bread, find a local bakery or wholesale bakery supplier. Week 2: Open accounts with each backup supplier — most UK food suppliers can set up accounts within 24-48 hours. Record all backup supplier details (contact person, phone number, email, account number, delivery lead time, minimum order, pricing) in a single Supplier Contingency Document. Print this document and keep it in the manager's folder and behind the till.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Expand backup coverage to all Important category suppliers. For each backup, record not just contact details but also: quality assessment (have you tested their products?), pricing comparison with the primary supplier, delivery schedule and minimum orders, and any known limitations. Place a test order with each backup supplier so that the account is live, the quality is verified, and the team has experience with their ordering process. Create a Contingency Action Card for each critical category — a single laminated card that says: 'If [primary supplier] fails, do this: 1. Call [backup] on [number], 2. Order [these items], 3. Expected delivery [timeframe], 4. Cost difference [amount], 5. Inform [owner/manager].' Post these cards in a visible location. Review backup supplier details quarterly to ensure contacts, pricing, and availability are still current. Include backup supplier testing in the annual supplier review cycle.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Supplier contingency should be embedded in the cafe's risk management culture. Conduct an annual Supplier Risk Assessment that evaluates each primary supplier's stability, reliability track record, and any early warning signs of future problems (financial difficulties, ownership changes, delivery deterioration). Maintain active relationships with backup suppliers through occasional small orders — this keeps accounts active and ensures the backup knows the cafe's needs. Use backup supplier pricing as a negotiation tool with primary suppliers ('your competitor can supply at £X per kilo'). Develop regional contingency awareness: if the cafe is in an area vulnerable to severe weather (flooding, snow), have contingency plans that account for delivery route disruptions. Review the entire Supplier Contingency Plan annually and update it whenever suppliers change.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: List all current suppliers and categorise by criticality (Critical, Important, Convenient)
Identify at least one backup supplier for every Critical category
Open accounts with all backup suppliers within the first 2 weeks
Record all backup supplier details in a single Supplier Contingency Document
Print the contingency document and place behind the till and in the manager's folder
Identify nearest cash-and-carry as emergency backup for multiple categories
Brief the management team on contingency procedures

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Expand backup coverage to all Important category suppliers
Place test orders with each backup supplier to verify quality and process
Create laminated Contingency Action Cards for each critical category
Record quality assessments and pricing comparisons for all backups
Review backup supplier details quarterly for currency and accuracy
Include backup supplier testing in the annual supplier review cycle
Train shift leads on contingency procedures so they can act without the owner

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct annual Supplier Risk Assessments for all primary suppliers
Maintain active backup relationships through occasional small orders
Use backup pricing as a negotiation tool with primary suppliers
Develop regional contingency plans for weather and transport disruptions
Review the full Supplier Contingency Plan annually

deliverables: Supplier Contingency Document with all backup details

tools.description: Matrix categorising every supplier as Critical, Important, or Convenient based on the impact of their failure, with backup supplier details and current status for each.
Laminated quick-reference cards for each critical supply category showing: who to call, what to order, expected delivery time, cost impact, and who to inform. Designed for use under pressure.
Log recording test orders placed with backup suppliers: date, items ordered, quality assessment, delivery time, pricing, and any issues. Ensures backups are verified before they are needed.
Annual assessment evaluating each primary supplier's financial stability, delivery reliability trend, quality consistency, and any emerging risks that could trigger a switch to the backup.

failure_modes: Backup suppliers are identified on paper but accounts are never opened, making emergency ordering impossible under time pressure
Contingency document is outdated — contact details, pricing, or availability have changed since it was last reviewed
Backup supplier quality has never been tested, and the emergency switch reveals unacceptable quality differences
Only the owner knows the contingency plan — when they are unavailable, the team has no idea how to handle a supply failure
kpis: Backup suppliers identified and accounts opened for 100% of Critical category items
All backup suppliers test-ordered at least once per year
Contingency document reviewed and updated quarterly
Zero unplanned closures or menu reductions caused by primary supplier failures per year
constraints: Backup supplier pricing may be higher than primary — budget for a 10-20% cost premium during contingency use
Some suppliers require minimum orders that may exceed emergency needs — identify flexible small-order options
Exclusivity agreements with primary suppliers may contractually restrict backup relationships — review before signing

time_load.L1: 3 hours (supplier listing, backup identification, account opening)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (test ordering, action card creation, quality assessment)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/year (risk assessment, contingency plan review)

blocks.review_protocol: Quarterly review of backup supplier contact details and pricing by manager. Annual backup supplier test orders. Annual Supplier Risk Assessment by owner. Immediate review after any contingency activation.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block ensures the cafe has pre-approved backup suppliers for every critical stock category so that a primary supplier failure never forces the cafe to close or significantly reduce its offering. It covers identification, account setup, quality testing, and maintaining contingency readiness.
implementation_risks: Opening backup accounts takes time and effort that feels unnecessary until a crisis hits — do it now, not later
Staff may not know contingency procedures exist if they are not trained — brief the full team, not just the owner
Backup supplier quality may differ from the primary, potentially disappointing regular customers during contingency use
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B104 (Coffee Roaster Relationship) as a complementary block. Flag as urgent if signals indicate supply disruptions, supplier financial instability, or the cafe having zero backup options for critical categories.

blocks.name: Supplier Performance Assessment
blocks.purpose: Implements a structured scoring system for evaluating supplier performance across quality, reliability, pricing, communication, and service, ensuring supplier relationships are managed on evidence rather than habit or personal relationships.

detailed_objectives: Define measurable performance criteria for all supplier categories
Create a quarterly Supplier Scorecard that rates each supplier objectively
Use scorecard data to drive improvement conversations, renegotiations, or supplier changes
Track supplier performance trends over time to identify deterioration before it becomes critical
Establish a formal supplier review meeting cadence
secondary_objectives: Build a data-driven negotiation position for contract renewals and pricing discussions
Identify high-performing suppliers for deeper partnership and preferred status
Create accountability for suppliers who may take a small independent cafe for granted

blocks.why_this_matters: Most independent cafe owners choose suppliers based on a recommendation, a tasting, or a sales pitch, and then never formally assess their performance again. Over time, delivery reliability may decline, quality may drift, prices may creep upward, and the service relationship may erode — but without a measurement framework, these changes happen so gradually that they go unnoticed until they become serious problems. A supplier who delivered perfectly 95% of the time when the account was new may have slipped to 85% two years later, costing the cafe £200 per month in emergency purchases and menu disruptions. A roaster whose beans were consistently scored at 85 points may have quietly shifted to cheaper green coffee, dropping to 80 without the cafe realising. A dairy supplier who used to resolve issues within hours now takes days. These declines are invisible without structured assessment. The Supplier Performance Assessment creates visibility by scoring each supplier quarterly across standardised criteria. It transforms vague feelings ('I think the bread has been less good lately') into documented evidence ('bread quality scored 3.2 out of 5 this quarter, down from 4.1 last quarter, with 3 delivery shorts and 2 quality complaints'). This evidence powers productive supplier conversations, supports switching decisions, and ensures the cafe's supply chain is actively managed rather than passively accepted.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Define 5 performance criteria applicable to all suppliers: Quality (product meets agreed standards consistently), Reliability (deliveries arrive on time, in full, with correct items), Pricing (competitive, transparent, no unexpected increases), Communication (responsive, proactive about issues, easy to contact), and Service (flexibility, willingness to resolve problems, value-added support). Create a simple scoring system: rate each criterion from 1 (poor) to 5 (excellent). List every active supplier and assign them an initial score based on current experience — this first score is subjective and serves as a baseline. Week 2: Begin tracking supplier performance incidents in real time. Create a simple Supplier Incident Log — a notebook or shared document where any team member can record: date, supplier name, what happened (late delivery, short delivery, quality issue, wrong items), and impact on the cafe. This log becomes the evidence base for future quarterly scoring. Communicate to the team that recording supplier issues is valuable and expected — many cafes miss this data because staff do not think to report problems that feel routine.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a formal Quarterly Supplier Scorecard template. For each supplier, score the 5 criteria using a blend of incident log data and qualitative assessment. Calculate an overall score (average of all criteria) and categorise: 4.0-5.0 = Excellent (maintain and reward), 3.0-3.9 = Acceptable (monitor, identify improvements), 2.0-2.9 = Concerning (formal improvement discussion required), Below 2.0 = Unacceptable (switch to backup or terminate). Schedule quarterly supplier review meetings — these can be phone calls — where the scorecard is shared with the supplier and improvement targets are agreed for any criteria scoring below 3.5. Track trends over time: is a supplier improving, stable, or declining? Trending data is more valuable than any single score. Integrate the scorecard with backup supplier planning (B105) so that declining suppliers trigger backup readiness escalation. Share scorecard summaries with the team so they understand how their incident reports drive real action.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Supplier assessment should be a natural part of business management, not a special exercise. Integrate scorecard data into annual contract negotiations — a supplier with consistently high scores deserves loyalty; a supplier with declining scores faces transparent consequences. Publish an internal 'Supplier of the Quarter' recognition to reinforce the importance of the assessment process. Use scorecard data to build a supplier portfolio strategy: primary, secondary, and potential suppliers for each category, with clear criteria for promotion and demotion. Benchmark supplier pricing annually against market rates, using assessment data to justify negotiations. Share anonymised performance trends with key suppliers to demonstrate the cafe's professionalism and build mutual respect. Review the scoring criteria annually to ensure they remain relevant and balanced.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Define 5 performance criteria: Quality, Reliability, Pricing, Communication, Service
Create a 1-5 scoring system for each criterion
List all active suppliers and assign baseline scores
Create a Supplier Incident Log accessible to all staff
Brief the team on the importance of recording supplier issues
Place the incident log in an accessible location (behind the till or on a shared device)
Review all incidents weekly to identify patterns

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a formal Quarterly Supplier Scorecard template
Score all suppliers at the end of the first full quarter of data collection
Schedule quarterly supplier review calls to share scorecards and agree improvements
Track score trends over time to identify improving and declining suppliers
Integrate scorecard data with backup supplier planning (B105)
Share scorecard summaries with the team
Establish consequence thresholds: scores below 2.0 trigger switching process

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Integrate scorecard data into annual contract negotiations
Recognise high-performing suppliers with internal 'Supplier of the Quarter' status
Build a supplier portfolio strategy with primary, secondary, and potential tiers
Benchmark supplier pricing annually against market rates
Review scoring criteria annually for relevance and balance

tools.description: Simple log for recording supplier performance incidents as they occur: date, supplier, issue description, impact on the cafe, and resolution. Used as evidence for quarterly scoring.
Quarterly assessment template scoring each supplier across Quality, Reliability, Pricing, Communication, and Service on a 1-5 scale, with trend tracking and category classification.
Agenda template for quarterly supplier review meetings: scorecard presentation, improvement targets, pricing discussion, upcoming needs, and action items.
Annual strategic view of all suppliers by category, showing primary/secondary/potential tiers, performance scores, contract terms, and recommendation for the year ahead.

failure_modes: Incident log is not used consistently, leaving the quarterly scorecard without data and based on recollection bias
Scorecards are completed but never shared with suppliers, removing the accountability that drives improvement
Personal relationships with supplier representatives override objective performance data in decision-making
Assessment becomes a box-ticking exercise rather than a genuine management tool driving real outcomes
kpis: All suppliers scored quarterly with documented evidence from the incident log
No supplier remains below 3.0 overall score for more than 2 consecutive quarters without action
Supplier review meetings conducted quarterly for all Critical category suppliers
At least one pricing improvement or service enhancement achieved per quarter through evidence-based negotiation
constraints: Supplier assessment requires consistent data collection — the incident log must be maintained daily, not retrospectively
Small cafes with limited supplier options may have less leverage — focus on communication and improvement rather than threats
Scoring must be honest and fair — inflated scores undermine the entire system

time_load.L1: 1 hour (criteria definition, log setup, team briefing)
time_load.L2: 3 hours/quarter (scorecard completion, supplier meetings, trend analysis)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/year (portfolio review, criteria update, annual negotiations)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly incident log review by manager. Quarterly supplier scorecard completion and supplier meetings. Bi-annual supplier portfolio assessment. Annual scoring criteria review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a structured supplier scoring system so that every supplier is assessed quarterly on Quality, Reliability, Pricing, Communication, and Service. It replaces gut feeling with evidence-based management, powering better negotiation and preventing the slow decline in supplier performance that many cafes accept without realising.
implementation_risks: Staff may feel awkward recording supplier issues if they have personal relationships with delivery drivers — emphasise that it is about the company, not the individual
Sharing scorecards with suppliers requires confidence — frame it as a professional partnership practice, not confrontation
Very small cafes with no alternative suppliers may find low scores frustrating rather than actionable — focus on improvement conversations
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B104 and B105 are established. Flag as urgent if signals indicate declining supplier quality, increasing delivery failures, or unexplained cost increases without corresponding quality improvements.

blocks.name: Supply Partnership Excellence
blocks.purpose: Elevates key supplier relationships from transactional purchasing into genuine strategic partnerships that deliver mutual value — preferential pricing, product exclusivity, co-marketing, innovation collaboration, and long-term supply security. This capstone block transforms the supply chain from a cost centre into a competitive advantage.

detailed_objectives: Identify which suppliers are candidates for strategic partnership based on performance, alignment, and mutual benefit potential
Develop partnership frameworks that go beyond price to include training, marketing, product development, and exclusivity
Create formal partnership agreements that protect both parties and define expectations clearly
Build a supplier innovation pipeline where partners bring new products, ideas, and market insights to the cafe
Establish a supply chain governance structure for cafes operating or planning multiple sites
secondary_objectives: Position the cafe as a 'valued account' that suppliers prioritise during shortages and disruptions
Leverage supplier partnerships for customer-facing marketing and brand storytelling
Create a supply chain that supports sustainable and ethical sourcing objectives

blocks.why_this_matters: The difference between a supplier and a partner is the difference between a transaction and a relationship. A supplier delivers what you order. A partner calls to tell you they have a stunning new single-origin coffee before it is available to anyone else. A partner holds stock for you during shortages because they value the relationship. A partner invests in training your team because your success is their success. In the UK cafe market, the most consistently excellent operations share a common trait: they have deep, genuine partnerships with a small number of key suppliers. These partnerships take years to build but deliver compound returns that transactional purchasing never can. A strategic partner roaster provides barista training worth £2,000 per year, exclusive access to competition-grade coffees, marketing collaboration that drives footfall, and pricing stability through volatile green coffee markets. A strategic partner baker co-develops seasonal pastries that differentiate the cafe's offering, provides merchandising support, and offers sale-or-return terms that reduce waste risk. These benefits are invisible on a simple price-per-unit comparison but represent enormous competitive advantage. Supply Partnership Excellence is the capstone that brings together the roaster relationship (B104), backup contingency (B105), and performance assessment (B106) into a unified supply chain strategy. It is relevant primarily for established cafes with stable operations and clear growth ambitions, and it is essential for any cafe planning multi-site expansion.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Review all current supplier relationships using the data from B106 (Supplier Performance Assessment) and identify which suppliers have partnership potential. Look for: consistently high scorecard ratings (4.0+), alignment with the cafe's values (sustainability, quality, locality), willingness to invest in the relationship beyond basic supply, and strategic importance (coffee and milk being the obvious candidates). For the top 2-3 supplier relationships, document the current scope of the relationship: what do they provide beyond product? Training? Equipment? Marketing? Exclusivity? Flexibility? Week 2: For each potential partner, create a Partnership Value Map — a simple document showing what the cafe provides to the supplier (consistent volume, prompt payment, brand association, customer exposure) and what the supplier provides to the cafe (quality product, training, equipment, marketing, innovation). Identify gaps in mutual value: what could each party offer that they currently do not? This map becomes the basis for a partnership conversation.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Schedule a partnership development meeting with each potential strategic partner. The agenda should cover: reviewing the current relationship, sharing the cafe's growth plans and vision, exploring areas for deeper collaboration (joint marketing, exclusive products, training programmes, events), discussing pricing structures that reward commitment (volume discounts, annual contracts with price locks), and agreeing on a formal partnership framework. Draft Partnership Agreements for the top 1-2 suppliers that formalise: supply terms, pricing and review mechanisms, training and support commitments, exclusivity provisions (if any), marketing collaboration, dispute resolution, and termination provisions. These do not need to be legal contracts for small cafes — a clear written agreement signed by both parties is sufficient. Begin executing partnership activities: schedule the first joint event, arrange the first training session, launch the first co-branded marketing initiative.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Supply partnerships should be actively managed as strategic assets. Conduct bi-annual partnership reviews with each strategic partner: reviewing performance, celebrating achievements, planning the next period's collaboration, and addressing any challenges openly. Build a Supplier Innovation Pipeline: ask partners to share new products, industry trends, and ideas for the cafe's menu development — the best suppliers want to be involved in their customers' innovation. For multi-site cafes or those planning expansion, establish a supply chain governance framework: centralised ordering, standardised quality specifications, consistent partnership terms across all sites, and a named supply chain manager. Explore sustainability partnerships: joint initiatives for waste reduction, carbon footprint measurement, ethical sourcing certification (Rainforest Alliance, Fairtrade, B Corp), and community projects. Share partnership success stories with customers through social media, in-store displays, and menu descriptions — customers increasingly value transparency about where their food and coffee comes from. Review the entire supply partnership strategy annually, aligned with the cafe's business plan.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Review B106 scorecard data to identify suppliers with partnership potential (4.0+ scores)
Document the current scope of each key supplier relationship beyond basic supply
Create a Partnership Value Map for the top 2-3 supplier relationships
Identify gaps in mutual value that could strengthen each relationship
Prepare for partnership development conversations with key suppliers
Assess alignment of supplier values with the cafe's brand and sustainability goals
Gather growth plans and vision documents to share with potential partners

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Schedule partnership development meetings with top 2-3 supplier candidates
Draft Partnership Agreements covering supply, training, marketing, and exclusivity terms
Begin executing partnership activities: joint events, training, co-marketing
Establish a structured quarterly touchpoint with each strategic partner
Create a shared calendar of partnership activities and milestones
Track the financial value of partnership benefits (training, equipment, marketing) separately
Communicate partnership developments to the team for buy-in and engagement

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct bi-annual formal partnership reviews with strategic suppliers
Build a Supplier Innovation Pipeline for menu development input
Establish supply chain governance for multi-site or expansion scenarios
Explore joint sustainability initiatives with key partners
Review the full supply partnership strategy annually with business plan alignment

deliverables: Partnership Value Maps for key suppliers

tools.description: Visual template mapping the value each party brings to the relationship and identifying gaps where deeper collaboration could create mutual benefit.
Written agreement framework covering supply terms, pricing, training, marketing collaboration, exclusivity, dispute resolution, and termination. Signed by both parties.
Structured agenda for bi-annual partnership review meetings: performance review, achievement celebration, next-period planning, challenge resolution, and innovation pipeline discussion.
Log tracking new product ideas, industry trends, and innovation suggestions brought by supplier partners, with evaluation status and implementation decisions.

failure_modes: Partnership language is used but the relationship remains transactional — words without substance
Exclusivity commitments lock the cafe into a single supplier without adequate quality or pricing protections
Partnership benefits are not tracked financially, making it impossible to quantify the return on relationship investment
Over-reliance on personal chemistry rather than formal frameworks means partnerships collapse when individuals change roles
kpis: At least 2 strategic supplier partnerships formalised with written agreements within 12 months
Partnership benefits (training, equipment, marketing) valued and tracked at a minimum £2,000 per year combined
Bi-annual partnership reviews completed for all strategic suppliers
At least 2 supplier-sourced innovations (new products, processes, or ideas) evaluated per year
constraints: Strategic partnerships require owner-level engagement — they cannot be delegated to junior staff
Partnership agreements should be reviewed by a solicitor if they include exclusivity or significant financial commitments
Not all suppliers are suitable for strategic partnership — some are best managed as efficient transactional relationships

time_load.L1: 3 hours (value mapping, relationship assessment, meeting preparation)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (partnership meetings, agreement drafting, activity launch)
time_load.L3: 4 hours/year (bi-annual reviews, strategy update, innovation pipeline management)

blocks.review_protocol: Quarterly informal partnership check-in by owner. Bi-annual formal partnership review with each strategic supplier. Annual supply chain strategy review aligned with business plan.

meta.summary_for_humans: This capstone block transforms the best supplier relationships from transactional purchasing into genuine strategic partnerships delivering training, exclusivity, co-marketing, and innovation. It is built on the foundation of B104, B105, and B106, and is essential for cafes with growth ambitions.
implementation_risks: Partnership requires reciprocity — the cafe must offer value (prompt payment, volume commitment, brand exposure) not just demand concessions
Smaller cafes may lack the volume to attract strategic partner interest from larger suppliers — focus on local, smaller suppliers where the cafe represents meaningful business
Partnership agreements that include exclusivity need careful consideration to avoid over-dependence on a single source
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a capstone block — activate only after B104, B105, and B106 are showing stable performance. Flag as high-value for cafes showing strong growth trajectory or planning multi-site expansion.

blocks.name: Milk Supplier Assessment
blocks.purpose: Evaluates and manages dairy and alternative milk suppliers — the cafe's highest-volume daily consumable — to ensure consistent quality, reliable supply, competitive pricing, and appropriate range across whole milk, semi-skimmed, oat, soy, almond, and any other milks the cafe offers.

detailed_objectives: Assess current milk suppliers across quality (steaming performance, taste, freshness), reliability, pricing, and range
Ensure the cafe offers a milk range that meets customer demand — particularly the growing UK demand for oat and other plant-based alternatives
Negotiate pricing that reflects volume — milk is a high-volume, low-margin consumable where pennies per litre matter
Establish delivery schedules that guarantee fresh milk availability every trading day
Monitor and compare the steaming and latte art performance of different milk brands
secondary_objectives: Reduce milk waste through accurate ordering aligned with demand patterns
Explore barista-edition alternative milks designed for steaming and latte art quality
Build backup supply routes for milk as the most time-critical daily consumable

blocks.why_this_matters: Milk is the single most consumed ingredient in a UK cafe by volume. A typical independent cafe serving 200+ coffees per day will use 40-60 litres of milk daily — over 1,000 litres per month. At current UK prices, this represents a monthly spend of £700-£1,500 on dairy alone, with alternative milks costing significantly more per litre. The quality of the milk directly impacts every milk-based drink: a milk that steams poorly produces flat, bubbly microfoam that ruins latte art and mutes espresso flavour. A milk that tastes thin or overly sweet changes the flavour balance of the drink. UK customers are increasingly discerning about milk — they notice the difference between whole and semi-skimmed, between standard oat milk and barista-edition oat milk, and between fresh milk and milk that has been open too long. The alternative milk market in the UK has grown dramatically: oat milk now accounts for 30-40% of all milk used in specialty cafes, and many customers have strong brand preferences (Oatly, Minor Figures, Bonsoy). The cafe's milk choice is therefore both an operational decision (quality, cost, supply) and a brand decision (which alternatives to offer, at what price, with what prominence). Getting milk wrong — whether through poor quality, inconsistent supply, inadequate alternatives, or excessive cost — undermines the cafe's core product on a daily basis. Unlike coffee beans that are ordered fortnightly, milk must arrive reliably every single day, making supplier reliability absolutely critical.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit current milk supply arrangements. For dairy milk, record: supplier name, milk type (whole, semi-skimmed, skimmed), brand, price per litre, delivery schedule, minimum order, shelf life upon delivery, and steaming quality assessment (how well does it microfoam?). For alternative milks, record the same plus: which alternatives are offered (oat, soy, almond, coconut), which brand for each, whether barista-edition variants are used, and customer demand split (what percentage of drinks use each milk type). Calculate the total weekly milk cost and the milk cost as a proportion of coffee revenue. Run a steaming quality test: steam each milk type and assess microfoam quality, texture, latte art holdability, and taste. If any milk performs poorly, identify alternatives. Week 2: Research alternative dairy suppliers in the local area — most UK regions have multiple dairy delivery options. Compare pricing per litre (including delivery charges), delivery frequency, freshness guarantees, and minimum orders. For alternative milks, if not already using barista-edition variants, source samples and test them — the difference in steaming quality between standard and barista-edition oat milk is dramatic. Survey customers informally: are there alternative milks they wish the cafe offered? Coconut, almond, and hemp milk have growing followings.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Create a Milk Supplier Scorecard using the criteria from B106 but tailored to milk-specific requirements: freshness on delivery, steaming performance, taste consistency, delivery reliability (milk is the one supply where a missed delivery is most damaging), pricing competitiveness, and range availability. Negotiate pricing based on volume — a cafe buying 200+ litres of dairy per week has leverage, and switching costs for dairy suppliers are low. Establish a backup milk supply route (see B105) — the nearest supermarket or cash-and-carry that opens early enough to provide emergency milk before the cafe opens. This is a Critical contingency. Implement weekly milk waste tracking: how many litres of milk are being poured away due to over-ordering, expiry, or jug waste (milk steamed but not used)? Jug waste alone can account for 5-10% of total milk used in a cafe with poor pour discipline. Optimise alternative milk pricing: plant-based milks cost 3-5 times more than dairy per litre, and most UK cafes charge a 30-60p surcharge for alternatives. Ensure the surcharge covers the cost differential at minimum.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Milk management should be a quiet operational strength that customers notice through consistent drink quality. Conduct quarterly milk quality benchmarking: test 2-3 alternative suppliers alongside the current one to ensure the cafe is using the best-performing milk at the best price. Track customer alternative milk preferences monthly and adjust the range accordingly — the plant milk market is evolving rapidly. Explore direct relationships with local dairies for provenance marketing ('our milk comes from [local farm]'). Negotiate annual pricing agreements with dairy suppliers that provide price stability through volatile wholesale milk markets. Investigate bulk purchasing of alternative milks to reduce per-unit cost. Review the entire milk supply strategy bi-annually, considering market trends, customer preferences, and emerging alternatives.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit all current milk suppliers: dairy and alternatives, pricing, delivery, quality
Calculate total weekly milk cost and milk cost as proportion of coffee revenue
Conduct steaming quality tests for all milk types currently used
Research alternative dairy suppliers and compare pricing and delivery
Source and test barista-edition alternative milks if not already using them
Survey customers informally about alternative milk preferences
Identify the nearest emergency milk supply source (supermarket or cash-and-carry)

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a milk-specific Supplier Scorecard using B106 framework
Negotiate volume-based pricing with dairy supplier
Establish a backup milk supply route as a Critical contingency
Implement weekly milk waste tracking including jug waste
Review and optimise alternative milk surcharge pricing
Track alternative milk usage by type weekly to match ordering to demand
Train team on efficient milk steaming to reduce jug waste

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Conduct quarterly milk quality benchmarking against alternative suppliers
Track customer alternative milk preferences monthly and adjust range
Explore local dairy partnerships for provenance marketing
Negotiate annual pricing agreements for price stability
Review full milk supply strategy bi-annually

deliverables: Alternative Milk Range and Pricing review

tools.description: Audit template covering all milk types used: supplier, brand, price per litre, delivery schedule, shelf life, steaming quality score, and customer demand percentage.
Scoring sheet for evaluating milk steaming performance: microfoam quality (1-5), texture smoothness, latte art holdability, taste, and sweetness balance. Used for comparing brands.
Weekly log tracking milk waste by category: expired/unused, jug waste (steamed but not served), spillage, and total. Calculates waste as a percentage of total milk purchased.
Calculator comparing the cost per serve of each alternative milk against the surcharge applied, ensuring every alternative milk drink covers its additional cost and contributes to margin.

failure_modes: Milk delivery fails and there is no backup plan, leaving the cafe unable to make 70%+ of its drinks
Standard (non-barista) alternative milks are used, producing poor steaming quality that disappoints customers
Milk waste from over-ordering and jug waste goes untracked, silently eroding margin by 5-10%
Alternative milk surcharge does not cover the cost differential, meaning every alternative milk drink loses money
kpis: Milk cost as a percentage of coffee revenue maintained within 8-12%
Milk waste below 5% of total milk purchased per week
