Zero milk stockouts during trading hours per month
Milk steaming quality scores above 4.0 for all milk types used
constraints: Milk deliveries must arrive before trading hours begin — negotiate morning delivery slots
Alternative milk brand switches affect regular customers who have preferences — communicate changes in advance
UK dairy pricing is subject to wholesale market volatility — consider annual agreements for price stability

time_load.L1: 2 hours (audit, steaming tests, supplier research)
time_load.L2: 3 hours (scorecard setup, waste tracking, pricing optimisation)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/quarter (benchmarking, range review, supplier negotiation)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily milk freshness and stock check by opening team. Weekly milk waste review by manager. Monthly alternative milk demand tracking. Quarterly milk supplier scorecard and quality benchmarking.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block manages dairy and alternative milk suppliers — the cafe's highest-volume daily consumable. It covers supplier assessment, steaming quality testing, waste reduction, alternative milk range and pricing, and backup supply for the one ingredient where a missed delivery is most damaging.
implementation_risks: Switching dairy supplier disrupts established delivery routines — trial new suppliers alongside existing ones before switching
Barista-edition alternative milks cost significantly more — ensure surcharges cover the differential
Oat milk brand loyalty among customers (particularly Oatly) means switching can generate strong reactions — communicate changes proactively
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B106 is operational. Flag as urgent if signals indicate milk quality complaints, frequent stockouts, high waste rates, or milk cost percentage above 12% of coffee revenue.

blocks.name: Bakery & Food Supplier Management
blocks.purpose: Manages the relationships with bakery, pastry, and food ingredient suppliers to ensure the cafe's food offering is consistently high quality, reliably supplied, competitively priced, and aligned with the cafe's brand positioning — whether that is artisan sourdough or reliable daily basics.

detailed_objectives: Evaluate bakery and food suppliers on product quality, freshness, consistency, delivery reliability, and pricing
Define quality specifications for key food items so that supplier expectations are explicit, not assumed
Negotiate terms that support the cafe's food cost targets (25-35% food cost percentage)
Manage the balance between bought-in food (pastries, cakes, bread) and in-house preparation
Establish sale-or-return or credit arrangements where possible to reduce waste risk on perishables
secondary_objectives: Build a food supplier portfolio that enables seasonal menu changes and specials
Reduce food waste from over-ordering or poor quality by improving supplier coordination
Use food provenance as a marketing differentiator — local bakeries, artisan producers, farm-direct ingredients

blocks.why_this_matters: Food revenue in UK cafes has grown significantly as consumers increasingly expect more than just coffee. A well-run cafe now generates 30-50% of its revenue from food — sandwiches, toasties, salads, soups, pastries, cakes, and breakfast items. Yet many cafe owners who obsess over coffee sourcing treat their food supply chain as an afterthought, ordering from the first bakery that knocked on the door and re-ordering the same items weekly without reviewing quality, pricing, or demand alignment. The consequences are significant. A pastry supplier delivering croissants that have been frozen and reheated tastes fundamentally different from one delivering fresh-baked that morning — customers notice, even if they cannot articulate why. A bread supplier whose sourdough varies in quality from delivery to delivery creates inconsistency in the cafe's signature sandwich. A food ingredient supplier whose prices have crept up 15% over two years has eroded the cafe's GP% without the owner realising. Food supplier management is complicated by the perishable nature of the product: over-order and the excess is waste; under-order and the menu runs short. Unlike coffee beans with a 4-6 week shelf life, pastries and sandwiches are one-day products. This makes accurate ordering, reliable delivery, and waste management interdependent challenges that require active supplier management rather than passive reordering.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit all bakery and food suppliers. For each, document: what they supply, delivery frequency, minimum orders, pricing per item, payment terms, and quality consistency. Categorise food supply into three types: Bought-In Complete (items delivered ready to sell — croissants, muffins, whole cakes), Bought-In Ingredients (items used to make food in-house — bread, cheese, meats, vegetables), and In-House Made (items the cafe produces from scratch — sandwiches, soups, salads). For each bought-in item, assess: does the quality match the cafe's brand? Is the pricing delivering a food cost percentage of 25-35%? Is the delivery reliable? Are there freshness concerns? Identify the top 3 issues with current food supply — these might be quality inconsistency, pricing creep, limited range, or inflexibility around order quantities. Week 2: For any identified issues, research alternative suppliers. In most UK towns, there are multiple options for bakery supply (wholesale bakeries, artisan bakeries, frozen pastry suppliers like Bridor or Coup de Pates) and food ingredients (local wholesalers, Booker, Bidfood, specialist suppliers). Request samples and conduct a quality comparison. Calculate the food cost percentage for the cafe's top 10 food items and compare against the 25-35% target.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Food Supplier Scorecard using the B106 framework with food-specific criteria: product freshness on delivery, consistency batch-to-batch, visual presentation quality, shelf life remaining on delivery, and flexibility to adjust order quantities at short notice. Negotiate food supply terms: target sale-or-return on perishable items where possible (some bakery suppliers offer this), credit for damaged or sub-standard deliveries, and volume discounts for committed weekly orders. Create food quality specifications for key items — a written document defining what the cafe expects: 'Croissants: butter-based, minimum 22% butter content, delivered within 4 hours of baking, golden colour, flaky layers, no freezer burn.' Share specifications with suppliers so expectations are explicit. Implement a daily food quality check: when deliveries arrive, check items against specifications and record any deviations. Build a food supplier diversification plan: avoid relying on a single supplier for all food items. Different suppliers may excel in different areas — one bakery for pastries, another for bread, a local producer for cakes.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Food supplier management should drive menu innovation and competitive differentiation. Collaborate with bakery suppliers on seasonal specials — hot cross buns in spring, mince pies at Christmas, fruit tarts in summer. Explore direct relationships with local producers (farm shops, cheese makers, charcuterie producers) for provenance-led menu items that command premium pricing. Conduct quarterly food supplier reviews using scorecard data, and rotate the supplier portfolio to maintain quality and competitive pricing. Implement a monthly new product tasting session where suppliers present new items for potential inclusion on the menu. Track food GP% by supplier to identify which relationships deliver the best margin. Review the entire food supply strategy bi-annually, aligned with menu development plans and seasonal calendar.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit all bakery and food suppliers: items, pricing, delivery, quality
Categorise food supply into Bought-In Complete, Bought-In Ingredients, and In-House Made
Calculate food cost percentage for top 10 food items against 25-35% target
Identify top 3 issues with current food supply and research alternatives
Request samples from alternative suppliers and conduct quality comparison
Assess whether current bakery products match the cafe's brand positioning
Review delivery reliability and freshness for all food suppliers

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Food Supplier Scorecard with food-specific quality criteria
Negotiate sale-or-return, credit, and volume discount terms
Create written food quality specifications for all key bought-in items
Implement daily food quality checks on delivery against specifications
Build a supplier diversification plan across food categories
Train team to conduct and record delivery quality inspections
Track food GP% by supplier and by item monthly

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Collaborate with bakery suppliers on seasonal specials
Explore local producer relationships for provenance-led menu items
Conduct quarterly food supplier reviews with scorecard data
Implement monthly new product tasting sessions with suppliers
Review full food supply strategy bi-annually with menu planning

deliverables: Food Cost Percentage analysis by item and supplier

tools.description: Audit template for evaluating all bakery and food suppliers: items supplied, pricing, delivery schedule, quality assessment, food cost percentage, and improvement recommendations.
Written quality specifications for key bought-in food items defining expected standards: appearance, freshness, ingredient requirements, packaging, and delivery conditions.
Daily checklist for inspecting food deliveries against quality specifications: freshness, temperature, appearance, quantity accuracy, and packaging integrity. Non-conformances are logged.
Strategic plan showing primary and alternative suppliers for each food category, with quality scores, pricing, and recommendations for portfolio optimisation.

failure_modes: Food suppliers are never quality-checked, and gradual quality decline goes unnoticed until customers stop ordering food
Over-reliance on a single bakery supplier creates critical risk — if they close for holidays or fail, the cafe has no food offering
Food cost percentage drifts above 35% because pricing is accepted without negotiation or monitoring
Food quality specifications are never communicated to suppliers, so expectations are misaligned and disappointment is inevitable
kpis: Food cost percentage maintained within 25-35% across all food items
Daily delivery quality check pass rate above 95%
Food supplier scorecard scores above 3.5 for all active suppliers
Zero days where food menu is significantly reduced due to supplier failure per quarter
constraints: Perishable food deliveries must arrive before trading hours with adequate remaining shelf life
Food quality specifications must be realistic and achievable for the cafe's price point — artisan quality at commodity prices is not sustainable
Seasonal menu changes require at least 4 weeks lead time for supplier coordination

time_load.L1: 3 hours (audit, quality assessment, supplier research, sample testing)
time_load.L2: 5 hours (scorecard setup, specifications drafting, negotiation, quality check training)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (reviews, tastings, seasonal planning)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily delivery quality check by receiving team. Weekly food cost percentage review by manager. Quarterly food supplier scorecard and review meeting. Bi-annual food supply strategy review with menu planning.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block manages bakery, pastry, and food ingredient suppliers to ensure the cafe's food offering is high quality, reliably supplied, and financially sustainable. It covers supplier evaluation, quality specifications, delivery inspection, food cost management, and seasonal collaboration.
implementation_risks: Bakery suppliers with early morning deliveries may have limited flexibility on delivery timing or minimum orders
Switching food suppliers changes the taste profile of menu items — transition carefully and communicate changes to regulars
Sale-or-return terms are valuable but not universally available — negotiate early and be prepared to accept partial terms
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B106 is operational. Flag as urgent if signals indicate food quality complaints, food cost percentage above 35%, bakery supplier reliability issues, or limited food menu availability.

blocks.name: Food Supply Chain Resilience
blocks.purpose: Builds a resilient, ethical, and sustainable food supply chain that can withstand disruptions, supports the cafe's values around sourcing and sustainability, and positions the business for long-term supply security in an increasingly volatile global food market.

detailed_objectives: Map the full food supply chain from farm to counter, identifying vulnerabilities and single points of failure
Build resilience through supplier diversification, local sourcing, and strategic buffer stock for non-perishable items
Integrate ethical and sustainable sourcing principles into supplier selection and purchasing decisions
Develop contingency protocols for supply chain disruptions (severe weather, transport strikes, supplier insolvency, global events)
Create a supply chain governance framework that balances cost, quality, resilience, and ethics
secondary_objectives: Use sustainable and local sourcing as a marketing differentiator and customer engagement tool
Reduce the cafe's environmental footprint through supply chain decisions (shorter transport distances, less packaging, less waste)
Build a supply chain that supports future growth without creating fragility

blocks.why_this_matters: The UK food supply chain has been tested repeatedly in recent years — Brexit-related import complications, COVID-19 disruptions, the 2021 lorry driver shortage, energy price spikes affecting food producers, and ongoing inflationary pressure on ingredients. For independent cafes, these macro disruptions manifest as real operational problems: the dairy supplier short-delivers because their warehouse is understaffed, the bakery raises prices by 20% to cover energy costs, the coffee roaster cannot source a particular origin because of shipping delays, and the packaging supplier switches to lower-quality alternatives without notice. Building supply chain resilience is not about predicting the next crisis — it is about creating a supply network that can absorb shocks without passing them directly to the customer as stockouts, price increases, or quality reductions. Resilient supply chains have three characteristics: diversification (multiple suppliers for critical categories), locality (shorter supply chains that are less vulnerable to transport disruption), and transparency (understanding where ingredients come from and what risks exist at each stage). Alongside resilience, customers increasingly expect ethical sourcing. Fair trade coffee, free-range eggs, responsibly sourced dairy, and sustainably grown produce are becoming baseline expectations in the UK cafe market, not premium differentiators. A cafe that cannot articulate its sourcing values is increasingly at a competitive disadvantage. This capstone block brings together the work from B104-B109 into a unified, resilient, and ethically grounded supply chain strategy.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Create a Supply Chain Map — a simple visual document showing every supplier, what they provide, where they are located, and the delivery route to the cafe. For each supplier, note: single source or diversified? Local or national? Direct or via distributor? This map immediately reveals concentration risks — if three critical categories all come from the same distributor, a single disruption point threatens the entire supply. Week 2: Assess the cafe's current ethical sourcing position. For each major ingredient category, answer: Is the coffee certified (Fairtrade, Rainforest Alliance, Direct Trade)? Are eggs free-range? Is dairy from responsible farms? Are there any ingredients the cafe should be sourcing more responsibly but currently is not? Create a simple Ethical Sourcing Statement — one paragraph that honestly describes the cafe's current sourcing values and commitments. This statement should be genuine, not aspirational. Post it on the website and consider displaying it in-store. Identify the three most vulnerable points in the supply chain (single-source suppliers with no backup, long-distance imports, suppliers with known reliability issues) and create immediate mitigation actions (linking to B105).
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Supply Chain Risk Register — a document listing each supply risk, its likelihood, its impact if realised, and the mitigation in place. Review quarterly. Increase local sourcing where quality and cost allow: a local bakery, a regional dairy, a nearby farm for salad ingredients. Local suppliers typically have shorter lead times, lower transport costs, and stronger relationships — all of which improve resilience. Calculate the 'local sourcing percentage' — what proportion of the cafe's supply spend goes to suppliers within 50 miles? This metric is increasingly important to customers and can be a marketing asset. Develop disruption response protocols: for each critical supply category, define what happens if supply is disrupted for 1 day, 3 days, and 1 week. Include menu adjustments, emergency sourcing, and customer communication. Build strategic buffer stock for non-perishable items that are critical and have long lead times: coffee beans (keep 2 weeks extra stock), packaging (keep 1 month buffer), ambient ingredients (maintain par levels with safety margin). These buffers absorb short-term disruptions without requiring emergency action.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Supply chain resilience and ethics should be embedded in the cafe's identity and operations. Achieve at least one supply chain certification or commitment: Fairtrade, Rainforest Alliance, or a documented Direct Trade relationship with the roaster. Publish an annual Sourcing Report (even a single page) detailing where key ingredients come from, the cafe's sustainability initiatives, and progress against ethical sourcing goals. Join or create a local cafe purchasing cooperative to increase collective buying power and share supply chain resilience. Attend supplier open days and farm visits to deepen understanding of the supply chain and build content for customer communication. Review the full supply chain strategy annually, stress-testing it against potential disruption scenarios and adjusting for market changes. Position supply chain excellence as a core competitive advantage and brand differentiator.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Create a Supply Chain Map showing all suppliers, locations, and delivery routes
Identify concentration risks: multiple categories from a single source or distributor
Assess current ethical sourcing position across all major ingredient categories
Draft an honest Ethical Sourcing Statement for the website and in-store display
Identify the three most vulnerable points in the supply chain
Create immediate mitigation actions for critical vulnerabilities (linking to B105)
Begin recording supplier locations and distances for local sourcing measurement

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Supply Chain Risk Register with quarterly review cycle
Increase local sourcing where quality and cost allow
Calculate and track the local sourcing percentage
Develop disruption response protocols for 1-day, 3-day, and 1-week scenarios
Build strategic buffer stock for non-perishable critical items
Create a supply chain resilience budget for buffer stock and diversification
Integrate supply chain resilience into the backup supplier contingency (B105)

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Achieve at least one supply chain certification or documented ethical commitment
Publish an annual Sourcing Report for customers and stakeholders
Explore or create a local cafe purchasing cooperative
Attend supplier open days and farm visits for relationship and content building
Review the full supply chain strategy annually with stress-test scenarios

tools.description: Visual document mapping all suppliers, their locations, what they provide, delivery routes, and identified concentration risks. Used to reveal vulnerabilities at a glance.
Register listing each supply chain risk with likelihood rating, impact assessment, current mitigation measures, and owner. Reviewed quarterly.
Laminated protocol cards for each critical supply category defining actions for 1-day, 3-day, and 1-week disruption scenarios: emergency sourcing, menu adjustments, and customer communication.
Template for the cafe's annual sourcing report: key supplier profiles, origin information, ethical certifications, sustainability initiatives, and progress against sourcing goals.

failure_modes: Supply chain mapping is done once and never updated, becoming outdated as suppliers change
Ethical sourcing claims are made without verification, creating reputational risk if challenged
Buffer stock for non-perishables is built up but not rotated, leading to quality degradation or expiry
Resilience planning focuses on dramatic scenarios (pandemics, wars) while ignoring common everyday risks (lorry breakdowns, staff illness at supplier)
kpis: No critical supply categories dependent on a single source without backup
Local sourcing percentage tracked and targeted at minimum 30% of spend within 50 miles
Supply chain disruptions resolved within 24 hours without customer-facing impact 90% of the time

constraints: Local sourcing is not always possible or financially viable for all categories — be realistic about trade-offs
Ethical sourcing certifications have associated costs (licence fees, audit costs) that may not be justified for very small cafes

time_load.L1: 3 hours (supply chain mapping, ethical assessment, vulnerability identification)
time_load.L2: 5 hours (risk register, response protocols, buffer stock planning, local sourcing research)
time_load.L3: 4 hours/year (annual review, sourcing report, certification, stress testing)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly buffer stock check by manager. Quarterly supply chain risk register review. Bi-annual supply chain resilience assessment. Annual full strategy review with sourcing report publication.

meta.summary_for_humans: This capstone block builds a resilient, ethical, and sustainable food supply chain through mapping, risk assessment, local sourcing, buffer stocking, and disruption protocols. It brings together the work from B104-B109 into a unified strategy that can withstand supply shocks while supporting the cafe's values.
implementation_risks: Supply chain mapping can feel overwhelming — start simple with a single-page visual and build detail over time
Ethical sourcing commitments must be genuine — customers and media quickly identify greenwashing
Resilience measures (buffer stock, multiple suppliers) increase costs — quantify the insurance value against disruption costs
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a capstone block — activate after B104, B108, and B109 are operational. Flag as high-value if signals indicate supply chain vulnerabilities, ethical sourcing gaps, or recent disruption incidents.

blocks.name: Menu Item Costing Calculator
blocks.purpose: Creates a detailed cost breakdown for every item on the menu — coffee, food, pastries, drinks — so the cafe knows exactly what each product costs to make, what margin it generates, and whether its price is set correctly. This block is the financial foundation upon which all pricing, profitability, and cost control decisions are built.

detailed_objectives: Calculate the exact ingredient cost of every menu item, including all components (coffee dose, milk volume, syrup, packaging for takeaway)
Determine the gross profit percentage for each item at current selling prices
Identify items that are under-priced (GP% too low) and items that are over-priced relative to their cost
Create a standardised costing template that can be updated when ingredient prices change
Enable informed pricing decisions by providing accurate cost data (feeding directly into B118)
secondary_objectives: Expose hidden costs that are often ignored in menu costing: packaging, sauces, garnishes, condiments
Support menu engineering by identifying high-margin and low-margin items
Provide the cost basis for delivery menu pricing where commission must be absorbed

blocks.why_this_matters: Most independent cafe owners set prices by looking at what nearby cafes charge and adding or subtracting a few pence. This approach ignores the cafe's own cost structure, which is unique — different rent, different wages, different suppliers, different waste levels. The result is that many cafes have menu items that lose money on every sale without the owner realising. A large oat milk latte priced at £4.20 might seem profitable, but when you cost the double espresso shot (18g at £20/kg = £0.36), the oat milk (250ml of barista oat at £2.40/litre = £0.60), the takeaway cup, lid, and sleeve (£0.25), and the labour time (say £0.30 for the 2 minutes it takes to make and serve), the total cost is £1.51. The gross profit is £2.69 or 64%. That is healthy. But a loaded avocado toast priced at £7.50 with sourdough bread (£0.45), two avocados in season (£1.60), poached eggs (£0.40), toppings and seasoning (£0.30), a garnish (£0.15), and packaging if delivered (£0.35) costs £3.25 — a GP of 57%, which drops to just 22% after a 35% delivery commission. Without item-level costing, the cafe cannot distinguish between its most profitable and least profitable items. The Menu Item Costing Calculator provides this visibility. It is not glamorous work, but it is the single most impactful financial exercise a cafe owner can undertake. Cafes that complete this exercise for the first time almost always discover at least 3-5 items that need immediate price adjustment.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Create a master ingredients price list. Go through every ingredient the cafe uses and record the purchase price per unit (per kg, per litre, per item). Include: coffee beans (price per kg), every milk type (price per litre), syrups (price per pump or ml), bread (price per slice or loaf), all sandwich fillings (price per kg or per item), pastries (price per unit if bought in), eggs (price per egg), vegetables and salad (price per kg), sauces and condiments (price per serving), and takeaway packaging (cup, lid, bag, box — price per unit). This list becomes the reference document for all costing. Week 2: Cost the menu's top 20 items by volume (the items sold most frequently). For each item, list every ingredient with the exact quantity used and calculate the cost per serve. Do not forget the small items that add up: a sprinkle of cocoa powder, a sachet of sugar, a napkin, a stirrer. These microcosts typically add £0.05-£0.15 per item but are almost always excluded from amateur costings. Calculate the GP% for each item: (Selling Price - Ingredient Cost) / Selling Price x 100. Flag any item with a GP% below 60% for coffee-based drinks or below 55% for food items — these need investigation.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Build a comprehensive Menu Costing Spreadsheet covering every item on the menu (not just the top 20). Include columns for: item name, category (hot drinks, cold drinks, food, pastries), each ingredient with quantity and unit cost, total ingredient cost, selling price, GP%, GP in £, and a flag column for items below target GP%. Create a separate section for delivery items showing the same data plus packaging cost and platform commission deducted. Generate a Menu Profitability Ranking — a sorted list from highest GP% to lowest — this immediately reveals which items are the cafe's profit drivers and which are margin drains. Update the costing spreadsheet whenever supplier prices change — create a simple process where any price change notification triggers a costing update within one week. Use the costing data to run 'what if' scenarios: what happens to the GP% if milk prices increase 10%? If the cafe switches to a cheaper bread supplier? If portion sizes are reduced by 10%? These scenarios inform proactive decision-making.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Menu costing should be a living system, not a one-off exercise. Establish a monthly costing review where the manager checks the top 10 items for any cost drift (ingredient price changes, portion creep, new components added without costing). Integrate costing data with sales data to calculate total GP contribution by item: an item with a lower GP% but very high sales volume may contribute more total profit than a high-GP% item that rarely sells. Use costing data to inform menu design: position high-margin items prominently, create combo offers that blend high and low margin items, and consider discontinuing items where the GP% is persistently unacceptable. Share simplified costing data with the team so they understand which items make money and can make informed recommendations to customers. Review the entire menu costing annually or whenever the menu changes significantly.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Create a master ingredients price list recording the cost per unit of every ingredient
Cost the top 20 menu items by volume with all ingredients and quantities
Include micro-costs: packaging, condiments, garnishes, napkins
Calculate GP% for each item and flag any below 60% (drinks) or 55% (food)
Identify items that need immediate price adjustment based on GP% analysis
Create a simple reference card showing ingredient costs for daily use
Store all costing data in a single accessible document or spreadsheet

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build a comprehensive Menu Costing Spreadsheet covering every menu item
Create a delivery-specific costing section including packaging and commission
Generate a Menu Profitability Ranking from highest to lowest GP%
Establish a process to update costings within 1 week of any supplier price change
Run 'what if' scenarios for key cost variables (milk, coffee, bread prices)
Integrate costing with B112 (Food Cost % Tracking) and B113 (Coffee Cost % Tracking)
Create a monthly costing review process for the top 10 items

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Establish monthly costing reviews checking for cost drift and portion creep
Integrate costing data with sales data for total GP contribution analysis
Use costing data to inform menu design and item positioning
Share simplified costing insights with the team for informed customer recommendations
Review entire menu costing annually or with any significant menu change

deliverables: Delivery Menu Costing with commission impact

tools.description: Comprehensive list of every ingredient used in the cafe with purchase price per unit (kg, litre, item), supplier, and last updated date. The reference document for all menu costing.
Per-item costing template listing every ingredient with quantity per serve, unit cost, extended cost, total cost, selling price, and GP% calculation. Includes micro-costs and packaging.
Sorted ranking of all menu items from highest to lowest GP%, with total GP in pounds, sales volume, and total profit contribution. Identifies the cafe's financial winners and drains.
Calculator showing the margin impact of delivery platform commission on each menu item: ingredient cost, packaging cost, commission at 25-35%, and resulting net margin per item.

failure_modes: Costing exercise is done once and never updated, becoming increasingly inaccurate as ingredient prices change
Micro-costs (packaging, garnishes, condiments) are excluded, understating true costs by 5-15%
Portion sizes drift upward over time (portion creep) without costing being updated, silently eroding margin
Staff do not know the cost impact of their portioning decisions, wasting expensive ingredients through inconsistency
kpis: 100% of menu items costed with documented ingredient breakdowns
All coffee-based drinks achieving GP% above 65%
All food items achieving GP% above 55%
Costing spreadsheet updated within 1 week of any supplier price change
constraints: Ingredient prices change frequently — the costing spreadsheet must be treated as a living document, not a one-off exercise
Delivery costing must include platform commission (25-35%) which dramatically changes the margin picture
Portion consistency is essential for costing accuracy — if portions are not standardised, costs are theoretical rather than actual

time_load.L1: 6 hours (ingredients list, top 20 item costing, GP% analysis)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (full menu costing, delivery costing, profitability ranking, scenario modelling)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (costing reviews, updates, team communication)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly check of any supplier price changes requiring costing updates. Monthly review of top 10 items for cost drift. Quarterly full menu profitability ranking review. Annual comprehensive recosting of entire menu.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a detailed cost breakdown for every menu item so the cafe knows exactly what each product costs to make, what margin it generates, and whether its price is correct. It is the financial foundation for all pricing, profitability, and cost control decisions.
implementation_risks: The initial costing exercise is time-intensive — block out a full day with the manager to complete it properly
Staff may feel uncomfortable if costing data reveals that their favourite menu items are unprofitable
Costing accuracy depends on portion consistency — invest in measuring and portioning standards before expecting precise cost data
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a foundational block that feeds directly into B112, B113, B114, and B118. Activate early in any cafe's financial improvement journey. Flag as urgent if signals indicate unknown food costs, pricing set by competitor matching, or GP% significantly below benchmarks.

blocks.name: Food Cost % Tracking
blocks.purpose: Implements weekly food cost percentage monitoring — the most important financial health indicator for the food side of a cafe's operation — tracking actual food costs against revenue to ensure the cafe maintains its target GP% on all food items and identifies cost drift before it erodes profitability.

detailed_objectives: Calculate actual food cost percentage weekly: (Food Purchases + Opening Stock - Closing Stock) / Food Revenue x 100
Establish a target food cost percentage range (25-35%) appropriate to the cafe's positioning
Identify the drivers of food cost variance: supplier price increases, portion creep, waste, theft, or sales mix shift
Create a weekly food cost review rhythm that catches problems within 7 days rather than at month-end
Link food cost tracking to menu item costing (B111) so that theoretical and actual costs can be compared
secondary_objectives: Build financial literacy among the team by sharing food cost data and explaining its significance
Enable rapid pricing or portioning adjustments when food costs move outside target range
Support monthly P&L accuracy by providing reliable weekly food cost data

blocks.why_this_matters: Food cost percentage is the pulse of a cafe's food profitability. If the cafe's food revenue is £2,000 per week and food purchases total £600, the food cost percentage is 30%. If that drifts to 38% — an increase that can happen gradually through supplier price increases, portion creep, and waste — the cafe loses £160 per week in GP, or £8,320 per year. That £8,320 is often the difference between a profitable cafe and a struggling one. The insidious nature of food cost drift is that it happens slowly: a supplier increases prices by 3% without notice, the team starts putting slightly more cheese on sandwiches, a new menu item is introduced without proper costing, and waste increases because par levels have not been adjusted. Each change is small, but they compound. Without weekly monitoring, the owner discovers the problem only when the quarterly accounts arrive — by which time thousands of pounds have been lost. Weekly food cost tracking creates an early warning system. A single week with food costs at 36% instead of the target 30% triggers an immediate investigation: what changed? Supplier prices? Menu mix? Waste? Volume? The weekly cadence means problems are caught within days, not months. For UK cafes, the benchmark food cost percentage is 25-35%, depending on the type of food served: simple sandwiches and pastries sit at the lower end, while complex cooked dishes with expensive ingredients sit at the higher end. The cafe's target should be set based on its specific menu and positioning.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Set up a simple food cost tracking system. This requires three data points each week: total food purchases (all invoices and cash-and-carry receipts for food items — exclude coffee, drinks, packaging, and cleaning supplies), food revenue (total food sales from the till, separate from drink sales — most EPOS systems can split these), and ideally a stock adjustment (opening stock value minus closing stock value, though this can be simplified initially). For the first 2 weeks, simply collect the data: file every food invoice, record food revenue daily from the till, and calculate: Food Cost % = Total Food Purchases / Food Revenue x 100. If the cafe's till does not separate food and drink sales, estimate the split using the menu mix (e.g., if 35% of transactions include a food item with an average food spend of £5, food revenue = number of food transactions x £5). Week 2: Calculate the food cost percentage for Week 1 and compare it against the target range (25-35%). If it is above 35%, the cafe is over-spending on food relative to what it earns. If it is below 25%, either the pricing is very strong or the food is being under-portioned. Record the figure and begin building a weekly trend.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Refine the food cost calculation to include stock adjustment: Food Cost % = (Opening Stock + Purchases - Closing Stock) / Food Revenue x 100. This is more accurate because it accounts for stock changes rather than just purchase timing. Conduct a brief stock count of food items at the same time each week (Sunday evening or Monday morning works well for most cafes). This does not need to be a full inventory count — value the key items (bread, fillings, dairy, pastries) and estimate the rest. Create a Weekly Food Cost Report that the manager completes every Monday: food revenue for the previous week, food purchases, stock adjustment, calculated food cost %, comparison to target, and variance commentary ('food cost was 33% this week vs 30% target due to a price increase from the bakery supplier and higher waste on Thursday'). Track the 4-week rolling average to smooth out weekly fluctuations and identify true trends. Compare actual food cost percentage against the theoretical food cost from B111 (Menu Item Costing) — the gap between theoretical and actual reveals waste, portioning inconsistency, or unrecorded costs.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Food cost percentage should be a number the entire team understands and cares about. Display the weekly food cost percentage (or a simplified version: 'This week: 29% — on target') in the staff area. Set a monthly team target for food cost percentage and celebrate when it is achieved. Implement a variance investigation protocol: any week where food cost exceeds target by more than 3 percentage points triggers a formal investigation covering supplier pricing, waste levels, portioning, and sales mix. Link food cost tracking to the waste log (B097) so that waste data and cost data tell a coherent story. Use food cost trend data in annual pricing reviews (B118) and menu development. Review and refine the food cost target annually based on menu changes, supplier agreements, and competitive positioning.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Set up a filing system for all food purchase invoices and receipts
Identify how to extract food revenue from the till system (separate from drink sales)
Calculate food cost percentage for the first full week of data
Compare result against the 25-35% benchmark and note the gap
Begin recording weekly food cost data in a simple tracker
If till system cannot split food/drink, estimate the split from menu mix data
Brief the manager on the weekly food cost calculation and reporting responsibility

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Implement weekly stock counts for key food items to refine cost calculation
Create a Weekly Food Cost Report template with variance commentary
Track the 4-week rolling average to identify true trends
Compare actual vs theoretical food cost from B111 to identify the gap
Investigate and document the drivers of any significant food cost variance
Link food cost data to waste tracking (B097) for coherent analysis
Present food cost trends in monthly management meetings

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Display weekly food cost percentage in the staff area
Set monthly team targets for food cost percentage
Implement a formal variance investigation protocol for overages above 3 points
Use food cost trend data in annual pricing reviews
Review and refine the food cost target annually

tools.description: Weekly report template: food revenue, food purchases, stock adjustment, calculated food cost %, target comparison, variance commentary, and actions required.
Spreadsheet tracking weekly food cost percentage over time with 4-week rolling average, target line, and visual chart. Highlights weeks outside the target range for investigation.
Comparison template showing theoretical food cost (from B111 menu item costing) against actual food cost, calculating the variance percentage and identifying likely causes.
Formal investigation template used when food cost exceeds target by more than 3 points: supplier pricing check, waste analysis, portioning audit, sales mix review, and corrective actions.

failure_modes: Food cost percentage is calculated monthly or quarterly rather than weekly, catching problems too late
Food purchases are not accurately separated from other costs (coffee, drinks, cleaning), producing unreliable data
Stock adjustments are not included, making food cost data volatile and misleading due to purchase timing
Food cost data is collected but never reviewed or acted upon, making the tracking system a waste of time
kpis: Food cost percentage maintained within the 25-35% target range on a 4-week rolling average
Weekly food cost report completed every Monday without exception
Variance investigations completed within 48 hours for any week exceeding target by 3+ points
Actual vs theoretical food cost gap below 5 percentage points
constraints: Accurate food cost tracking requires till systems that can separate food and drink revenue
Weekly stock counts add time but are essential for data accuracy — simplify to key items if full counts are impractical
Purchase invoice filing must be disciplined — missing invoices make food cost data unreliable

time_load.L1: 1 hour/week (invoice collection, calculation, recording)
time_load.L2: 2 hours/week (stock count, report completion, variance analysis)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (trend review, investigation, target refinement)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly food cost report by manager. Monthly trend review in management meeting. Quarterly target assessment. Annual food cost target review aligned with pricing strategy.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block implements weekly food cost percentage tracking — calculating actual food costs against food revenue to maintain the 25-35% target. It catches cost drift within days rather than months, preventing the slow margin erosion that damages so many independent cafes.
implementation_risks: Separating food and drink purchases on invoices from mixed suppliers (e.g., cash-and-carry runs) requires discipline
If the till cannot split food and drink revenue, food cost calculations will be approximate until the system is configured
Weekly stock counts can feel burdensome — keep them focused on the top 20 highest-value food items
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B111 (Menu Item Costing Calculator) is complete. Flag as urgent if signals indicate unknown food costs, GP% significantly below expectations, or food cost percentage above 35% persistently.

blocks.name: Coffee Cost % Tracking
blocks.purpose: Implements weekly coffee cost percentage monitoring — tracking the cost of all coffee-related inputs (beans, milk, syrups, alternative milks, chocolate, tea) against total drink revenue to maintain the cafe's target GP% and catch cost drift early.

detailed_objectives: Calculate actual coffee/drink cost percentage weekly: (Coffee & Drink Purchases) / Drink Revenue x 100
Establish a target coffee cost percentage range (20-28%) appropriate to the cafe's pricing and milk mix
Track individual cost components (beans, dairy milk, alternative milks, syrups) to identify which is driving any cost increase
Compare actual coffee costs against theoretical costs from B111 to quantify waste, portioning errors, and unrecorded usage
Create a weekly coffee cost review rhythm integrated with the food cost tracking (B112)
secondary_objectives: Monitor the impact of alternative milk usage on coffee cost percentage — oat milk costs 3-5x more than dairy
Track the cost impact of coffee dose and grind changes on margin
Build awareness among baristas of the cost of each drink they make

blocks.why_this_matters: Coffee drinks are the highest-margin items on a cafe's menu — a well-managed espresso-based drink should achieve a GP% of 70-80%. But this high margin is vulnerable to silent erosion from three directions. First, coffee bean costs: a price increase of £1 per kilogram on a cafe using 20kg per week adds £1,040 to annual costs. Second, milk costs: as the proportion of drinks using alternative milks grows (now 30-40% in many UK specialty cafes), the blended milk cost per drink rises significantly. A standard dairy flat white uses milk costing approximately £0.10, while an oat milk flat white uses milk costing £0.60 — a £0.50 difference that most cafes only partially recoup through a 30-60p surcharge. Third, waste and portioning: baristas who run espresso machines slightly longer, steam too much milk, or pour excessive syrup are eroding margin with every drink. A barista who uses a 20g dose instead of the recipe's 18g dose wastes 11% of the cafe's coffee — approximately £30 per week. Weekly coffee cost tracking catches all three erosion factors by comparing what the cafe spends on drink inputs against what it earns from drink sales. The benchmark for UK cafes is a coffee cost percentage of 20-28%, with specialty cafes using premium beans and high alternative milk usage sitting at the upper end. Any cafe consistently above 28% is either under-pricing, over-portioning, or wasting excessively.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Separate all drink-related purchases from food purchases. Drink costs include: coffee beans (all types — espresso, filter, decaf), all milk (dairy and alternatives), syrups, chocolate powder, tea, chai, matcha, and any other drink ingredients. This separation is essential — blending food and drink costs produces a single average that hides problems in both categories. Record total drink revenue from the till for the same period. Calculate: Coffee Cost % = Total Drink Purchases / Total Drink Revenue x 100. The UK benchmark target is 20-28%. Week 2: If the figure is above 28%, break down the cost into components: coffee beans, dairy milk, alternative milks, syrups and other ingredients. Calculate each as a percentage of drink revenue. This decomposition immediately reveals where the problem lies. In most cases, rising coffee cost percentage is driven by alternative milk usage or coffee dose creep rather than bean prices. Begin recording the weekly figure alongside the food cost data to build a trend.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Create a Weekly Coffee Cost Report mirroring the food cost report (B112): drink revenue, drink purchases by component (beans, dairy, alternatives, other), total drink cost, coffee cost %, target comparison, and variance commentary. Track the alternative milk ratio: what percentage of total drinks use alternative milks? This ratio, combined with the surcharge applied, determines whether alternative milk usage is eroding or contributing to margin. Most UK cafes need an alternative milk surcharge of at least 40p to break even on the cost differential, and 60p to maintain the same GP% as dairy. Monitor coffee dose accuracy: weigh 10 espresso doses weekly and compare against the recipe. If the average dose has drifted upward by even 1g, that is a 5-6% increase in coffee bean cost that will show up in the tracking. Implement a monthly reconciliation: total coffee beans purchased versus theoretical usage (doses pulled x recipe weight). Any significant gap indicates waste, spills, or unrecorded consumption (staff drinks, training, etc.).
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Coffee cost awareness should be part of barista culture, not just management reporting. Share the weekly coffee cost percentage with the team and explain the impact of their actions: 'Every gram over the recipe dose costs the cafe £X per week.' Implement barista dose discipline checks during service. Track the GP contribution of each drink category: espresso-based drinks, filter coffee, cold drinks, hot chocolate, tea. This analysis reveals where the drink menu makes money and where it does not. Use coffee cost data in roaster negotiations (B104): accurate consumption data strengthens the cafe's position when discussing pricing. Link coffee cost tracking to the overall financial dashboard and monthly P&L. Review and adjust the coffee cost target annually based on bean price trends, milk pricing, and menu changes.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Separate all drink-related purchase invoices from food purchases
Calculate total drink revenue from the till for the first full week
Calculate coffee cost percentage for Week 1 and compare against 20-28% benchmark
Break down drink costs by component: beans, dairy, alternatives, syrups, other
Identify which component is the largest cost driver
Begin recording weekly coffee cost data alongside food cost data
Check current alternative milk surcharge covers the cost differential

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a Weekly Coffee Cost Report template with component breakdown
Track the alternative milk usage ratio and its margin impact weekly
Monitor coffee dose accuracy through weekly 10-dose weigh checks
Implement monthly bean reconciliation: purchased vs theoretical usage
Investigate any gap between actual and theoretical coffee consumption
Compare coffee cost trends with roaster pricing to isolate internal vs external factors
Present coffee cost data alongside food cost data in monthly management meetings

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Share weekly coffee cost percentage with the barista team
Implement barista dose discipline checks during service
Track GP contribution by drink category for menu optimisation
Use consumption data in roaster pricing negotiations
Review coffee cost target annually based on market trends

tools.description: Weekly report: drink revenue, coffee bean cost, dairy milk cost, alternative milk cost, other drink ingredient costs, total coffee cost %, target comparison, and variance commentary.
Weekly tracker showing alternative milk usage as a percentage of total drinks, cost per alt milk serve, surcharge applied, and net margin impact versus dairy equivalent.
Weekly log of 10 randomly weighed espresso doses compared to the recipe weight, calculating average dose, variance, and estimated cost impact of any drift.
Monthly comparison of coffee beans purchased against theoretical consumption (total shots pulled x recipe dose weight), identifying unexplained gaps due to waste, staff drinks, or training.

failure_modes: Drink and food costs are tracked as a single blended figure, hiding problems in both categories
Alternative milk cost impact is ignored because the surcharge creates an illusion of coverage
Coffee dose drift is never measured, allowing baristas to unknowingly waste 10-15% of bean stock
Staff drinks, training, and waste are not accounted for, creating a persistent gap between theoretical and actual costs
kpis: Coffee cost percentage maintained within 20-28% on a 4-week rolling average
Alternative milk surcharge covering at least 100% of the cost differential over dairy
Average espresso dose within 0.5g of recipe weight across weekly checks
Bean reconciliation gap below 10% between purchased and theoretical consumption
constraints: Accurate tracking requires till systems that separate drink and food revenue
Alternative milk cost tracking requires recording which milk type is used per drink — EPOS modifiers are essential
Bean reconciliation requires shot counting capability from the espresso machine or EPOS

time_load.L1: 1 hour/week (invoice separation, calculation, recording)
time_load.L2: 2 hours/week (component breakdown, dose checks, reconciliation)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (trend review, team communication, target adjustment)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly coffee cost report by manager. Monthly bean reconciliation and dose accuracy review. Quarterly coffee cost trend analysis. Annual target review aligned with roaster pricing and menu changes.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block implements weekly coffee cost percentage tracking — monitoring beans, milk, alternatives, and syrups against drink revenue to maintain the 20-28% target. It catches the silent margin erosion from dose creep, alternative milk costs, and waste before it compounds into significant financial loss.
implementation_risks: If the till system cannot distinguish drink from food revenue, this tracking requires manual estimation which reduces accuracy
Baristas may perceive dose checks as a lack of trust — frame it as quality control, not surveillance
Alternative milk brands have different per-litre costs that change frequently — update pricing regularly
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B111 (Menu Item Costing Calculator) is complete. Flag as urgent if signals indicate coffee cost percentage above 28%, declining drink GP%, or high alternative milk usage without adequate surcharging.

blocks.name: Packaging Cost Optimisation
blocks.purpose: Analyses and optimises the cost of all packaging — cups, lids, sleeves, bags, boxes, napkins, cutlery, and delivery containers — ensuring the cafe minimises this significant cost line without compromising quality, customer experience, or sustainability commitments.

detailed_objectives: Audit every packaging item the cafe uses, recording unit cost, volume used per week, and annual spend
Identify the largest packaging cost drivers and evaluate alternatives that reduce cost without reducing quality
Calculate packaging cost per transaction for dine-in, takeaway, and delivery channels separately
Negotiate volume-based pricing with packaging suppliers for the highest-volume items
Balance cost optimisation with sustainability goals — cheaper packaging is not always better packaging
secondary_objectives: Reduce packaging waste through right-sizing (using the correct size cup, not defaulting to the largest)
Explore reusable cup schemes and customer-owned cup incentives to reduce takeaway packaging volume
Ensure compliance with UK packaging waste regulations where applicable

blocks.why_this_matters: Packaging is the invisible cost that bleeds cafes silently. A typical UK cafe spends £200-£500 per month on packaging alone — cups, lids, sleeves, paper bags, napkins, food boxes, delivery containers, and cutlery. This spend is rarely tracked, rarely optimised, and rarely questioned. Yet packaging cost directly reduces GP% on every takeaway and delivery transaction. A 12oz takeaway flat white in a double-wall cup (£0.12) with a sip-through lid (£0.04) and a napkin (£0.02) costs £0.18 in packaging per drink. If 60% of the cafe's 200 daily drinks are takeaway, that is 120 cups at £0.18 each — £21.60 per day, £648 per month, £7,776 per year. And that is just cups. Delivery packaging is even more expensive: insulated food boxes, leak-proof containers, branded bags, and cutlery packs can add £0.80-£1.50 per delivery order. For a cafe doing 30 delivery orders per day, that is £24-£45 daily in packaging alone. The optimisation opportunity is significant. Switching from double-wall cups to single-wall with a recycled kraft sleeve saves £0.04 per cup — £1,750 per year on a cafe doing 120 takeaways daily. Negotiating a 10% bulk discount on the most-used cup size saves another £500-£800 per year. Offering a 20p discount for customers bringing their own cup (increasingly common in UK cafes) reduces both cost and waste. These are not transformational changes — they are small, evidence-based decisions that compound into meaningful savings.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Conduct a complete packaging audit. List every packaging item the cafe uses: takeaway hot cups (by size — 8oz, 12oz, 16oz), cold cups, lids, sleeves, paper bags (small and large), food boxes/deli containers, sandwich wraps, napkins, stirrers, wooden cutlery, delivery bags, and any branded items. For each, record: supplier, unit price, weekly usage, and annual cost (unit price x weekly usage x 52). Calculate the total annual packaging spend and packaging cost as a percentage of total revenue. Most cafes will find packaging accounts for 2-4% of revenue. Week 2: Identify the top 5 packaging items by annual cost — these are where optimisation effort will deliver the most return. For each, research at least one alternative: a different supplier, a different material, a different size, or a different brand. Request samples and quotes. Check current cup sizes against actual usage: if 70% of drinks sold are 12oz but the cafe also stocks 8oz and 16oz cups, consider whether all three sizes are necessary — reducing to two sizes simplifies stock management and may unlock volume discounts on the remaining sizes.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Calculate the packaging cost per transaction for each service channel: Dine-In (plates, cutlery — essentially zero additional packaging cost for most items), Takeaway (cup, lid, sleeve, bag, napkin), and Delivery (all takeaway packaging plus food containers, delivery bag, labels). This channel-specific costing reveals the true cost of each channel and feeds into delivery pricing decisions (B103). Negotiate volume-based pricing with the top 2-3 packaging suppliers. UK packaging suppliers like Biopak, Vegware, and Dart commonly offer tiered pricing for committed volumes. If the cafe uses 5,000 cups per month, negotiate pricing at that volume rather than buying in small batches. Implement a reusable cup incentive: offer a 20-30p discount for customers bringing their own cup. Track uptake — even a 10% adoption rate reduces cup purchases significantly. Review cup sizing policy: ensure staff ask customers what size they want rather than defaulting to the largest size, and price size upgrades to cover the packaging and ingredient cost increment.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Packaging cost and sustainability should be continuously improved. Track packaging cost monthly as a line item on the P&L, giving it the same visibility as food and coffee costs. Evaluate sustainable packaging alternatives annually — compostable cups, plant-based plastics, recycled materials — balancing environmental benefits against cost premiums. Explore partnerships with reusable cup schemes (e.g., Clubzeroco, CupClub) if available in the cafe's area. Calculate and communicate the cafe's packaging waste footprint: how many cups per year? How many end up in landfill versus recycling? This data supports sustainability marketing and customer engagement. Negotiate annual packaging supply agreements that lock in pricing and provide delivery reliability. Review the entire packaging strategy annually, considering new products, pricing changes, regulatory developments (such as potential UK single-use plastic levies), and sustainability targets.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Conduct a complete packaging audit: every item, unit cost, weekly usage, annual spend
Calculate total annual packaging spend and packaging cost as % of revenue
Identify the top 5 packaging items by annual cost
Research at least one alternative for each high-cost item (different supplier, material, or size)
Request samples and quotes from alternative packaging suppliers
Review cup size range — can it be simplified without affecting customer choice?
Calculate packaging cost per transaction for takeaway and delivery channels

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Calculate packaging cost per transaction by channel (dine-in, takeaway, delivery)
Negotiate volume-based pricing with top 2-3 packaging suppliers
Implement a reusable cup incentive (20-30p discount) and track uptake
Ensure staff ask for cup size rather than defaulting to largest
Add packaging cost as a tracked line item in weekly cost reporting
Include packaging in par-level management and mid-service stock checks
Build packaging costs into delivery menu pricing calculations (B103)

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track packaging cost monthly on the P&L with the same rigour as food and coffee costs
Evaluate sustainable packaging alternatives annually
Explore reusable cup scheme partnerships
Calculate and communicate the cafe's packaging waste footprint
Negotiate annual packaging supply agreements for price stability

deliverables: Packaging Audit Report with annual cost analysis
Packaging Cost Per Transaction by channel

tools.description: Comprehensive audit spreadsheet listing every packaging item with supplier, unit cost, weekly volume, annual spend, and percentage of total packaging budget.
Calculator showing the total packaging cost per transaction for each service channel (dine-in, takeaway, delivery), itemising every component.
Side-by-side comparison of packaging suppliers for the top 5 highest-cost items: unit price at various volumes, quality assessment, delivery terms, and sustainability credentials.
Monthly tracking of packaging spend as a line item, with trends, percentage of revenue, reusable cup uptake, and comparison to budget and prior year.

failure_modes: Packaging costs are never tracked separately, remaining invisible within general purchases
Cheapest-possible packaging is selected without considering impact on customer experience and brand perception
Reusable cup discounts are offered but never promoted or tracked, delivering no measurable impact
Sustainability claims about packaging are made without verification, creating greenwashing risk
kpis: Packaging cost maintained below 3% of total revenue
Top 5 packaging items re-quoted and price-compared at least annually
Reusable cup uptake rate tracked and targeted at minimum 10% of takeaway transactions
Packaging spend reduced by 10% within 12 months of optimisation implementation
constraints: Packaging quality cannot be reduced to a point where it damages customer experience or brand perception
Sustainable packaging typically costs 10-30% more — the premium must be justified by customer expectations or regulatory requirements
Cup size reduction affects customer perception of value — any changes must be tested carefully

time_load.L1: 3 hours (audit, costing, alternative research)
time_load.L2: 4 hours (supplier negotiation, reusable cup setup, channel costing)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/quarter (tracking review, supplier comparison, sustainability assessment)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly packaging cost review as P&L line item. Quarterly supplier pricing comparison. Annual full packaging strategy review including sustainability assessment.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block audits and optimises all packaging costs — cups, lids, bags, boxes, delivery containers — which silently consume 2-4% of revenue. It covers supplier negotiation, channel-specific costing, reusable cup schemes, and sustainability, turning an invisible cost into a managed, optimised spend.
implementation_risks: Staff may default to the largest cup size if not trained to ask — this wastes both packaging and ingredients
Bulk buying packaging to get better prices requires storage space that small cafes may lack
Switching packaging brands changes the customer's tactile experience — test before committing to large orders
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate after B111 (Menu Item Costing Calculator) is complete. Flag as urgent if signals indicate packaging costs above 4% of revenue, delivery packaging complaints, or no visibility of packaging spend.

blocks.name: Inventory Count System
blocks.purpose: Establishes a regular, structured inventory counting process that gives the cafe accurate visibility of what stock it actually holds, enabling precise cost calculations, waste identification, theft detection, and ordering accuracy.

detailed_objectives: Define which items are counted and at what frequency (weekly for high-value, monthly for low-value)
Create a standardised count sheet that makes the process efficient, consistent, and auditable
Train all relevant staff on counting procedures to ensure accuracy and consistency
Use count data to calculate actual cost of goods sold (feeding into B112 and B113)
Identify stock variances (differences between expected and actual stock) that reveal waste, theft, or recording errors
secondary_objectives: Improve ordering accuracy by knowing exactly what is on hand before placing orders
Support financial reporting with accurate stock valuation for month-end and year-end accounts
Create an audit trail that demonstrates stock management discipline to potential investors or buyers

blocks.why_this_matters: You cannot manage what you do not measure, and stock is the most commonly unmeasured asset in independent cafes. Most cafe owners have a rough idea of what they have in stock — 'a couple of boxes of cups, about 10 litres of milk, some bags of beans' — but this approximation leads to over-ordering, under-ordering, undetected waste, and a complete inability to calculate accurate cost of goods sold. Without a regular count, the cafe's food cost and coffee cost percentages (B112 and B113) are based on purchases rather than consumption, which can be misleading when purchase timing differs from usage timing. A cafe that buys £800 of stock in one week and £200 the next has not halved its costs — it has simply shifted the purchasing pattern while consumption remains steady. Stock counts correct for this by measuring actual usage: (Opening Stock + Purchases - Closing Stock) = Cost of Goods Consumed. Stock counts also serve as a theft detection mechanism. Pilferage in hospitality is more common than owners want to admit — even small amounts add up. If the count reveals that 2kg of coffee beans have disappeared without corresponding sales, that is a £30 loss per occurrence. Multiplied across all stock categories and weeks, undetected shrinkage can represent 2-5% of total stock cost. Regular counting creates visibility that deters casual theft and identifies problems early. For UK cafes seeking external investment, franchise agreements, or business valuation, accurate stock records are a basic expectation of financial credibility.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Identify all stock items that need counting and categorise them by value and volatility: Category A (high-value, fast-moving — coffee beans, milk, bread, pastries, sandwich fillings), Category B (moderate-value, steady — syrups, chocolate, tea, packaging), Category C (low-value, slow-moving — cleaning supplies, napkins, condiments). Create a simple Count Sheet organised by storage location (behind the bar, walk-in fridge, dry store, under-counter storage) with columns for: item name, unit of measure (kg, litres, units), expected stock level (from par levels, if established), actual count, and variance. Conduct the first full count at close of business on Sunday evening or Monday morning before any deliveries arrive. Count everything — this first count establishes the baseline. It will take 45-60 minutes for a small cafe. Week 2: Conduct a second count at the same time and compare against the first, adjusting for purchases and sales during the intervening week. Calculate usage: Opening Stock + Purchases - Closing Stock = Usage. Compare this usage figure against what the till says was sold — any significant gap is worth investigating.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Establish a tiered counting frequency: Category A items counted weekly (every Sunday evening), Category B items counted fortnightly, Category C items counted monthly. This reduces the counting burden while maintaining visibility of the most important items. Create a digital count sheet (Google Sheet or simple inventory app) that calculates variances automatically when counts are entered. Train at least 2 team members to conduct counts so the process is not dependent on a single person. Implement a blind count procedure: the counter should not see the expected stock level before counting — this prevents them from 'counting to the number' rather than counting accurately. After counting, the manager reviews variances and investigates any item where actual stock differs from expected by more than 5%. Use count data to feed accurate stock values into the weekly food cost (B112) and coffee cost (B113) calculations. File all count sheets for a minimum of 6 months — they provide essential evidence for cost analysis and any discrepancy investigation.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Stock counting should be an ingrained routine, not a special event. Consider transitioning to a perpetual inventory system where high-value items are counted daily as part of the opening or closing routine (e.g., counting milk and coffee beans daily takes 2 minutes and provides real-time visibility). Implement cycle counting: instead of counting everything weekly, count a different section each day so that all items are counted at least monthly with high-value items counted more frequently. Use stock variance data to set improvement targets: reduce total stock variance below 2% of stock value per month. Benchmark stock accuracy against industry standards and share results with the team. Integrate stock count data with the ordering system so that counts automatically trigger reorders when stock falls below par levels (B095). Review the counting system annually to ensure it remains efficient and aligned with the cafe's operational scale.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Categorise all stock items into Category A (high-value/fast), B (moderate), and C (low-value/slow)
Create a Count Sheet organised by physical storage location
Conduct the first full stock count at close of business on Sunday or Monday morning
Record all counts with item, unit of measure, and actual quantity
Calculate baseline stock value across all categories
Conduct a second count the following week and calculate usage
Compare usage against till sales to identify any significant gaps

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Establish tiered counting frequency: A weekly, B fortnightly, C monthly
Create a digital count sheet with automatic variance calculations
Train at least 2 team members on accurate counting procedures
Implement blind count procedure (counter does not see expected levels)
Investigate any item variance exceeding 5% of expected stock
Feed count data into weekly food cost and coffee cost calculations
File all count sheets for minimum 6 months

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Implement daily counts for highest-value items (milk, beans) as part of routine
Introduce cycle counting to spread the counting load across the week
Set stock variance targets below 2% of stock value per month
Integrate count data with ordering system for automatic reorder triggers
Review the counting system annually for efficiency and alignment with operations

deliverables: Stock Count Sheet template (by storage location)
Stock Item Category Matrix (A, B, C)

tools.description: Count sheet organised by physical storage location with columns for item name, unit of measure, actual count, expected level, and variance. Used weekly for Category A items.
Step-by-step guide for conducting accurate stock counts: blind counting protocol, unit of measure standards, partial-unit estimation, recording procedures, and filing requirements.
Investigation template for stock variances exceeding 5%: item details, expected vs actual, possible causes (waste, portioning, theft, recording error), and corrective actions.
Monthly summary of stock accuracy by category: total variance value, variance as percentage of stock, trend over time, and items requiring attention.

failure_modes: Counts are performed inaccurately because staff are rushing or estimating rather than physically counting
Counting is skipped during busy periods and never caught up, creating gaps in the data
Variances are identified but never investigated, removing the accountability that stock counting creates
Count data is not used to improve ordering or cost calculations, making the effort feel pointless to staff
kpis: Category A stock counted weekly without exception
Stock variance below 2% of total stock value per month
All variances exceeding 5% investigated and documented within 48 hours
Stock count data feeding into weekly food and coffee cost calculations
constraints: Stock counts must be conducted at a consistent time (ideally before any deliveries) for comparability
At least 2 staff members must be trained to count, preventing single-person dependency
Count sheets must be filed for a minimum of 6 months for audit and investigation purposes

time_load.L1: 2 hours (category setup, count sheet creation, first count, staff training)
time_load.L2: 45 minutes/week (Category A count) + 30 minutes/fortnight (Category B) + 30 minutes/month (Category C)
time_load.L3: 30 minutes/month (accuracy review, variance analysis, system refinement)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly Category A count by shift lead. Fortnightly Category B count by manager. Monthly full stock accuracy review. Quarterly counting procedure review and staff refresher.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block establishes a regular stock counting system so the cafe knows exactly what it holds, enabling accurate cost calculations, waste identification, and theft detection. It uses a tiered approach: high-value items weekly, moderate items fortnightly, low-value items monthly.
implementation_risks: Stock counting feels like a chore — keep it efficient with well-organised count sheets and clear procedures
Inaccurate counts are worse than no counts — they provide false confidence. Invest in training and blind counting
If count data is collected but never reviewed or acted upon, staff will quickly stop taking it seriously
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate alongside B116 (FIFO & Stock Rotation) as a pair. Flag as urgent if signals indicate unknown stock levels, persistent discrepancies between purchases and usage, or suspected shrinkage.

blocks.name: FIFO & Stock Rotation
blocks.purpose: Implements First-In-First-Out (FIFO) stock rotation across all storage areas, ensuring the oldest stock is always used first to minimise waste from expiry, maintain freshness, and comply with food safety regulations.

detailed_objectives: Train all staff on FIFO principles: new stock goes to the back, old stock comes to the front
Implement a date labelling system for all perishable items received and opened
Organise all storage areas (fridges, freezers, dry store) to physically support FIFO rotation
Establish a daily check for items approaching use-by or best-before dates
Reduce food waste from expired stock to near zero through disciplined rotation
secondary_objectives: Improve food quality by ensuring customers always receive the freshest available product
Comply with food safety regulations requiring proper stock rotation and date control
Support accurate stock counting (B115) by maintaining organised, clearly labelled storage

blocks.why_this_matters: FIFO is one of the simplest concepts in stock management and one of the most frequently violated. The principle is straightforward: the first items to arrive should be the first items used. New deliveries go behind or beneath existing stock so that older items are naturally pulled forward. When FIFO is not followed, the consequences are predictable and expensive: milk delivered on Monday gets pushed to the back of the fridge when Wednesday's delivery arrives, and by Thursday the Monday milk is approaching its use-by date — if not past it. Sandwich fillings opened on Tuesday are buried behind fresh containers and discovered a week later, unusable. Pastries delivered first thing this morning sit behind yesterday's leftover pastries, and the stale ones get sold first (or both get stale). In a UK cafe, food waste from poor rotation typically represents 3-8% of food purchases. For a cafe spending £1,500 per week on food, that is £45-£120 per week, or £2,340-£6,240 per year, thrown in the bin because nobody rotated the stock properly. Beyond waste, poor rotation is a food safety issue. The Food Standards Agency's SFBB guidance explicitly requires proper stock rotation, and an Environmental Health Officer finding expired items in the fridge during an inspection will mark the cafe down on its Food Hygiene Rating. The fix is not complicated — it requires training, labelling, storage organisation, and daily checking — but it requires consistency. FIFO only works when every team member follows it every time.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Implement a universal date labelling system. Purchase a roll of food-safe day-dot labels or use a label maker. From Day 1, every perishable item that enters the cafe must be labelled with either the delivery date (for items with printed use-by dates) or the date opened/prepared (for items decanted from bulk packaging). Establish a simple colour-coded day-dot system: Monday = blue, Tuesday = orange, Wednesday = green, etc. This makes it visually instant to see how old something is. Brief all staff on the FIFO principle with a practical demonstration: take them to the fridge, show them how to move old stock forward and place new deliveries behind. Make it a clear rule: 'If you put new stock in front of old stock, you are creating waste.' Week 2: Reorganise all storage areas to support FIFO physically. In the fridge, items with the nearest use-by dates should be at the front and top. In the dry store, older boxes should be in front. Use shelf-edge labels showing what goes where so that anyone restocking places items in the correct position. Conduct a 'first-thing' daily check: each morning, the opening team member does a 2-minute walk of all storage areas, pulling forward any items approaching their use-by date and flagging anything that has expired for disposal.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks).
