Share draft budget with business partners or key staff for review

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build three budget scenarios: pessimistic, expected, and optimistic
Establish a monthly budget-vs-actual review meeting with documented variance analysis
Create a rolling forecast updating remaining months based on actual trading
Set up a KPI dashboard tracking daily revenue, labour percentage, food cost, and net margin
Translate annual budget into daily and weekly till targets for the team
Conduct a formal mid-year budget review and adjustment in Month 6
Link capital expenditure decisions to the budget approval process

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce weekly flash financial reports for management review
Share simplified monthly financial updates with the whole team
Benchmark performance against published industry data
Develop a 3-year financial plan covering equipment, leases, and growth strategy
Integrate cloud accounting software with POS for real-time financial visibility
Review and refine the budget process annually to improve forecast accuracy

deliverables: Annual Budget Spreadsheet (12-month revenue and cost plan)
Cash Flow Forecast Template with seasonal adjustments
Budget Review Meeting Agenda and Minutes Template

tools.description: A 12-month spreadsheet template with pre-built rows for all cafe revenue streams and cost categories, including formulae for gross margin, net profit, and running cash balance. Includes a tab for assumptions and known cost changes.
Monthly one-page report comparing budgeted figures against actuals across all categories, highlighting variances exceeding 5% and documenting root causes and corrective actions.
Three-scenario budget model (pessimistic, expected, optimistic) showing the impact on monthly profit and cash balance, with trigger points for when contingency actions should be activated.
One-page weekly summary showing revenue, key cost lines, and profit against the weekly budget target. Designed for rapid management review and early identification of trends.

failure_modes: Budget is created at the start of the year and never revisited, becoming irrelevant as actual trading diverges from assumptions
Owner builds the budget on aspirational revenue figures rather than evidence-based projections, masking a structural deficit
Cost increases such as rent reviews, supplier hikes, and wage uplifts are not incorporated into the forecast until they hit the bank account
Cash flow is ignored in favour of profit projections, leading to a profitable-on-paper business that cannot pay its bills on time
kpis: Budget completed and approved before the start of each financial year
Monthly variance review completed within 10 days of month-end, every month
Revenue forecast accuracy within plus or minus 10% of actual for 9 out of 12 months
No unplanned cash shortfalls requiring emergency borrowing or late supplier payments
constraints: Requires access to at least 6 months of historical trading data for meaningful projections
Owner or manager must have basic spreadsheet competency or access to an accountant
Budget must be updated whenever significant cost changes are confirmed

time_load.L1: 8 hours (data gathering, initial budget build, review)
time_load.L2: 4 hours/month (variance analysis, rolling forecast updates, review meetings)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/week (flash reports, benchmarking, strategic planning integration)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly budget-vs-actual review within 10 days of month-end. Mid-year full budget reassessment in Month 6. Annual budget rebuild in the final quarter for the following year. Quarterly review of forecast accuracy to improve projection methodology.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block establishes a proper annual budget and forecasting process for the cafe, covering revenue projections, cost planning, cash flow forecasting, and monthly variance reviews. It transforms financial management from reactive guesswork into structured planning.
implementation_risks: Owners without financial backgrounds may find budgeting intimidating and avoid starting
Seasonal trading patterns can be dramatic in cafe businesses, making first-year budgets particularly inaccurate
The budget can become a source of stress rather than empowerment if variances are treated punitively
meta.notes_for_engine: This block is foundational for D07 and should be activated early in any engagement. Many downstream financial blocks depend on B123 data. Flag as urgent if signals indicate the cafe has no formal budget, is experiencing cash flow problems, or is planning significant expenditure without financial modelling.

blocks.name: Financial Literacy Programme
blocks.purpose: Builds financial understanding across the cafe team so that every staff member — from barista to shift lead — understands how their daily actions affect revenue, costs, and profitability. This block moves financial awareness beyond the owner's spreadsheet and into the operational culture of the business.

detailed_objectives: Train shift leaders and senior staff to understand basic P&L concepts: revenue, cost of goods, gross margin, labour cost, and net profit
Connect daily operational behaviours to financial outcomes so staff understand why waste control, upselling, and efficiency matter
Create a shared financial language within the team so that targets and performance discussions are understood by everyone
Establish regular financial communication rhythms that keep the team informed without overwhelming them
Develop at least two team members (beyond the owner) who can interpret basic financial reports
secondary_objectives: Reduce owner isolation by creating a team that shares responsibility for financial performance
Improve staff retention by demonstrating that the business is well-managed and sustainable
Enable more effective delegation of cost-sensitive decisions such as ordering and wastage management
Prepare future shift leaders and managers for financial responsibilities

blocks.why_this_matters: In most independent cafes, the owner carries the full weight of financial understanding alone. Staff make dozens of decisions daily that affect profitability — over-dosing coffee, discarding usable food, giving away extra shots, failing to upsell, leaving lights and equipment running — without any understanding of the financial impact. A barista who wastes 200g of coffee per shift does not know that this costs the business over 1,500 pounds per year. A kitchen team member who bins slightly bruised fruit rather than using it in a smoothie does not connect this to the cafe's food cost percentage. Financial literacy is not about turning baristas into accountants. It is about ensuring that every team member understands three things: how the cafe makes money, where the money goes, and how their behaviour directly affects whether the business thrives or struggles. UK hospitality has some of the tightest margins of any sector, with net profit for cafes typically between 5-12%. In that narrow band, a team that understands and cares about financial performance can easily contribute 2-3 percentage points of margin improvement through better waste control, more consistent upselling, and smarter operational decisions. Research by UK Hospitality consistently shows that businesses with financially literate teams outperform those where financial knowledge is concentrated in the owner alone.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: The owner or manager prepares a simple one-page financial overview of the cafe — not the full P&L, but the key numbers that matter: average daily revenue, what percentage goes to ingredients, what percentage goes to wages, what is left as profit, and what that profit needs to cover (rent, bills, equipment, owner's income). Express these in terms staff can relate to: 'For every pound a customer spends, roughly 30p goes to ingredients, 35p goes to wages, 25p goes to rent and bills, and only 10p is actual profit.' Week 2: Hold a 30-minute team meeting to share these numbers. Use a visual format — a simple pie chart or a pound coin divided into segments works well. Explain that the cafe is not a charity and not a gold mine: it is a tight-margin business where small daily decisions add up. Give three concrete examples relevant to your cafe: what a wasted flat white actually costs, what happens to profit if every customer buys a pastry with their coffee, and how an extra hour of unnecessary staffing affects the weekly wage bill. Invite questions and make it clear this is about shared understanding, not blame.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Financial Awareness Training Module that all new starters complete within their first month. This should cover: how the cafe earns revenue, the difference between revenue and profit, what COGS means in practice (linking to recipe costing from B056), why labour scheduling matters (linking to B085), and three things every team member can do to protect margins. Create a visible daily revenue target board — this can be a whiteboard near the till area showing today's target, yesterday's actual, and the week-to-date position. Train shift leaders to do a basic shift cost calculation: revenue taken during their shift minus estimated ingredient cost and wage cost for those hours. This does not need to be precise — it needs to be directional so they develop an intuition for whether a shift was financially productive. Introduce a monthly team financial update: 5 minutes at the start of a team meeting sharing how the cafe performed against budget, what went well, and one area for improvement. Keep it positive and solutions-focused.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Financial awareness should now be embedded in daily operations. Shift leaders should naturally reference financial targets when making decisions: 'We are 80 pounds behind target today, let us focus on upselling pastries this afternoon.' Introduce a simple incentive or recognition programme linked to financial targets — this could be as simple as celebrating when the team hits the weekly revenue target or achieves a new low on waste percentage. Develop a Financial Champion role: a team member (not the owner) who takes responsibility for tracking daily revenue, monitoring waste, and presenting the monthly numbers to the team. This is a development opportunity that also reduces the owner's burden. Consider sharing a simplified quarterly P&L with senior staff, showing them how the numbers flow from daily revenue through to profit. Benchmark the team's financial literacy by periodically asking questions: What is our average transaction value? What does a flat white cost us to make? What is our busiest day of the week? A financially literate team is an operationally excellent team.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Prepare a one-page financial overview expressing the cafe's cost structure in simple terms
Create a visual representation of where each pound of revenue goes
Hold a 30-minute team meeting to share the financial overview and take questions
Provide three concrete examples of how daily behaviours affect profitability
Ensure the conversation is framed positively — shared understanding, not blame
Document the key financial facts on a reference card for the team noticeboard

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Financial Awareness Training Module for new starter induction
Install a daily revenue target board visible to the team
Train shift leaders to estimate basic shift-level profitability
Introduce a 5-minute monthly financial update at team meetings
Link financial awareness training to recipe costing (B056) and labour scheduling (B085)
Create a glossary of financial terms relevant to cafe operations for the team handbook

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Establish a Financial Champion role within the team
Introduce recognition or incentives linked to financial target achievement
Share simplified quarterly P&L summaries with senior staff
Conduct periodic financial literacy checks with the team
Integrate financial awareness into performance reviews and development conversations
Celebrate financial wins publicly to reinforce the connection between effort and results

deliverables: One-Page Cafe Financial Overview (visual cost breakdown)
Financial Awareness Training Module for new starters
Monthly Team Financial Update slide template

tools.description: A one-page visual document showing how each pound of revenue is allocated across ingredients, wages, overheads, and profit. Designed to be shared with the whole team in plain language.
A structured training module covering revenue, COGS, labour cost, and profit concepts in cafe-specific terms. Includes worked examples and three actionable behaviours every team member can adopt.
A whiteboard or printed template showing daily revenue target, yesterday's actual, week-to-date position, and a motivational target for the team. Updated daily by the shift leader.
A simple 3-slide template for monthly team financial updates: how the month went vs budget, one thing that went well, and one area for improvement. Designed to be delivered in 5 minutes.

failure_modes: Financial information is shared in a blaming or fear-inducing way, causing staff to disengage rather than take ownership
Owner shares headline numbers but not the context, leaving staff confused about what they can actually influence
Financial literacy training happens once and is never reinforced, fading from team consciousness within weeks
Revenue targets are posted but never updated or discussed, becoming invisible wallpaper
kpis: All shift leaders can explain the cafe's approximate cost structure within 10% accuracy
Daily revenue target board updated every trading day
Monthly financial update delivered to the team within 2 weeks of month-end
At least two team members beyond the owner can interpret a basic monthly financial summary
constraints: Owner must be willing to share financial information with the team at an appropriate level of detail
Financial communication must be adapted to the team's level — avoid jargon and accounting terminology
Sensitive information such as owner's salary or exact profit figures can be kept confidential while still sharing the overall picture

time_load.L1: 3 hours (preparing overview, running team meeting)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (developing training module, setting up target board, training shift leaders)
time_load.L3: 1 hour/month (preparing updates, coaching Financial Champion)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly check that revenue target board is being updated daily. Quarterly review of team financial literacy through informal questioning. Annual refresh of the Financial Awareness Training Module with updated figures. New starter induction reviewed each time to ensure financial module is being delivered.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block builds financial understanding across the whole cafe team, from simple cost-per-cup awareness for baristas to shift-level profitability analysis for shift leaders. It ensures financial responsibility is shared, not siloed with the owner.
implementation_risks: Some owners are reluctant to share financial information with staff, fearing it will be misused or cause anxiety
Staff may feel that financial targets are pressure tactics rather than shared goals if communication is poorly handled
In very small teams (1-2 people), this block may be less relevant as the owner is the team
meta.notes_for_engine: This block depends on B122 (financial tracking) and B123 (budget) being in place. Without actual numbers to share, financial literacy is theoretical. Activate once the cafe has at least basic financial data available. Particularly valuable when signals indicate staff waste, poor upselling, or owner burnout from carrying all financial responsibility alone.

blocks.name: Payroll Cost Optimisation
blocks.purpose: Ensures that payroll — typically the single largest cost in a cafe business — is managed strategically to achieve the right balance between service quality and financial sustainability. This block connects labour scheduling decisions to revenue data so that staffing levels are driven by evidence rather than habit.

detailed_objectives: Establish payroll as a percentage of revenue as a core management KPI, with a target range appropriate to the venue type
Analyse labour cost at the shift level to identify overstaffing during quiet periods and understaffing during peaks
Align rota planning with revenue forecasts from B123 to ensure staffing matches expected demand
Identify opportunities to reduce payroll cost without cutting service quality — through better scheduling, cross-training, and productivity improvement
Ensure compliance with UK employment law including National Minimum Wage, auto-enrolment pensions, and working time regulations
secondary_objectives: Reduce reliance on expensive agency or last-minute casual staff through better forward planning
Develop a staffing model that flexes with seasonal demand rather than maintaining a fixed cost base year-round
Create transparency around labour cost so that shift leaders understand the financial implications of their scheduling decisions
Build a business case for investment in equipment or technology that can reduce labour requirements

blocks.why_this_matters: Labour is the largest controllable cost in any cafe, typically accounting for 28-38% of revenue in UK independent cafes. Unlike rent (fixed) or ingredients (variable but proportional), labour cost can be actively managed through smarter scheduling, cross-training, and productivity improvements. Yet most independent cafe owners schedule staff based on habit — the same people work the same shifts regardless of whether Monday is half as busy as Saturday. The result is chronic overstaffing on quiet days and desperate understaffing on busy ones. A cafe turning over 6,000 pounds per week with labour at 38% rather than 32% is spending an extra 360 pounds per week — nearly 19,000 pounds per year — on wages that are not generating proportional revenue. That 19,000 pounds is often the difference between a profitable business and one that barely breaks even. The National Living Wage increased again in April 2025 and further increases are expected, making payroll optimisation not a luxury but a survival skill. Employer National Insurance contributions, auto-enrolment pension costs, and holiday accrual add approximately 15-20% on top of gross wages, meaning the true cost of an employee earning 12 pounds per hour is closer to 14 pounds. Cafe owners who only think in terms of hourly rate significantly underestimate their true labour cost. This block does not advocate cutting staff to the bone — that destroys service quality, increases turnover, and ultimately costs more. It advocates spending labour pounds intelligently: the right people, in the right roles, at the right times.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Calculate the cafe's current payroll cost as a percentage of revenue. Include gross wages, employer NI, pension contributions, and any benefits. Pull this from the last 3 months of payroll records and compare against till revenue for the same period. Most POS systems can provide hourly and daily revenue breakdowns. Map current staffing against revenue by day of week: create a simple table showing each day's average revenue and total labour cost (hours worked multiplied by average hourly cost including oncosts). Identify the days where labour percentage is highest — these are the overstaffing opportunities. Week 2: Set a target labour percentage based on your venue type. For most independent cafes, 30-35% is a reasonable target; specialty coffee shops with higher average spend can sometimes achieve 25-30%. Compare your current position against this target and calculate the weekly saving if you close the gap. Review the current rota with fresh eyes: Are there shifts where two people are working but one could manage? Are staff starting 30 minutes before opening when 15 minutes would suffice? Are closing shifts dragging on longer than necessary? Make one or two conservative scheduling adjustments this week and measure the impact.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Build a labour scheduling model that maps staffing levels to expected revenue by hour. Use your POS data to create an hourly revenue profile for each day of the week — this reveals exactly when you need more hands and when you are paying for idle time. Develop shift templates that flex based on the day: a Monday might need two staff from opening to close, while a Saturday needs three from 8am to 12pm and two from 12pm to close. Introduce a weekly labour cost review: every Monday, the manager calculates last week's total labour cost against revenue and compares to the target percentage. If labour is consistently over target, investigate whether the issue is scheduling, productivity, or revenue shortfall. Cross-train staff so that the same person can work the till, make coffee, and prepare food — this flexibility allows you to run leaner shifts because each person can cover more roles. Review overtime patterns: if staff regularly work over their contracted hours, this may indicate poor scheduling rather than genuine demand. Implement a rota approval process where the manager must sign off the weekly rota after checking it against the revenue forecast and labour budget.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Labour cost management should now be an integrated part of operational decision-making. Shift leaders should understand the labour cost of their shift and be empowered to make real-time adjustments — sending someone home early on a quiet afternoon with their agreement, or calling in extra support when unexpected demand hits. Track labour productivity metrics: revenue per labour hour is the key measure, calculated as total revenue divided by total labour hours. A well-run UK cafe should target 35-50 pounds per labour hour depending on average transaction value and speed of service. Benchmark quarterly against industry data and your own historical performance. Evaluate technology investments that reduce labour requirements: self-service ordering kiosks, automated milk steamers, batch brew systems, or dishwashers that free up a person from manual washing. Calculate the payback period by comparing the investment cost against the labour hours saved. Review the staffing model seasonally: summer may require more staff for outdoor seating and longer trading hours, while January may warrant reduced hours or temporary role consolidation.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Calculate current payroll cost as a percentage of revenue for the last 3 months
Include all oncosts: employer NI, pension, holiday accrual in the calculation
Map daily revenue against daily labour cost for each day of the week
Set a target labour percentage appropriate to the venue type
Identify the two or three shifts with the highest labour percentage
Make one or two conservative scheduling adjustments and measure impact
Review start and finish times for unnecessary early arrivals or late departures

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build an hourly revenue profile for each day of the week using POS data
Create flexible shift templates that match staffing to expected demand patterns
Introduce a weekly labour cost review every Monday morning
Cross-train staff across till, coffee, and food preparation roles
Implement a rota approval process checking against revenue forecast and labour budget
Review and reduce overtime patterns through better forward scheduling
Calculate true hourly cost per employee including all oncosts

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track revenue per labour hour as a core weekly KPI
Empower shift leaders to make real-time staffing adjustments based on demand
Evaluate technology investments for labour hour reduction and calculate payback periods
Benchmark labour productivity quarterly against industry data
Review the staffing model seasonally and adjust for trading pattern changes
Integrate labour cost analysis into annual budget planning cycle

deliverables: Labour Cost Analysis Spreadsheet (daily and weekly breakdown)
Flexible Shift Template Library (by day type)

tools.description: Weekly spreadsheet tracking total labour cost against revenue by day, calculating labour percentage and identifying overstaffed and understaffed periods. Includes oncost calculations for true hourly cost.

A set of shift templates for different day types (quiet weekday, busy weekday, Saturday, Sunday) specifying staffing levels by hour, roles required, and expected revenue. Used as the basis for weekly rota building.
Weekly and monthly tracker showing revenue per labour hour trend, benchmarked against target and industry averages. Highlights shifts or days with unusually low productivity for investigation.
A simple calculator for evaluating equipment or technology investments that reduce labour hours. Compares purchase cost against monthly labour savings to determine payback period in months.

failure_modes: Labour cost is cut too aggressively, leading to poor service, stressed staff, higher turnover, and ultimately higher costs
Scheduling remains habit-based despite having revenue data available, because changing rotas causes staff complaints
Oncosts are ignored in calculations, underestimating true labour cost by 15-20% and giving a false sense of control
Revenue per labour hour improves but only because staff are burning out, creating an unsustainable short-term gain
kpis: Payroll cost (including oncosts) maintained within target percentage range for the venue type
Revenue per labour hour tracked weekly and trending upward or stable
Weekly rota built against revenue forecast with labour cost pre-approved before the week starts
Zero unplanned overtime exceeding 5% of total scheduled hours in any given week
constraints: All scheduling must comply with UK employment law including National Minimum Wage, Working Time Regulations, and rest break requirements
Staff contracts and agreed hours must be respected — optimisation cannot involve unilateral reduction of contracted hours
Payroll changes should be communicated transparently to avoid damaging team morale and trust

time_load.L1: 4 hours (data gathering, initial analysis, first scheduling adjustments)
time_load.L2: 3 hours/week (rota building against revenue forecast, weekly labour review)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (productivity benchmarking, technology evaluation, seasonal model review)

blocks.review_protocol: Weekly labour cost review every Monday. Monthly payroll percentage trend analysis. Quarterly review of revenue per labour hour against targets and industry benchmarks. Annual review of staffing model against business plan and seasonal patterns.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block ensures payroll — the cafe's biggest controllable cost — is managed strategically by linking staffing levels to revenue data, setting target labour percentages, and building flexible rotas that match demand rather than habit.
implementation_risks: Staff may resist schedule changes, particularly if they lose preferred shifts or hours
Cutting labour too quickly can damage service quality and create a vicious cycle of declining revenue
Owner-operators often undercount their own labour cost, distorting the true picture
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate labour cost is above 35% of revenue, when the owner reports difficulty making payroll, or when rotas appear to be built without reference to trading patterns. This block connects directly to B085 (scheduling) and B122 (financial tracking). Handle sensitively — labour cost is ultimately about people's livelihoods.

blocks.name: Occupancy Cost Strategy
blocks.purpose: Provides a structured approach to managing rent, rates, and property-related costs — typically the second largest cost after payroll — ensuring the cafe is not overpaying for its premises and is maximising the revenue potential of every square foot it occupies.

detailed_objectives: Calculate total occupancy cost as a percentage of revenue and benchmark against industry norms
Establish a rent review and lease management calendar to ensure the business is never caught off guard by landlord actions
Evaluate whether the current premises deliver sufficient revenue per square foot to justify the occupancy cost
Identify opportunities to reduce rates, service charges, or utility costs through active management
Develop a negotiation strategy for lease renewals or rent reviews that is evidence-based rather than emotional
secondary_objectives: Assess whether the lease terms are commercially appropriate and identify restrictive clauses that may limit the business
Evaluate the financial case for subletting unused space, adding outdoor seating, or extending trading hours to spread occupancy cost
Build a relationship with the landlord that supports the long-term viability of the cafe
Create a contingency plan for significant rent increases at review points

blocks.why_this_matters: Occupancy costs — rent, business rates, service charges, buildings insurance, and property maintenance — are the largest fixed cost in most cafe businesses, typically representing 10-18% of revenue. Unlike labour or ingredients, these costs do not flex with trading volume: the rent is due whether the cafe serves 50 customers or 500. This makes occupancy cost management strategically critical. A cafe paying 30,000 pounds per year in rent on a 5-year lease is committing to 150,000 pounds in property costs — likely the single largest financial obligation the owner will ever make. Yet many cafe operators sign leases without professional advice, agree to upward-only rent review clauses without understanding the implications, and fail to challenge business rates assessments even when they are clearly incorrect. In England, business rates are based on the Valuation Office Agency's assessment of rateable value, and small cafes may qualify for Small Business Rate Relief — potentially saving thousands of pounds per year. Many cafe owners do not know this relief exists or assume they do not qualify. Lease negotiations are among the few moments where a cafe can make a decision worth tens of thousands of pounds. A 10% reduction in rent negotiated at a 5-year lease renewal saves 15,000 pounds over the term — equivalent to serving 10,000 additional flat whites. This block ensures the cafe takes an informed, strategic approach to its property costs rather than simply accepting what the landlord or the council demands.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Calculate the total occupancy cost for the last 12 months. Include rent, business rates, service charges, buildings insurance, and any landlord-required maintenance or property costs. Express this as a percentage of total revenue. The industry benchmark for independent cafes is 10-15% of revenue — anything above 18% is a red flag. Check the business rates assessment: log into the Valuation Office Agency website and verify the rateable value of your property. If you believe it is too high, note the appeal deadline. Check whether you qualify for Small Business Rate Relief — if your rateable value is below 15,000 pounds, you may be entitled to partial or full relief. Week 2: Review your lease document. Note the key dates: lease expiry, rent review dates, break clause dates. Identify the rent review mechanism — is it upward-only, open market, or linked to an index? Note any restrictions on use, assignment, alterations, or subletting. If you do not have a copy of your lease, request one from your solicitor or landlord. Create a lease management calendar with all critical dates flagged at least 6 months in advance.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Calculate revenue per square foot by dividing annual revenue by the total trading floor area. This metric allows you to compare your premises efficiency against industry benchmarks and evaluate whether the space is working hard enough. If revenue per square foot is low, consider whether layout changes, extended trading hours, or additional revenue streams could improve utilisation. Commission a professional lease review from a commercial property solicitor if you have not had one — costs are typically 300-500 pounds and can identify clauses that save or cost thousands. Prepare for your next rent review by gathering evidence: comparable rents in the area, any negative factors affecting the property (limited parking, poor footfall, building condition), and your trading history. Landlords negotiate with professional advisors; you should too — consider instructing a commercial surveyor to act on your behalf at the next rent review. Investigate utility cost reduction: compare energy tariffs, consider LED lighting, check whether heating and cooling systems are efficient. Utility costs are often grouped with occupancy costs and can represent 3-5% of revenue in their own right.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Occupancy cost management should become a strategic competency, not a reactive exercise. Maintain a rolling 12-month view of total occupancy cost as a percentage of revenue and track trends. Build a relationship with your landlord based on commercial respect — landlords prefer reliable, long-term tenants who look after the property, and this relationship can be leveraged during negotiations. Evaluate the premises strategically at each lease milestone: Is this the right location for the next 5 years? Has the local area changed? Would a different premises offer better rent-to-revenue ratio? Explore revenue-enhancing uses of the space: can you sublet a corner for a florist or retail display? Can you offer the space for events outside trading hours? Can outdoor seating be added or expanded? Consider whether a rent deposit or bank guarantee could be renegotiated to free up working capital. For multi-site operators, centralise lease management and negotiate portfolio terms with landlords who own multiple properties.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Calculate total occupancy cost for the last 12 months (rent, rates, service charge, insurance, property maintenance)
Express occupancy cost as a percentage of revenue and compare against the 10-15% benchmark
Check business rates assessment on the Valuation Office Agency website
Verify eligibility for Small Business Rate Relief
Locate and review the lease document, noting key dates and clauses
Create a lease management calendar with rent review and break clause dates flagged 6 months ahead

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Calculate revenue per square foot and compare against industry benchmarks
Commission a professional lease review from a commercial property solicitor
Gather comparable rent evidence for the next rent review negotiation
Consider instructing a commercial surveyor for rent review representation
Audit utility costs and compare energy tariffs for potential savings
Evaluate layout or operational changes to improve revenue per square foot
Review service charge invoices for accuracy and challenge any discrepancies

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track occupancy cost as a percentage of revenue on a rolling 12-month basis
Build a constructive landlord relationship that supports lease negotiations
Evaluate premises strategically at each lease milestone against business needs
Explore revenue-enhancing uses of the space (subletting, events, outdoor seating)
Renegotiate rent deposit or guarantee terms to optimise working capital
Integrate occupancy cost strategy into the annual budget and 3-year plan

deliverables: Occupancy Cost Analysis (annual breakdown with revenue percentage)
Lease Management Calendar with key dates

tools.description: Annual spreadsheet tracking all occupancy-related costs (rent, rates, service charge, insurance, maintenance) against revenue, calculating occupancy percentage monthly and identifying trends.
A calendar document flagging all critical lease dates — rent reviews, break clauses, lease expiry, insurance renewal — with 6-month advance warnings and action checklists for each milestone.
A structured document for gathering and presenting evidence at rent review negotiations, including comparable rents, property condition notes, footfall data, and any negative factors affecting value.
Calculates annual revenue per square foot of trading space and compares against industry benchmarks, highlighting whether the premises is being used efficiently.

failure_modes: Rent review dates pass without preparation, leaving the owner to accept whatever the landlord proposes
Business rates are paid without challenge even when the rateable value is clearly excessive or relief is available
The lease is signed without professional review, locking the business into unfavourable terms for years
Occupancy cost creep goes unnoticed because property costs are treated as fixed rather than manageable
kpis: Total occupancy cost maintained below 15% of revenue
All lease milestone dates actioned at least 6 months in advance
Business rates assessment reviewed annually and challenged where appropriate
Revenue per square foot tracked quarterly and trending upward
constraints: Lease terms are legally binding and cannot be changed unilaterally — negotiation requires landlord agreement
Business rates appeals have specific deadlines and procedures that must be followed
Professional advice (solicitor, surveyor) involves cost that must be weighed against potential savings

time_load.L1: 4 hours (cost analysis, rates check, lease review, calendar setup)
time_load.L2: 8-12 hours (professional lease review, rent review preparation, utility audit)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (tracking, landlord relationship, strategic premises evaluation)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly tracking of occupancy cost percentage. Lease milestone actions triggered 6 months before each key date. Annual business rates assessment review. Full strategic premises evaluation at each lease break or renewal point.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block ensures the cafe actively manages its property costs — rent, rates, service charges — rather than passively accepting them. It covers lease management, rates relief, rent review preparation, and premises efficiency analysis.
implementation_risks: Challenging business rates or negotiating rent can feel confrontational, and some owners avoid it
Professional advice costs money upfront, which can deter cash-strapped operators even when the potential savings are significant
Poor landlord relationships can make lease negotiations adversarial rather than commercial
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate occupancy cost exceeds 15% of revenue, when a rent review or lease renewal is approaching, or when the owner mentions they have never checked their business rates. High priority if the cafe is in a marginal financial position where occupancy cost reduction could be the difference between survival and closure.

blocks.name: Cost Structure Excellence
blocks.purpose: Provides a holistic view of the cafe's entire cost base and establishes a continuous improvement approach to cost management. This block synthesises payroll optimisation (B125), occupancy strategy (B126), and all other cost lines into an integrated cost structure that is benchmarked, monitored, and actively refined.

detailed_objectives: Map the complete cost structure of the cafe into a clear, categorised framework: COGS, labour, occupancy, operating overheads, and discretionary spend
Benchmark each cost category against industry norms and identify areas where costs are above benchmark
Establish a quarterly cost review process that systematically examines each cost line for reduction opportunities
Create a cost hierarchy that distinguishes between essential costs, efficiency-improvable costs, and discretionary costs that can be flexed
Develop supplier management practices that leverage buying power and prevent cost creep
secondary_objectives: Build a cost database that tracks unit costs over time, revealing trends and enabling early intervention
Create a decision framework for evaluating make-vs-buy decisions (e.g., baking in-house vs. buying in)
Identify hidden costs that are often overlooked: card processing fees, packaging, breakage, shrinkage, and waste disposal
Establish a cost-conscious culture where every team member considers value for money in their daily decisions

blocks.why_this_matters: Cafe profitability is determined not by any single cost line but by the overall cost structure — the relationship between all costs and the revenue they generate. An operator who controls food cost brilliantly but ignores utility waste, overpays for packaging, and never negotiates card processing fees may still run an unprofitable business. Cost structure excellence means understanding every significant outflow and ensuring that each one is appropriate, competitive, and regularly challenged. UK independent cafes typically have a cost structure that looks roughly like this: COGS 28-35%, labour 28-35%, occupancy 10-15%, other overheads 10-15%, leaving a net profit of 5-12%. The difference between a cafe at 5% net margin and one at 12% is rarely about one dramatic cost saving — it is about disciplined management of every cost category, compounding small improvements across the whole business. A 1% improvement in food cost, 1% in labour, and 1% in overheads collectively delivers a 3% improvement in net margin — potentially doubling profit on a 5% margin business. Hidden costs are a particular danger: card processing fees (1.5-3% of card revenue), delivery platform commissions (15-35%), packaging costs, waste disposal charges, and equipment maintenance can collectively represent 5-8% of revenue but are often not tracked individually. This block brings every cost into the light, benchmarks it, and establishes a rhythm of continuous improvement that protects and grows the bottom line.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Create a complete cost map for the cafe. Using the last 3 months of bank statements, invoices, and payroll records, categorise every outgoing payment into five groups: Cost of Goods Sold (ingredients, packaging, coffee beans, milk, bakery items), Labour (wages, employer NI, pensions, benefits), Occupancy (rent, rates, service charge, insurance), Operating Overheads (utilities, cleaning supplies, card processing fees, POS subscription, waste disposal, accounting fees, equipment maintenance, internet, phone), and Discretionary (marketing, subscriptions, training, equipment upgrades). Calculate each category as a percentage of revenue. Week 2: Compare your cost structure against industry benchmarks. Identify any category that is more than 2 percentage points above the benchmark — these are your priority areas. For each priority area, list three potential actions to reduce cost without reducing quality. Review every subscription and recurring payment: cancel anything unused, downgrade anything over-specified, and renegotiate anything that has not been reviewed in 12 months. Check card processing fees — many cafes are paying rates set when they first opened and have never renegotiated despite increased transaction volumes.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Establish a quarterly cost review meeting (1-2 hours) where each major cost category is examined systematically. For each category, review: current cost as a percentage of revenue, trend over the last 3 months, comparison to benchmark, and any upcoming changes (contract renewals, price increases). Develop a supplier management approach: for your top 5 suppliers by spend, ensure you have competitive quotes, review pricing at least annually, and maintain relationships that support negotiation. Create a make-vs-buy decision framework for key products: is it cheaper and better to bake cakes in-house or buy from a wholesale bakery? This analysis should include the full cost of in-house production (ingredients, labour, equipment, energy, waste) not just ingredient cost. Track unit costs for your top 20 products — what does each flat white, sandwich, and slice of cake actually cost to produce including all direct costs? This granular understanding enables pricing decisions and menu engineering. Implement a purchase order approval process where any non-routine expenditure above a set threshold (e.g., 100 pounds) requires manager or owner approval before commitment.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Cost management should be embedded in the culture as a shared responsibility, not just the owner's burden. Every team member should understand that the cafe operates on thin margins and that waste in any form directly reduces profitability. Introduce a cost innovation programme: encourage team members to suggest cost-saving ideas and reward the best ones. Some of the most effective cost savings come from frontline staff who see waste and inefficiency daily. Benchmark costs annually against published industry data and, if possible, against peer cafes through informal networking or business groups. Develop long-term supplier partnerships that provide price stability, preferential terms, and early access to new products. Evaluate group purchasing opportunities through buying groups or local cafe alliances. Review the cost structure as part of the annual budget process (B123), ensuring that cost targets for the coming year are realistic, evidence-based, and improvement-oriented. Consider engaging an accountant for an annual cost efficiency review — a fresh pair of professional eyes can often spot savings that become invisible to the owner.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Create a complete cost map categorising all outgoings into COGS, Labour, Occupancy, Overheads, and Discretionary
Calculate each category as a percentage of revenue for the last 3 months
Benchmark against industry norms and identify categories more than 2 points above benchmark
Review and cancel or renegotiate all unused subscriptions and recurring payments
Check card processing fees and request competitive quotes
List three potential cost reduction actions for each above-benchmark category

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Establish a quarterly cost review meeting examining each major cost category
Develop a supplier management approach with annual pricing reviews for top 5 suppliers
Create a make-vs-buy decision framework for key products
Track unit costs for the top 20 products sold
Implement a purchase order approval process for non-routine expenditure
Build a cost trend tracker showing each category's percentage of revenue over time

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce a cost innovation programme encouraging staff cost-saving suggestions
Benchmark costs annually against industry data and peer cafes
Develop long-term supplier partnerships for price stability and preferential terms
Evaluate group purchasing opportunities through buying groups
Engage an accountant for an annual cost efficiency review
Integrate cost structure targets into the annual budget process

deliverables: Complete Cost Map (categorised cost breakdown with revenue percentages)
Quarterly Cost Review Meeting Agenda and Template
Unit Cost Calculator for top products

tools.description: A comprehensive template categorising all cafe costs into five groups (COGS, Labour, Occupancy, Overheads, Discretionary), calculating each as a percentage of revenue and benchmarking against industry norms.
Structured agenda for the quarterly cost review meeting, examining each cost category's current level, trend, benchmark comparison, and action items for improvement.
Tracker for the cafe's top suppliers showing annual spend, last price review date, contract terms, alternative suppliers identified, and next review date.

A structured template for evaluating whether to produce in-house or buy externally, comparing full costs (ingredients, labour, equipment, energy, waste) against wholesale purchase price and quality factors.

failure_modes: Cost reviews focus only on the obvious categories (food, labour) while hidden costs like processing fees and packaging grow unchecked
Supplier relationships become complacent, with prices never challenged because the owner values convenience over competitiveness
Cost cutting is approached with a hatchet rather than a scalpel, damaging quality or team morale in pursuit of short-term savings
The cost map is created once but never maintained, becoming outdated as costs change and new expenses are added
kpis: Total cost structure (excluding owner compensation) maintained within target range for the venue type
Quarterly cost review completed every quarter with documented actions
Top 5 suppliers by spend reviewed for pricing competitiveness at least annually
Net profit margin improving or stable year-on-year, driven by cost structure improvements
constraints: Cost reduction must not compromise food safety, product quality, or regulatory compliance
Supplier changes require careful transition planning to avoid service disruption
Some costs are genuinely fixed or non-negotiable and should not consume disproportionate management time

time_load.L1: 6 hours (initial cost mapping, benchmarking, subscription review)
time_load.L2: 4 hours/quarter (quarterly cost review, supplier management, unit cost tracking)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (innovation programme, benchmarking, strategic cost planning)

blocks.review_protocol: Quarterly formal cost review examining all categories. Annual supplier pricing review for top 5 suppliers. Monthly informal check of cost percentages against budget. Annual cost structure assessment as part of budget process.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block takes a holistic view of the cafe's entire cost base, mapping every outgoing into categories, benchmarking against industry norms, and establishing continuous improvement processes. It is the integration point for payroll (B125) and occupancy (B126) strategies into an overall cost excellence approach.
implementation_risks: The breadth of cost categories can feel overwhelming, leading to paralysis rather than action
Small cost lines are individually insignificant but collectively material — the owner must resist the temptation to dismiss them
Cost benchmarks vary significantly by location and venue type, so industry averages should be used as guides not absolute targets
meta.notes_for_engine: This is a capstone block for cost management within D07. It should be activated after B125 (payroll) and B126 (occupancy) are underway. Particularly valuable when signals indicate the cafe is profitable but margins are thin, or when costs are rising faster than revenue. Also relevant when the owner cannot clearly articulate where their money goes.

blocks.name: Delivery Commission Analysis
blocks.purpose: Ensures the cafe understands the true cost of third-party delivery platforms (Deliveroo, Uber Eats, Just Eat) and makes evidence-based decisions about whether delivery generates genuine profit or merely creates revenue that costs more than it earns. This block brings financial rigour to a channel that many cafes adopt without understanding its economics.

detailed_objectives: Calculate the true net margin on delivery orders after accounting for all costs: platform commission, packaging, additional labour, and food cost adjustments
Compare delivery channel profitability against dine-in and takeaway channels on a like-for-like basis
Determine the break-even point for delivery operations and whether current volumes justify the channel
Negotiate improved commission rates or terms with delivery platforms based on volume data
Make an informed strategic decision about which platforms to use, which to exit, and what role delivery plays in the business
secondary_objectives: Identify which menu items are profitable for delivery and which are loss-makers when commission is factored in
Understand the impact of delivery on kitchen capacity and whether delivery orders displace higher-margin dine-in customers
Evaluate whether delivery acts as a genuine customer acquisition channel or merely cannibalises existing customers
Build a data-driven case for adjusting delivery menu pricing upward to recover commission costs

blocks.why_this_matters: Third-party delivery platforms have transformed UK cafe trading since 2018, but the economics are brutal. Deliveroo charges 25-35% commission on each order, Uber Eats 15-30%, and Just Eat 14-25% depending on the service level. On a 12 pound delivery order with a food cost of 35%, a cafe paying 30% commission retains 35% (4.20 pounds) after commission and food cost — but has not yet accounted for packaging (typically 0.50-1.00 per order), the additional labour required to process delivery orders, or the energy cost of keeping the kitchen running for a lower-margin channel. When all costs are properly allocated, many cafes discover that delivery orders generate a net margin of 5-10% compared to 15-25% for dine-in orders. Some discover they are actively losing money on every delivery. Yet operators continue offering delivery because the revenue looks good on the top line, because customers expect it, or because they fear losing visibility if they leave the platforms. This is a classic case of vanity metrics over financial reality. The decision to offer delivery is not inherently wrong — for some cafes, delivery provides genuine incremental revenue, keeps the kitchen productive during quiet periods, and introduces the cafe to customers who later visit in person. But this decision must be made with clear financial data, not assumptions. The cafe needs to know exactly what delivery is costing, whether it is genuinely incremental, and whether the same kitchen capacity could be used more profitably for other purposes.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Pull delivery reports from each platform you use for the last 3 months. Most platforms provide detailed sales reports showing gross order value, commission charged, and net payment. Create a simple spreadsheet tracking monthly delivery revenue, commission paid, and net revenue received from each platform. Calculate the effective commission rate (commission divided by gross revenue) — this may differ from the headline rate due to promotions, service fees, or tiered pricing. Week 2: Calculate the true cost of a delivery order. Take your average delivery order value and subtract: platform commission, estimated food cost (use menu item costs from B056), packaging cost per order, and an estimate of additional labour cost per order (typically 3-5 minutes of staff time at their full hourly rate including oncosts). The result is the true net margin per delivery order. Compare this against your average dine-in and takeaway margin per order. If delivery margin is negative or below 5%, this is a red flag requiring immediate attention. Document the findings clearly — many owners are shocked when they see the actual numbers.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Analyse delivery profitability at the product level. Not all menu items perform equally on delivery: a 4.50 pound flat white at 30% commission leaves almost nothing after costs, while a 14 pound brunch platter may still generate acceptable margin. Identify which items are profitable for delivery and which are loss-makers. This analysis feeds directly into B129 (Delivery-Only Menu Engineering). Review delivery volume patterns: what times of day and days of the week generate most delivery orders? Is delivery displacing dine-in capacity during peak periods, or filling quiet gaps? If delivery orders compete with dine-in customers for kitchen capacity during the Saturday morning rush, they may be actively reducing total profitability. Contact your platform account managers and negotiate improved terms. Platforms are more willing to negotiate than most operators realise, especially for established cafes with good reviews and consistent order volumes. Ask for reduced commission rates, marketing credits, or removal of penalty clauses. Track delivery customer behaviour: do delivery customers ever convert to dine-in? Most platforms restrict access to customer data, but you can track this indirectly through loyalty programmes or delivery-exclusive offers with dine-in vouchers.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Delivery channel management should now be a strategic discipline, not a passive acceptance of platform terms. Review delivery performance monthly as part of the financial review cycle (B123). Track delivery as a separate profit centre with its own revenue, costs, and margin — do not merge it with dine-in figures or it becomes invisible. Develop a platform strategy: you do not need to be on every platform. Some cafes find that being on one platform with negotiated terms is more profitable than being on three at standard rates. Consider whether delivery at certain times should be paused to protect dine-in capacity during peak periods — most platforms allow you to control availability hours. Evaluate the marketing value of platform presence: even if individual order margins are thin, the visibility on Deliveroo or Uber Eats may introduce new customers who later visit in person. Quantify this value rather than assuming it. Review annually whether delivery remains strategically appropriate for the business, factoring in the growth of direct ordering channels (B130).

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Pull delivery sales reports from each platform for the last 3 months
Calculate effective commission rate for each platform
Calculate the true net margin per delivery order including commission, food cost, packaging, and labour
Compare delivery margin against dine-in and takeaway margins
Flag any platform or product category where delivery margin is negative or below 5%
Document findings in a clear summary for business decision-making

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Analyse delivery profitability at the product level to identify profitable and loss-making items
Map delivery order volume by time of day and day of week against dine-in patterns
Contact platform account managers to negotiate improved commission rates
Assess whether delivery orders displace higher-margin dine-in customers during peak periods
Track delivery customer conversion to dine-in through indirect measurement
Consider adjusting delivery menu pricing upward to recover commission costs

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Track delivery as a separate profit centre with its own P&L
Review delivery performance monthly as part of the financial review cycle
Develop a platform strategy selecting optimal platforms and negotiating terms
Control delivery availability hours to protect dine-in capacity during peaks
Evaluate delivery marketing value and customer acquisition contribution annually
Integrate delivery strategy with direct ordering channel development (B130)

tools.description: Monthly spreadsheet tracking gross delivery revenue, commission paid, net revenue, packaging costs, and estimated additional labour cost for each delivery platform, calculating the true effective commission rate and net margin.

A per-order calculator showing the true profitability of a delivery order after all costs: platform commission, food cost, packaging, and labour allocation. Compares against dine-in and takeaway margins.

Analyses each menu item's profitability when sold via delivery, accounting for platform commission and packaging. Identifies items that should be promoted, repriced, or removed from the delivery menu.
A structured document for preparing delivery platform commission negotiations, including current volumes, order values, review ratings, comparable rates, and specific terms being requested.

failure_modes: Delivery revenue is celebrated on the top line while the true cost is never calculated, masking losses
Commission rates are accepted as fixed and non-negotiable when platforms often have flexibility for good operators
Delivery orders during peak periods displace higher-margin dine-in customers without anyone recognising the trade-off
The cafe stays on multiple platforms out of fear of missing orders, paying duplicate fixed costs without proportional incremental revenue
kpis: True net margin per delivery order calculated and tracked monthly
Delivery channel profitability reviewed as a separate profit centre each month
Platform commission rates negotiated or reviewed at least annually
Delivery margin per order above 8% after all costs, or strategic justification documented for continuing below this threshold
constraints: Platform contracts may include minimum commitment periods or exclusivity clauses that limit immediate changes
Removing unprofitable delivery items may reduce order frequency and affect platform ranking algorithms
Customer expectations around delivery availability must be managed carefully to avoid negative reviews

time_load.L1: 4 hours (pulling reports, building analysis, calculating true margins)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (product-level analysis, platform negotiation preparation, volume pattern mapping)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (monthly delivery P&L review, strategy refinement)

blocks.review_protocol: Monthly delivery channel P&L review. Quarterly product-level profitability assessment. Annual platform strategy review and commission negotiation. Immediate review if delivery volume changes significantly or platform terms are altered.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block forces a hard look at whether delivery platforms are actually making or losing money for the cafe. It calculates true per-order margins after commission, packaging, and labour, and provides the data needed to negotiate better terms or exit unprofitable platforms.
implementation_risks: Owners may resist the analysis if they suspect delivery is unprofitable but depend on the revenue psychologically
Platform algorithms may penalise cafes that reduce menu items or availability, creating a fear of making changes
Exiting a platform may be complicated by contract terms or customer expectations
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate the cafe uses delivery platforms but has never calculated true delivery margin, when delivery revenue is significant but profitability is unclear, or when the owner mentions feeling trapped by platform commissions. This block is a prerequisite for B129 (Delivery-Only Menu Engineering) and B130 (Direct Ordering Channel).

blocks.name: Delivery-Only Menu Engineering
blocks.purpose: Designs a delivery-specific menu that is engineered for profitability within the commission-heavy delivery platform model. Rather than simply listing the dine-in menu on delivery apps, this block creates a curated delivery offering where every item has been assessed for margin viability, travel durability, and operational efficiency.

detailed_objectives: Create a delivery menu that is distinct from the dine-in menu, optimised for items that travel well and maintain margin after commission
Price delivery menu items to recover or partially offset platform commission without deterring customers
Engineer delivery bundles and meal deals that increase average order value and improve per-order profitability
Remove or reformulate items that are loss-makers on delivery or arrive in poor condition
Reduce kitchen complexity during delivery peaks by limiting the delivery menu to operationally efficient items
secondary_objectives: Develop delivery-exclusive items that cannot be compared to dine-in pricing, supporting higher price points
Design packaging requirements into the menu selection so that every delivery item has appropriate, cost-effective packaging
Improve delivery review scores by only offering items that genuinely work for delivery
Create a menu architecture on delivery platforms that guides customers toward higher-margin items

blocks.why_this_matters: Most cafes make the fundamental mistake of uploading their entire dine-in menu onto delivery platforms without modification. This creates multiple problems. First, many items do not travel well — a carefully plated eggs Benedict becomes a soggy mess after 20 minutes in a delivery bag, generating poor reviews and refund requests. Second, items that are marginally profitable in-house become loss-makers when 25-35% commission is deducted. A 4.50 pound flat white with a 30% food cost leaves 3.15 pounds in-house but only 1.80 pounds after a 30% delivery commission — barely covering the cup, lid, and labour to make it. Third, the full dine-in menu creates unnecessary kitchen complexity during delivery-heavy periods, slowing both delivery and dine-in service. Smart delivery operators design a separate delivery menu from the ground up. This menu features items with higher inherent margins, items that travel well and arrive looking appetising, items that can be prepared quickly and consistently, and items that are packaged cost-effectively. Pricing is typically 10-20% higher than dine-in to offset commission — and customer research consistently shows that delivery customers expect and accept higher prices because they are paying for convenience. The best delivery menus also include bundles and meal deals that increase average order value from 12-15 pounds to 18-22 pounds per order. Since many delivery costs are fixed per order (packaging, labour time, commission minimum), increasing order value dramatically improves per-order profitability.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Review your current delivery menu. List every item currently available on delivery platforms and, using the analysis from B128, mark each item as Profitable (margin above 10% after all delivery costs), Marginal (margin 0-10%), or Loss-Making (negative margin). Separately assess each item's delivery suitability: does it travel well, does it arrive looking good, does it require expensive packaging, and does it generate complaints or refund requests? Items that are both loss-making and poor travellers should be removed from the delivery menu immediately. Week 2: Design a streamlined delivery menu using only items that are both profitable and delivery-suitable. Adjust pricing upward by 10-15% compared to dine-in — most delivery platforms allow different pricing for delivery versus collection. Create two or three bundle deals (e.g., coffee plus pastry, sandwich plus drink plus snack) at price points that increase average order value while maintaining margin. Review and update the delivery menu on each platform, removing poor performers and adding bundles.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop delivery-exclusive items that cannot be directly compared to your dine-in prices. These might include branded meal boxes, signature bundles, or items available only for delivery. These exclusive items allow you to set delivery-appropriate prices without customers feeling overcharged compared to dine-in. Engineer items specifically for delivery durability: wraps travel better than open sandwiches, grain bowls travel better than plated salads, and cold brew travels better than hot pour-over. Work with packaging suppliers to find the right packaging for each delivery item — packaging that maintains food quality and temperature while being cost-effective and environmentally responsible. Optimise menu positioning on the delivery platforms: featured images, item descriptions, and menu category order all influence what customers select. Promote higher-margin items with professional photography and compelling descriptions. Test menu changes systematically — modify one element at a time (pricing, menu composition, bundles) and measure the impact on order volume, average order value, and overall delivery profitability over a 2-4 week period.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The delivery menu should be a living document, reviewed quarterly against profitability data. Track delivery customer ratings and refund rates by item to identify ongoing quality issues. Develop seasonal delivery menus that reflect ingredient availability and customer preferences — warm soups and stews in winter, cold salads and iced drinks in summer. Monitor competitor delivery menus and pricing on the same platforms to ensure your offering remains competitive and well-positioned. Consider limited-time delivery specials that create urgency and drive trial. Use platform analytics to understand customer ordering patterns: when do they order, what do they search for, what do they add to basket but not purchase? Use these insights to refine the menu continuously. Integrate delivery menu planning with overall menu engineering (B056) to ensure consistency in brand quality while optimising for channel-specific requirements.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Review current delivery menu and categorise every item as Profitable, Marginal, or Loss-Making
Assess each item's delivery suitability: travel quality, packaging needs, complaint rate
Remove items that are both loss-making and poor travellers
Adjust delivery pricing upward by 10-15% compared to dine-in
Create two or three bundle deals to increase average order value
Update delivery menus on all platforms with the streamlined offering

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop delivery-exclusive items that support higher price points without direct dine-in comparison
Engineer menu items specifically for delivery durability and presentation on arrival
Work with packaging suppliers to optimise cost and quality for each delivery item
Optimise menu positioning, photography, and descriptions on delivery platforms
Test menu changes systematically over 2-4 week periods and measure impact
Track delivery refund rates and customer ratings by menu item

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Review delivery menu profitability quarterly and adjust based on data
Develop seasonal delivery menus reflecting ingredient availability and customer preferences
Monitor competitor delivery menus and pricing on the same platforms
Use platform analytics to understand customer ordering patterns and optimise accordingly
Integrate delivery menu planning with overall menu engineering processes
Test limited-time delivery specials to drive trial and measure customer response

deliverables: Streamlined Delivery Menu with pricing strategy
Bundle and Meal Deal Design Sheet
Delivery Item Specification Cards (including packaging requirements)

tools.description: A matrix assessing every potential delivery menu item across four dimensions: margin after commission, travel durability, packaging cost, and customer satisfaction. Items are scored and ranked to determine inclusion on the delivery menu.

A worksheet for designing delivery bundles and meal deals, calculating the combined margin, packaging requirements, and target price point that increases average order value while maintaining profitability.

A card for each delivery menu item specifying preparation method, packaging type, sealing instructions, maximum hold time, and any modifications needed versus the dine-in version to ensure delivery quality.

A structured quarterly review examining each delivery menu item's sales volume, margin, customer rating, and refund rate. Includes prompts for seasonal adjustments, new item testing, and competitor monitoring.

failure_modes: The delivery menu is a carbon copy of the dine-in menu, including items that travel poorly and lose money after commission
Delivery pricing remains identical to dine-in despite the 25-35% commission burden, destroying margins
Bundle deals are designed for appeal rather than profitability, actually worsening margins through discounting
Menu changes are made reactively based on complaints rather than proactively based on profitability data
kpis: Every item on the delivery menu has a positive margin after all delivery costs
Average delivery order value increased by at least 15% through bundles and menu design
Delivery refund and complaint rate below 3% of orders
Delivery menu reviewed and updated at least quarterly based on profitability data
constraints: Platform terms may restrict the degree of pricing differentiation between delivery and collection
Delivery menu changes require updating across all active platforms, which can be time-consuming
Customer expectations set by the dine-in experience must be managed when the delivery menu is different

time_load.L1: 6 hours (menu assessment, pricing adjustments, bundle design, platform updates)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (delivery-exclusive item development, packaging optimisation, platform positioning)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/quarter (quarterly menu review, competitor monitoring, analytics review)

blocks.review_protocol: Quarterly delivery menu profitability review. Monthly check of delivery customer ratings and refund rates by item. Seasonal menu updates aligned with ingredient availability. Immediate review if delivery profitability drops below target or new competitors enter the market.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a delivery-specific menu engineered for profitability within the commission-heavy delivery platform model. It removes loss-making items, adjusts pricing, develops bundles to increase order value, and ensures every delivery item travels well and arrives in good condition.
implementation_risks: Reducing the delivery menu size may temporarily reduce order volume as regular delivery customers adjust
Price increases on delivery may meet initial resistance, though data shows delivery customers are generally less price-sensitive
Delivery-exclusive items require additional recipe development and testing investment
meta.notes_for_engine: This block depends on B128 (Delivery Commission Analysis) for the profitability data and B056 (menu engineering) for recipe costing. Activate once B128 has been completed and delivery margins are understood. Particularly urgent when signals indicate high delivery volume but poor delivery margins, high refund rates, or customer complaints about delivery food quality.

blocks.name: Direct Ordering Channel
blocks.purpose: Establishes a direct ordering capability — typically a branded online ordering system or app — that allows customers to order for delivery or collection without going through third-party platforms. This block aims to migrate delivery and collection revenue away from commission-heavy platforms toward a direct channel where the cafe retains the full margin.

detailed_objectives: Implement a direct online ordering system that is branded to the cafe and accessible via the website and social media
Create financial incentives for customers to order directly rather than through Deliveroo, Uber Eats, or Just Eat
Migrate at least 20-30% of current delivery and collection orders to the direct channel within 6 months
Own the customer data from direct orders, enabling marketing, loyalty programmes, and relationship building
Reduce total delivery commission cost as a percentage of delivery revenue through channel mix shift
secondary_objectives: Build a customer database with email addresses and order history for targeted marketing
Offer collection ordering as a convenience that bypasses queues during peak periods
Create a branded ordering experience that reinforces the cafe's identity rather than a platform's
Establish a channel that is fully within the cafe's control, immune to platform algorithm changes or commission increases

blocks.why_this_matters: Every order placed through a third-party delivery platform costs the cafe 15-35% in commission. On a 15 pound order, that is 2.25 to 5.25 pounds going to the platform rather than the business. If the same customer ordered directly, the cafe retains that entire amount minus only the payment processing fee (typically 1.5-2.5%). For a cafe processing 50 delivery orders per week at an average of 15 pounds, switching half of those to direct ordering could save 75-130 pounds per week — over 4,000-7,000 pounds per year. Beyond the margin benefit, direct ordering gives the cafe something platforms deliberately withhold: customer data. When a customer orders through Deliveroo, the cafe does not know their name, email address, or order history. The platform owns that relationship and can redirect the customer to a competitor at any time. When a customer orders directly, the cafe captures their contact information, builds an order history, and can market to them directly — birthday offers, loyalty rewards, new menu announcements. The technology for direct ordering has become remarkably accessible. Systems like Square Online, Flipdish, Slerp, and Orderswift offer branded ordering pages that integrate with existing POS systems, provide delivery logistics (either through the cafe's own delivery or through delivery partnerships), and cost a fraction of platform commissions — typically a flat monthly fee or 3-5% per transaction. The key challenge is not technology but customer behaviour: convincing customers who are used to ordering through platforms to switch to the direct channel. This requires deliberate incentivisation and consistent promotion.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Research direct ordering solutions appropriate to your size and budget. For most independent cafes, the leading options include Square Online (if already using Square POS), Flipdish, Slerp, and Orderswift. Key evaluation criteria: monthly cost, per-transaction fee, integration with your existing POS, ability to offer both delivery and collection, branded experience, and customer data access. Request demos from at least two providers. Week 2: Select and set up your direct ordering system. Upload your delivery menu (use the optimised delivery menu from B129), configure pricing, set delivery zones and minimum order values, and test the ordering process end to end. If you offer delivery, decide whether to use your own staff for delivery (only viable within a small radius) or integrate with a delivery logistics partner — several direct ordering platforms offer this at a lower cost than full platform commission. Create a simple, memorable ordering URL and set up a QR code that links directly to your ordering page.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Promote the direct ordering channel aggressively. Every dine-in customer should know it exists: table cards with QR codes, till receipts with the ordering URL, verbal reminders from staff ('Did you know you can order directly from us next time?'). Offer a clear incentive for direct ordering — this could be a 10% discount, free delivery over a certain value, a loyalty point bonus, or an item not available on the platforms. The incentive needs to be compelling enough to change behaviour but not so generous that it erodes the margin benefit. Add the direct ordering link to your Google Business Profile, Instagram bio, and any other online presence. Include it in email signatures and any customer communications. Monitor the channel mix weekly: what percentage of delivery and collection orders come through the direct channel versus platforms? Set targets for shifting this mix — moving from 0% to 20% direct within 3 months is achievable with consistent promotion. Train all staff to promote direct ordering naturally in customer interactions, especially at the till when customers pay for takeaway.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The direct ordering channel should now be generating meaningful revenue and the team should actively promote it.
