meta.summary_for_humans: This block develops a complete cold coffee programme — from cold brew production and iced espresso recipes to seasonal specials and merchandising. It turns cold drinks from a summer afterthought into a year-round revenue driver with strong margins.
implementation_risks: Cold brew demand is weather-dependent and difficult to predict, requiring flexible batch sizing
Staff may default to lazy iced drink preparation if recipes and standards are not enforced
Investment in dedicated cold brew equipment should be justified by demand data before committing capital
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate summer revenue dip, customer requests for cold options, or competitor cold drink programmes gaining traction. Pairs with B200 (Batch Brew & Filter) and B201 (Seasonal Specials Playbook).

blocks.name: Batch Brew & Filter Programme
blocks.purpose: Implements a batch brew and filter coffee programme that offers customers a high-quality, lower-priced black coffee option served quickly and consistently. This block covers equipment selection, recipe development, freshness management, coffee selection for filter, and the operational workflows needed to make batch brew a profitable and respected part of the menu.

detailed_objectives: Select and install appropriate batch brew equipment suited to the cafe's volume and format
Develop standardised batch brew recipes with defined dose, water volume, grind size, and brew time
Establish a freshness protocol ensuring batch brew is never served stale — maximum hold time and discard rules
Curate a rotating filter coffee selection that showcases different origins and flavour profiles
Train all staff to brew, monitor, and talk about batch brew with the same confidence as espresso
secondary_objectives: Increase speed of service during peak hours by offering an instant-pour black coffee option
Attract black coffee drinkers who may currently feel underserved by an espresso-only menu
Provide a lower price point option that drives footfall without cannibalising espresso sales
Use filter coffee as a gateway to educate customers about single-origin flavour diversity

blocks.why_this_matters: Espresso dominates UK cafe culture, but filter coffee is experiencing a significant resurgence, particularly in the specialty sector. Batch brew — high-quality filter coffee brewed in a commercial machine and held in an insulated airpot or server — combines the flavour clarity of pour-over with the speed and consistency of a push-button operation. A well-made batch brew takes 4-6 minutes to produce 2-3 litres (roughly 10-15 cups) and can be served in under 5 seconds per customer — dramatically faster than pulling espresso shots. This makes it invaluable during morning peak when every second counts. From a financial perspective, batch brew is extraordinarily profitable: a 2.5-litre batch using 150g of specialty coffee (cost approximately £4-5) produces 12 servings priced at £2.50-3.00 each, yielding revenue of £30-36 from £5 of input. The margin exceeds 85% before overheads. For customers, batch brew offers a different experience from espresso — lighter body, cleaner flavour, more nuanced acidity, and the ability to taste the character of a single-origin coffee without milk or sugar masking it. Many specialty cafes use batch brew as their primary vehicle for showcasing guest coffees and seasonal arrivals. The challenge is freshness: batch brew deteriorates within 30-60 minutes of brewing as oxidation and temperature decline alter the flavour. A stale batch brew is worse than no batch brew at all — it communicates carelessness. This block provides the systems to ensure freshness is never compromised.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Evaluate equipment options. For most cafes, a Moccamaster, Fetco, or Marco batch brewer is the standard choice, ranging from £300 (Moccamaster) to £1,500+ (Fetco/Marco) depending on volume needs. If budget is constrained, a large V60 or Chemex can serve as a manual batch brew solution for lower-volume sites, though these require more staff attention. Choose a brewer that matches expected demand — a 1.8-litre Moccamaster suits a small cafe selling 15-20 filter coffees per day; a Fetco with dual 3-litre servers suits higher-volume operations. Install the brewer in a visible, accessible position — customers should see it and smell it. Select the first filter coffee: choose something different from the espresso to give customers a reason to try it. A washed Ethiopian or Kenyan with bright acidity contrasts nicely against a typical Brazilian or Colombian espresso blend. Week 2: Develop the recipe. A standard starting ratio is 60g of coffee per litre of water, ground medium (between espresso and French press). Brew into an insulated server (airpot or thermal carafe). Taste the first batch as a team and adjust grind size to optimise extraction — if it tastes sour and thin, grind finer; if bitter and harsh, grind coarser. Once dialled in, record the recipe on a Batch Brew Recipe Card: coffee name, dose, grind setting (by number on your grinder), water volume, and target brew time. Set a maximum hold time of 45 minutes in a thermal server, or 90 minutes on a hot plate (though thermal servers are strongly preferred for quality). After the hold time, discard and rebrew. Price batch brew 20-30% below espresso-based drinks to encourage trial.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Standardise the production schedule. Analyse peak hour demand to determine how many batches are needed and when. Typically: first batch brewed 10 minutes before opening, second batch mid-morning, and possibly a third for the lunchtime rush. On quiet afternoons, suspend batch brew rather than letting it sit. Create a Batch Brew Log tracking: brew time, coffee used, grind setting, discard time, and cups sold per batch. This data reveals demand patterns and waste rates. Introduce a rotating filter coffee programme: change the filter coffee weekly or fortnightly, selecting from different origins and processes to keep the offering fresh and educational. Create simple tasting note cards displayed next to the batch brewer so customers know what they are drinking — origin, process, roast level, and two or three flavour descriptors in plain language. Train all staff on the batch brew workflow, including when to brew, how to check freshness (taste a small pour before serving — if it tastes flat or papery, discard immediately), and how to describe the current coffee to customers. Establish a waste tracking metric: batch brew waste should stay below 15% of total production. If waste is consistently high, reduce batch sizes or frequency.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Batch brew should now be an established, respected part of the menu with its own following. Introduce pour-over or manual brew options alongside batch brew for customers who want a single-cup experience — V60, AeroPress, or Kalita Wave brewed to order. This two-tier approach serves both speed-focused and experience-focused customers. Develop a coffee subscription or loyalty programme specifically for filter coffee, encouraging daily batch brew customers. Host monthly filter coffee tasting events where customers can try the next month's selection before it launches. Track filter coffee as a separate category in sales reporting to understand its contribution to revenue, average transaction value, and customer acquisition. Collaborate with the roaster to source exclusive or limited micro-lot coffees for the filter programme that are unavailable elsewhere locally. Display origin information prominently — a map, farm photos, processing details — to educate customers and differentiate from chain filter coffee. Conduct quarterly recipe reviews, optimising extraction using a refractometer (TDS meter) to ensure consistency as coffees change.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Evaluate and select a batch brew machine suited to the cafe's volume and budget
Select the first filter coffee — choose a contrasting profile to the house espresso
Develop and record the batch brew recipe (dose, grind, water volume, brew time)
Set a maximum hold time (45 minutes in thermal server) and enforce discard rules
Add batch brew to the menu board with price and a brief tasting description
Train all staff on the brewing and service procedure
Position the brewer visibly so customers can see and smell the fresh coffee

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a Batch Brew Production Log tracking brew times, discard times, and cups sold per batch
Establish a production schedule aligned to peak demand periods
Introduce a rotating filter coffee programme with weekly or fortnightly changes
Design and display tasting note cards next to the batch brewer for each coffee
Train all staff on freshness checking, waste management, and customer coffee descriptions
Track waste rates weekly with a target below 15% of production
Calculate cost-per-serve and confirm pricing delivers target margin

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce pour-over or manual brew-to-order options alongside batch brew
Launch a filter coffee loyalty or subscription programme
Host monthly filter coffee tasting events for customers
Track filter coffee as a separate sales category in reporting
Source exclusive micro-lot coffees for the filter programme
Display origin stories and farm information prominently for each coffee
Conduct quarterly recipe optimisation using a refractometer for TDS measurement

tools.description: Laminated recipe card for each coffee showing dose, grind setting, water volume, brew time, maximum hold time, and flavour profile notes. Positioned at the brew station for reference during service.
Daily log tracking each batch brewed: time brewed, coffee used, grind setting, discard time, cups served, and waste volume. Used to optimise production scheduling and minimise waste.
Planning template for the rotating filter coffee programme: scheduled coffees by week, origin and process details, order quantities, roast dates, and changeover dates to ensure seamless transitions.
A5 card template displayed next to the batch brewer showing the current coffee's name, origin, farm or cooperative, process, roast profile, and three flavour descriptors in accessible language.
Spreadsheet calculating batch brew cost-per-serve including coffee cost, water, filters, and waste factor, with margin analysis and recommended pricing to achieve profitability targets.

failure_modes: Batch brew sits past its hold time and is served stale, creating a terrible first impression that deters repeat orders
Production schedule is not aligned to demand, resulting in either frequent stockouts (frustrating customers) or chronic overproduction (wasting coffee)
Staff do not taste-check batches before serving, allowing sub-standard brews to reach customers
Filter coffee is not rotated or described, making it feel like an anonymous afterthought rather than a curated offering
kpis: Batch brew waste rate below 15% of total production volume
Filter coffee sales reaching at least 10% of total drink sales within 6 months
Zero stale batches served — 100% compliance with maximum hold time and discard protocol
Filter coffee rotation maintained with a new coffee introduced at least every fortnight
constraints: Batch brew equipment requires counter space and a power outlet near the service area
Freshness management demands discipline — the 45-minute discard rule must be non-negotiable even when waste feels painful
Filter-roasted coffees may need to be sourced separately from espresso roasts if the roaster does not offer omni-roast profiles

time_load.L1: 3 hours (equipment setup, recipe development, first batches, staff training)
time_load.L2: 6 hours (production log setup, rotation planning, tasting card design, cost analysis)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (rotation management, data analysis, tasting events, recipe optimisation)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily freshness compliance check by shift lead. Weekly production log review by head barista. Monthly waste rate and sales analysis. Quarterly recipe optimisation and rotation planning session.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block implements a batch brew and filter coffee programme that delivers fast, consistent, high-quality black coffee with exceptional margins. It covers equipment, recipes, freshness protocols, rotation planning, and staff training to make filter coffee a respected revenue stream.
implementation_risks: Stale batch brew will actively damage the cafe's reputation — freshness discipline must be absolute
Some customers may perceive filter coffee as 'cheap' — presentation and origin storytelling counter this
Equipment failure mid-service leaves no batch brew backup — a manual brew method should be available as fallback
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate peak-hour bottlenecks, demand for black coffee, or desire to showcase single-origin diversity. Pairs with B199 (Cold Drink Programme) for iced filter and B198 (Cupping & Quality Benchmarking) for coffee selection.

blocks.name: Seasonal Specials Playbook
blocks.purpose: Creates a structured playbook for planning, developing, launching, and retiring seasonal specials across coffee, food, and non-coffee drinks. This block ensures the cafe maintains menu freshness and customer excitement through a disciplined calendar of limited-time offerings, without overwhelming operations or creating waste from untested recipes.

detailed_objectives: Build an annual seasonal specials calendar with planned launch windows, themes, and retirement dates
Establish a recipe development and testing process that validates specials before they reach the menu
Create marketing and in-store merchandising templates that generate excitement for each seasonal launch
Define operational guidelines so specials can be executed by any trained team member without supervision
Track sales performance of each special to build an evidence base for future seasonal planning
secondary_objectives: Drive social media engagement through photogenic, shareable seasonal offerings
Create urgency and repeat visits through limited-time scarcity
Test new flavour concepts and ingredients that may graduate to the permanent menu
Respond to competitor seasonal launches with distinctive alternatives

blocks.why_this_matters: A static menu is a slowly dying menu. Customers who visit daily or weekly crave novelty — they love their regular order but are delighted when something new catches their eye. Seasonal specials satisfy this craving while creating commercial urgency: a drink available only for six weeks drives impulse purchases that a permanent menu item never does. The psychology of scarcity is powerful — people value what is temporary more than what is always available. The major chains understand this intimately: their seasonal launches (pumpkin spice, gingerbread, iced summer drinks) are meticulously planned months in advance, supported by national marketing campaigns, and generate measurable revenue spikes. Independent cafes can compete not with marketing budgets but with creativity, quality, and authenticity. A seasonal lavender honey latte made with local honey and house-made lavender syrup has a story that no chain can replicate. However, seasonal specials fail when they are improvised rather than planned. An untested recipe launched on a Monday morning creates operational chaos — staff do not know how to make it, ingredients run out by Wednesday, the flavour is not balanced, and the special quietly disappears without anyone noticing. This block provides the structure to make seasonal specials a strategic advantage rather than an operational headache: plan in advance, test rigorously, launch with impact, track performance, and retire gracefully.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Map the year into four seasonal windows aligned to customer behaviour and ingredient availability. A typical UK cafe calendar: Spring (March-May) — lighter, floral, citrus themes; Summer (June-August) — cold drinks, fruity, refreshing; Autumn (September-November) — warming spices, caramel, apple, pumpkin; Winter (December-February) — indulgent, chocolate, gingerbread, festive. For each window, plan 1-2 drink specials and optionally 1 food special. Start with the current or upcoming season. Week 2: Develop the first seasonal special. Choose a concept that uses readily available ingredients and does not require new equipment. For example, an autumn spiced latte using a house-made syrup (cinnamon, nutmeg, clove, brown sugar simmered in water) added to a standard latte. Make the syrup, test the recipe with the team, adjust sweetness and spice levels, and document the final recipe on a recipe card. Calculate the cost-per-serve including syrup ingredients, coffee, milk, and any garnish (cinnamon stick, star anise). Price at a 30-50% premium over the standard latte to reflect the special nature and additional ingredients. Create a simple chalkboard announcement or A4 poster for in-store promotion.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Build a Seasonal Specials Playbook document that becomes the living reference for all future launches. The playbook should contain: the annual calendar with launch and retirement dates, a recipe development process (concept, first test, team tasting, refinement, final recipe card), a launch checklist (staff training, ingredient ordering, signage, social media content, till button setup), an in-season management protocol (daily prep checklist, ingredient reorder triggers, customer feedback collection), and a retirement process (last day communication, remaining ingredient use-up, performance review). Create a Seasonal Special Recipe Development Template that standardises how new specials are conceived and tested: concept name, inspiration, target flavour profile, ingredient list, method, cost-per-serve, pricing, allergen information, and presentation specification including cup or plate, garnish, and photo reference. Develop a library of house-made syrups, sauces, and bases that can be prepared in advance and stored — vanilla bean syrup, salted caramel sauce, matcha paste, chai concentrate. These become building blocks that make seasonal specials faster to develop and more consistent in execution. Track each special's daily sales in a Seasonal Special Performance Log.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The seasonal specials programme should now generate anticipation — regular customers should ask what is coming next. Build pre-launch hype through social media teasers, email newsletter previews, and in-store countdowns. Involve the team in recipe development — run quarterly brainstorming sessions where anyone can pitch a seasonal concept. The best ideas come from baristas who see what customers enjoy and what ingredients are trending. Develop a Seasonal Specials Archive documenting every special ever launched: recipe, performance data, customer feedback, and lessons learned. This becomes an invaluable reference — the autumn spiced latte recipe that sold brilliantly two years ago can be revived or evolved. Collaborate with local suppliers for seasonal ingredients: a local beekeeper for spring honey, a nearby farm for summer berries, a local bakery for winter mince pie specials. These partnerships add authenticity and story. Consider a seasonal specials loyalty programme — try all four seasonal specials in a year and earn a reward. Analyse annual seasonal data to refine timing, pricing, and concept selection year on year.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Map the year into four seasonal windows with themes and approximate launch dates
Choose a concept for the current or upcoming seasonal special
Develop and test the recipe with the team, adjusting until balanced
Document the final recipe on a recipe card with full cost-per-serve analysis
Create in-store signage announcing the seasonal special
Train all staff on preparation and how to describe the special to customers
Set the price at a premium reflecting seasonal exclusivity and ingredient cost

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Build a Seasonal Specials Playbook document with calendar, processes, and templates
Create a standardised Recipe Development Template for all future specials
Develop a library of house-made syrups, sauces, and bases for rapid recipe development
Establish a launch checklist covering training, ordering, signage, social media, and till setup
Create a Seasonal Special Performance Log tracking daily sales per special
Define retirement protocols: last day communication, ingredient use-up, and performance review
Design social media content templates for consistent seasonal special promotion

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Build pre-launch hype through social media teasers and email newsletter previews
Run quarterly team brainstorming sessions for seasonal special concepts
Develop a Seasonal Specials Archive documenting all past specials with performance data
Collaborate with local suppliers for seasonal ingredients that add story and authenticity
Analyse annual seasonal data to refine timing, pricing, and concept selection
Consider a seasonal specials loyalty programme to drive engagement across the full year

tools.description: Year-view calendar showing four seasonal windows with planned specials, launch dates, retirement dates, ingredient ordering deadlines, and promotional milestones for the entire year.
Standardised template for developing new specials: concept name, inspiration, ingredients, method, cost-per-serve, allergens, presentation spec, and sign-off by manager before launch.

Pre-launch checklist covering: recipe card completed, all staff trained, ingredients ordered and received, till button created, signage installed, social media content scheduled, and opening day quality check.
Daily sales tracker for each seasonal special showing units sold, revenue, cost-of-goods, margin, and customer feedback notes. Used for post-season performance review and future planning.
Historical archive of every seasonal special launched, including recipe, cost data, sales performance, customer feedback summary, and lessons learned. Referenced when planning future seasons.

failure_modes: Specials are launched without adequate testing, resulting in inconsistent quality and poor customer reception
Ingredients for the seasonal special run out within days because ordering was not planned against expected demand
The special is never officially retired, lingering on the menu until ingredients are exhausted, losing its limited-time appeal
Staff are not trained on the new recipe, leading to slow preparation and variable quality during the launch period
kpis: Each seasonal special achieving at least 10% of total drink sales during its launch week
100% of seasonal specials launched with all checklist items completed on launch day
Seasonal specials contributing a measurable revenue uplift compared to equivalent non-seasonal periods
Customer feedback score for seasonal specials averaging 4+ out of 5 stars
constraints: Recipe development requires lead time — plan at least 4 weeks before each seasonal launch date
House-made syrups and sauces require food safety consideration: shelf life, storage, allergen labelling
Seasonal ingredient availability may vary by year — have backup options for key components

time_load.L1: 4 hours (calendar planning, first recipe development and testing, staff training)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (playbook development, template creation, syrup library, launch system setup)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/quarter (brainstorming, archive maintenance, supplier collaboration, data analysis)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily sales check during first week of each seasonal launch by manager. Weekly performance review during the special's run. Post-season review within one week of retirement. Annual planning session in January for the full year's seasonal calendar.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block creates a disciplined playbook for seasonal specials — from annual planning and recipe development through launch, in-season management, and retirement. It ensures specials drive excitement and revenue rather than operational chaos.
implementation_risks: Over-ambitious seasonal menus strain operations — start with 1-2 specials per season and expand gradually
Seasonal specials that are too similar to permanent menu items cannibalise rather than complement sales
Social media hype without delivery disappoints — ensure the product matches the promotion
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate menu staleness, declining repeat visit rates, competitor seasonal launches, or customer requests for novelty. Pairs with B199 (Cold Drink Programme) for summer specials and B054 (menu structure).

blocks.name: Bakery Partnership Assessment
blocks.purpose: Provides a structured framework for evaluating, selecting, onboarding, and managing bakery partnerships that supply pastries, cakes, bread, and baked goods to the cafe. This block ensures that bakery partners deliver consistent quality, reliable logistics, competitive pricing, and food safety compliance while aligning with the cafe's brand positioning and customer expectations.

detailed_objectives: Define the cafe's bakery requirements: product range, quality standards, delivery schedule, and volume
Evaluate potential bakery partners against a structured scorecard covering quality, reliability, price, and values
Establish contractual terms including pricing, delivery windows, minimum orders, returns policy, and exclusivity
Implement quality control processes for incoming bakery deliveries including inspection and feedback protocols
Build a long-term partnership dynamic with regular reviews, co-development opportunities, and mutual growth
secondary_objectives: Reduce food waste through accurate demand forecasting shared with the bakery partner
Develop exclusive or co-branded products that differentiate the cafe from competitors
Ensure allergen information is complete, accurate, and legally compliant for all bakery items
Maintain a backup bakery relationship for business continuity in case the primary partner fails

blocks.why_this_matters: For most cafes, baked goods represent 20-35% of total revenue and are the primary food offering alongside coffee. The quality of a croissant or a slice of cake communicates as much about the cafe's standards as the coffee does — a stale pastry next to a perfectly extracted espresso creates a jarring disconnect that undermines the entire experience. Yet bakery partnerships are frequently managed informally: the owner knows someone who bakes, orders are placed by text message, deliveries arrive whenever convenient, and quality assessment consists of an occasional taste. This informality creates vulnerability. A bakery partner that delivers inconsistently — sometimes at 6am, sometimes at 9am after the morning rush has peaked — costs the cafe sales every late morning. One that varies portion sizes or recipe quality forces the cafe to absorb customer complaints about products it did not make. One without proper allergen documentation exposes the cafe to serious legal liability under UK food labelling regulations (Natasha's Law requires full ingredient labelling on all pre-packaged for direct sale food). The stakes are high: a single allergen incident can result in prosecution, closure, and civil liability. A structured bakery partnership assessment process protects the business commercially, legally, and reputationally while building a collaborative relationship that benefits both parties.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Define bakery requirements. List every baked product currently sold or planned: croissants, pain au chocolat, sourdough bread, banana bread, brownies, cookies, seasonal cakes, savoury pastries. For each product, define: required quality standard (artisan, mid-range, or basic), daily quantity needed (weekday versus weekend), required delivery time (must arrive before 7am for a cafe opening at 7:30am), shelf life expectation, and price ceiling. Assess whether the cafe needs a single full-range bakery partner or multiple specialists (e.g., one for viennoiserie, one for cakes, one for bread). Week 2: If switching or adding a bakery partner, identify candidates through local bakery directories, trade recommendations, social media research, and wholesale bakery platforms. Request samples from 2-3 shortlisted bakeries. Conduct a structured tasting session with the team: score each product on appearance, texture, flavour, freshness, and portion size. Document scores on a Bakery Partner Evaluation Scorecard. For the current partner, conduct the same evaluation to establish a baseline. Request allergen documentation, food safety certificates (minimum Level 2 HACCP), and liability insurance details from every candidate.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Formalise the partnership. Agree terms in writing covering: product list with specifications, pricing per unit (review frequency — typically annually with quarterly adjustment for ingredient cost changes), delivery schedule (days of week, time window, late delivery penalties), minimum order quantities and lead times for changes, returns and credit policy for damaged or substandard deliveries, allergen and labelling responsibilities, and exclusivity terms if applicable. Create an Incoming Delivery Inspection Checklist: every delivery should be checked against the order (correct items, correct quantities), inspected for quality (visual check, temperature for chilled items), and signed off. Any issues should be photographed and reported to the bakery immediately using a standardised Delivery Issue Report. Establish a weekly order planning process: review the previous week's sales data by product, adjust the coming week's order to match demand, and communicate changes to the bakery by the agreed cut-off day. Track bakery-related food waste separately — products unsold and discarded at end of day — and use this data to fine-tune ordering.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Move the bakery relationship from supplier-customer to genuine partnership. Schedule quarterly review meetings to discuss: quality trends, delivery performance, sales data, new product development ideas, and seasonal planning. Share sales data with the bakery — they benefit from understanding which products sell best and can adjust production accordingly. Co-develop exclusive products: a signature cookie or pastry that carries the cafe's name, developed jointly with the bakery and available nowhere else. This creates a compelling point of difference. Conduct annual partner reviews using the original evaluation scorecard to ensure standards are maintained and to benchmark against alternative suppliers. Develop a contingency plan: identify a backup bakery partner who could supply at short notice if the primary partner has a production failure, equipment breakdown, or staffing crisis. Test the backup relationship annually with a small trial order. Explore seasonal collaboration — Easter hot cross buns, Christmas stollen, summer fruit tarts — planned jointly three months in advance.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Define bakery product requirements: range, quality standard, quantity, delivery time, and price ceiling
Evaluate the current bakery partner against a structured scorecard or identify 2-3 new candidates
Request and taste samples from shortlisted bakeries, scoring on quality criteria
Collect allergen documentation, food safety certificates, and insurance details from partners
Select the preferred partner based on scorecard results and conduct initial negotiations
Agree delivery schedule that ensures products arrive before the cafe opens
Confirm allergen information for every product and update menu labelling accordingly

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Formalise partnership terms in a written agreement covering pricing, delivery, returns, and allergens
Create an Incoming Delivery Inspection Checklist for daily use
Establish a standardised Delivery Issue Report process with photo documentation
Implement a weekly order planning process based on sales data and demand forecasting
Track bakery product waste separately to inform ordering adjustments
Set up a cost tracking system to monitor bakery spend against revenue
Schedule the first quarterly partnership review meeting

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Hold quarterly review meetings with the bakery partner covering quality, performance, and planning
Co-develop exclusive products that differentiate the cafe
Share sales data with the partner to enable mutual demand planning
Conduct annual partner evaluation against the original scorecard and benchmark alternatives
Establish a backup bakery partner for business continuity
Plan seasonal product collaborations at least three months in advance

deliverables: Quarterly Partner Review agenda and minutes template

tools.description: Structured evaluation matrix scoring bakery candidates across product quality, range, pricing, delivery reliability, food safety credentials, allergen documentation, insurance, and brand alignment.

Daily checklist for inspecting bakery deliveries: order accuracy, quantity verification, visual quality check, temperature check for chilled items, packaging integrity, and sign-off with issue flagging.
Standardised report for documenting delivery problems: issue type (late, missing items, quality failure, damaged goods), photo evidence, immediate action taken, and credit or replacement requested.
Planning template matching previous week's sales data to upcoming order quantities, with adjustments for weather, events, and seasonal factors. Reduces over-ordering and minimises waste.
Agenda and minutes template for quarterly bakery partner reviews: quality performance, delivery metrics, pricing review, new product pipeline, seasonal planning, and any issues requiring resolution.

failure_modes: Bakery deliveries arrive after the morning rush, meaning the cafe has no pastries during its highest-footfall period
Allergen documentation is incomplete or outdated, exposing the cafe to legal liability under Natasha's Law
Quality inconsistency goes unchallenged because there is no inspection process — customers experience variable product quality
Over-reliance on a single bakery partner with no contingency means any supply disruption leaves the cafe with empty display cases
kpis: Bakery deliveries arriving within the agreed time window on at least 95% of occasions
Delivery quality issues flagged and resolved within 24 hours in 100% of cases
Bakery product waste rate below 10% of total bakery order value
Full allergen documentation on file and up-to-date for 100% of bakery products at all times
constraints: Allergen documentation and labelling compliance under Natasha's Law is non-negotiable and legally required
Delivery timing is critical — a bakery that cannot deliver before opening time is unsuitable regardless of product quality
Switching bakery partners mid-contract may have financial or relationship implications that need managing

time_load.L1: 6 hours (requirements definition, candidate sourcing, tasting sessions, initial negotiation)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (agreement formalisation, checklist creation, order planning system, waste tracking setup)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/quarter (review meetings, performance analysis, co-development planning)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily delivery inspection at point of receipt. Weekly order planning review against sales data. Monthly waste and cost analysis. Quarterly formal partnership review meeting. Annual full evaluation and benchmark against alternatives.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block provides a structured approach to evaluating, selecting, and managing bakery partnerships. It covers tasting evaluations, contractual terms, delivery inspection, allergen compliance, and ongoing partnership development to ensure consistently excellent baked goods.
implementation_risks: Changing bakery partners can temporarily disrupt supply — plan a transition period with overlapping orders
Small artisan bakeries may struggle with the formality of written agreements — frame them as mutual protection, not bureaucracy
Allergen compliance responsibility ultimately sits with the cafe regardless of what the bakery provides — always verify
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate bakery quality complaints, delivery reliability issues, allergen compliance gaps, or excessive bakery waste. Pairs with B109 (food supplier management) and food safety blocks.

blocks.name: Drive-Through Workflow
blocks.purpose: Designs and optimises the complete drive-through workflow from order point to window handoff, ensuring speed of service, order accuracy, and a positive customer experience within the unique constraints of vehicle-based service. This block addresses lane management, communication systems, menu board design, staffing positions, and performance metrics specific to drive-through cafe operations.

detailed_objectives: Map and optimise the end-to-end drive-through journey: approach, order, payment, collection, and departure
Define staffing positions and role responsibilities for each station in the drive-through workflow
Establish target service times and order accuracy standards with real-time monitoring
Design effective menu board presentation that enables fast, confident ordering from vehicles
Implement communication systems between order point, preparation area, and handoff window
secondary_objectives: Reduce queue abandonment by managing visible queue length and perceived wait times
Maximise average transaction value through effective upselling at the order point
Minimise order errors that are costlier to correct in drive-through than dine-in (no easy re-serve)
Ensure drive-through operations do not compromise quality standards or food safety

blocks.why_this_matters: Drive-through cafes operate under fundamentally different pressures from sit-in venues. The customer is in a vehicle, often in a hurry, with limited patience for delays. The average drive-through customer expects their order within 3-4 minutes of reaching the order point — exceed this consistently and they will switch to a competitor. Unlike a dine-in customer who can be engaged with ambience and conversation while waiting, a drive-through customer experiences wait time as pure frustration compounded by the inability to leave the queue once committed. Every car in the queue is visible to approaching traffic — a long queue can either signal popularity (positive) or slowness (negative), and the tipping point between these perceptions is surprisingly thin. Order accuracy is more critical in drive-through than any other format because errors are discovered after departure, meaning the customer cannot easily return for correction. A wrong order in drive-through creates a disproportionately negative experience and often a permanent customer loss. The operational choreography is complex: someone takes the order (either through a speaker system or at a window), someone makes the drinks, someone assembles the food, someone processes payment, and someone hands out the order — all potentially happening simultaneously for multiple cars. Without clear role definition, communication protocols, and workflow design, this choreography breaks down during peak periods, resulting in bottlenecks, errors, and frustrated customers. Drive-through coffee has grown substantially in the UK market, driven by commuter convenience and post-pandemic habits, making it essential that operations are optimised for this format.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Map the current drive-through workflow. Stand at each station and observe during a peak hour — document every step from the moment a car enters the lane to departure. Note: where do bottlenecks occur? Is it at order-taking, payment, preparation, or handoff? Time 20 consecutive transactions from order to departure and calculate the average. Identify the constraint — the single slowest step that determines the maximum throughput. In most drive-through cafes, the constraint is either espresso preparation time (limiting drinks output) or a combined order-and-payment window (doubling the time each car spends stationary). Week 2: Address the primary bottleneck. If espresso preparation is the constraint, consider pre-batching common orders during peak (having flat whites ready to pour), adding a second grinder, or simplifying the peak-hour menu. If a single window handles both ordering and payment, evaluate whether a speaker-based pre-order system could separate these functions. Establish clear role assignments: during peak, each station should have a dedicated person — order taker, barista, food assembler, payment handler, and window runner. During quieter periods, roles can be combined. Create a simple Drive-Through Position Guide listing each role, its responsibilities, and the handoff points between roles. Post it visibly in the preparation area.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Install or upgrade communication systems. A reliable headset system between the order point and preparation area is essential — the barista must hear the order in real time and begin preparation before the car reaches the window. If headsets are not feasible, a screen-based order relay system works, but adds latency. Design (or redesign) the drive-through menu board for speed: the most popular items should be the largest and most visible, with clear pricing. Limit visible options to 15-20 items maximum — a cluttered menu board slows ordering decisions. Use numbering or meal-deal groupings to simplify verbal ordering. Establish standard service time targets: order-to-handoff of 3 minutes for a single drink, 4 minutes for a multi-item order. Track times using a simple stopwatch log or, if available, a timer system integrated with the POS. Create a Drive-Through Performance Log capturing: transactions per hour, average service time, order accuracy rate (track errors through customer complaints and window corrections), and queue length at 15-minute intervals during peak. Develop an upselling script for the order point — concise, natural prompts that add value: 'Would you like a pastry with that?' or 'We have a special today on iced lattes.' Train order takers to upsell without slowing throughput.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Drive-through operations should now run with the precision of a production line during peak and the warmth of personal service during quieter periods. Introduce a pre-ordering capability — a mobile app or phone-ahead system where customers place and pay for orders before arriving, reducing window time to a simple handoff. Analyse performance data weekly: identify which days and hours have the longest service times, correlate with staffing levels, and adjust rotas to match demand patterns. Test peak-hour menu simplification — offering a reduced menu of the 10 fastest-to-prepare items during the busiest 90 minutes can dramatically increase throughput. Implement mystery shopper visits specifically for the drive-through experience — time, accuracy, friendliness, product quality, and presentation assessed from the customer's perspective. Explore dual-lane configurations if physical space and volume warrant it. Celebrate and reward the team for hitting service time and accuracy targets — drive-through excellence is a team sport. Share performance dashboards weekly so everyone understands how the operation is performing.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Map the complete drive-through workflow from lane entry to departure
Time 20 consecutive transactions to establish a baseline average service time
Identify the primary bottleneck in the workflow
Address the bottleneck with an immediate operational adjustment
Define and assign drive-through station roles for peak periods
Create a Drive-Through Position Guide and post it visibly
Brief all staff on role responsibilities and handoff protocols

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Install or upgrade headset or screen-based communication between order point and preparation area
Redesign the menu board for clarity, speed, and upselling effectiveness
Establish target service times (3 minutes single drink, 4 minutes multi-item)
Create a Drive-Through Performance Log tracking service time, accuracy, and throughput
Develop and train order-takers on upselling scripts that do not slow service
Implement a queue monitoring system to manage peak period capacity
Review and adjust role assignments based on performance data

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce pre-ordering capability via mobile app or phone-ahead system
Analyse weekly performance data and adjust rotas to match demand patterns
Test peak-hour menu simplification to increase throughput during the busiest periods
Implement mystery shopper assessments for drive-through experience quality
Share weekly performance dashboards with the entire team
Celebrate service time and accuracy achievements to reinforce operational excellence

tools.description: Visual process diagram showing every step in the drive-through journey: approach, order point, payment, preparation, assembly, handoff, and departure, with role assignments and timing targets at each stage.

Role-by-role guide for each drive-through station: order taker, barista, food assembler, payment handler, and window runner. Covers responsibilities, communication protocols, and peak-period escalation.
Hourly tracker capturing transactions per hour, average service time, order errors, queue length at peak intervals, and upsell success rate. Feeds into weekly performance review.
Design specification for the drive-through menu board: item hierarchy, pricing layout, font sizes for vehicle-distance readability, meal deal groupings, and seasonal special placement.

Assessment form for mystery shopper visits scoring: queue wait time, greeting quality, order accuracy, service time, product quality and temperature, presentation, upsell attempt, and farewell interaction.

failure_modes: Peak-hour bottleneck goes unaddressed, causing queue times to exceed customer tolerance and driving cars away
Communication breakdown between order point and preparation area leads to wrong drinks being made and wasted
Staff roles are not clearly defined, resulting in confusion and duplicated effort during busy periods
Menu board is cluttered or poorly lit, slowing ordering decisions and increasing time per car at the order point
kpis: Average order-to-handoff time of 3 minutes or less for single-drink orders
Order accuracy rate of 98% or above measured through error tracking
Peak-hour throughput of at least 30 transactions per hour with full staffing
Queue abandonment rate below 5% during peak periods
constraints: Drive-through layout is physically fixed — workflow optimisation must work within existing lane and window configuration
Headset or communication systems require investment and staff training to use effectively in a noisy environment
Menu board changes require signage production which has lead time and cost implications

time_load.L1: 4 hours (workflow observation, timing study, bottleneck analysis, position guide creation)
time_load.L2: 10 hours (communication system setup, menu board redesign, performance tracking, upsell training)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/month (data analysis, rota optimisation, mystery shopper review, team performance meetings)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily service time spot-check by shift lead. Weekly performance log review by manager. Monthly throughput and accuracy analysis. Quarterly mystery shopper assessment and workflow optimisation review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block optimises every aspect of the drive-through experience — from order point to handoff window. It covers workflow mapping, role definition, communication systems, menu board design, and performance tracking to deliver fast, accurate, friendly service from a vehicle lane.
implementation_risks: Physical lane constraints limit workflow redesign options — focus on process and people improvements first
Headset systems require maintenance and battery management — always have backup communication methods
Speed targets must not compromise quality or food safety — never sacrifice drink standards for throughput
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate only for drive_through venue type. Core operational block for this format. Signals: long queue times, order accuracy complaints, low throughput during peak, or staff confusion about roles.

blocks.name: Kiosk Operations
blocks.purpose: Establishes the operational framework for running a coffee kiosk or compact format venue, addressing the unique challenges of limited space, reduced equipment, minimal staff, and high-footfall locations. This block covers workflow design within tight footprints, condensed menu strategies, efficient stock management, and the operational discipline required when there is no back-of-house to fall back on.

detailed_objectives: Design an optimised workflow for a 1-2 person operation within a compact kiosk footprint
Develop a focused, high-margin menu suited to kiosk service speed and equipment constraints
Establish stock management protocols for limited storage capacity with daily or near-daily deliveries
Define opening, operating, and closing procedures specific to kiosk environments
Implement quality standards that match full-size cafe expectations despite operational constraints
secondary_objectives: Maximise revenue per square metre through menu engineering and efficient space utilisation
Maintain food safety compliance in an environment without traditional kitchen facilities
Manage weather exposure, power supply, and water access challenges common to outdoor kiosks
Create a customer experience that feels premium despite the compact format

blocks.why_this_matters: Kiosk coffee operations are booming across the UK — from railway station concourses and shopping centre atriums to converted shipping containers and purpose-built street kiosks. The format offers dramatically lower rent and fit-out costs compared to traditional cafes, making it an attractive entry point for new operators and an efficient expansion vehicle for established brands. However, the constraints are severe: a typical kiosk is 3-8 square metres, staffed by one or two people, with limited power supply, no running hot water in some cases, minimal storage, and zero separation between preparation and service areas. Every movement matters — there is no room for wasted steps, poorly organised stations, or an overly complex menu that bogs down service. A kiosk that serves 200 drinks per day from a 5-square-metre space must achieve extraordinary efficiency per square metre and per staff member. The menu must be ruthlessly focused: offer the 10-15 items that sell best and execute them brilliantly, rather than attempting the full range of a sit-down cafe. Stock management is a daily discipline — there is nowhere to store a week's worth of milk, so deliveries must be frequent and precisely sized. Food safety is equally demanding: without a separate food prep area, the entire kiosk is the production zone, meaning hygiene standards must be maintained constantly with no retreat to a 'back of house' for cleanup. The operators who succeed in kiosk format are those who treat the constraint as a design challenge, not a limitation — every inch planned, every second of workflow choreographed, every product earning its place on the menu.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit the kiosk space and map the workflow. Measure every surface, shelf, and storage area. Draw a floor plan showing equipment placement: espresso machine, grinder, fridge, till, water supply, bin, and cup storage. Stand in the serving position and reach — can you touch the machine, the grinder, the milk fridge, and the till without taking more than one step? If not, rearrange. The principle is zero wasted movement: the most frequently repeated actions (pulling shots, steaming milk, serving) should require the least movement. Identify and eliminate any step that involves turning your back to the customer or walking away from the service counter. Week 2: Define the kiosk menu. Start with the items that account for 80% of sales — typically: espresso, flat white, latte, cappuccino, americano, hot chocolate, and 2-3 cold drinks. Add a maximum of 3-4 food items that require no on-site preparation: wrapped pastries from a bakery partner, pre-packaged sandwiches, or energy bars. Every menu item must be producible within the kiosk's equipment constraints and storable within available refrigeration. Remove anything that slows service disproportionately or requires ingredients that spoil before they are used. Calculate the maximum number of drinks per hour the setup can produce — this is the kiosk's theoretical throughput ceiling. Create a Kiosk Opening and Closing Checklist tailored to the specific site.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop Standard Operating Procedures for the kiosk covering: daily opening sequence (equipment startup, stock check, milk preparation, till float, cleaning), peak-hour workflow (order queuing, drink batching for efficiency, customer communication during waits), and closing procedure (equipment shutdown, stock count, waste recording, cleaning, security). Establish a daily stock management system: count stock at close, calculate tomorrow's requirement based on the day's sales and the next day's expected footfall (adjust for day of week, weather, and events), and place orders for next-day delivery. Kiosks cannot afford to run out of milk at 2pm — ordering precision is critical. Implement food safety protocols specific to the kiosk format: handwashing procedure (especially challenging in kiosks without hot running water — antibacterial gel and wet wipes as supplements, not replacements), temperature monitoring for the fridge, cleaning schedule for surfaces that double as preparation and service areas, and waste management when there may be no mains drainage. Create a Kiosk Maintenance Log tracking equipment condition, cleaning, and any site issues (leaks, power problems, drainage). Train staff on the specific demands of solo kiosk operation — they must be comfortable running the entire service alone, managing queues, handling payments, and maintaining quality simultaneously.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The kiosk should now operate with visible efficiency that impresses customers. Introduce pre-ordering — a simple QR code or app-based system where regular customers can order before arriving, reducing queue time for everyone. Analyse sales data to identify hour-by-hour demand patterns and adjust staffing: if 60% of sales happen between 7:30-9:30am, ensure a second person is rostered for those hours even if the rest of the day is solo operation. Develop a rain and weather contingency plan — outdoor kiosks see dramatic demand shifts with weather, so have protocols for adjusting stock orders and staffing based on forecasts. Build a regular customer programme: in a high-footfall kiosk, regulars are the revenue backbone. Know their names, remember their orders, and reward their loyalty. Track kiosk-specific KPIs: revenue per square metre, revenue per staff hour, drinks per hour at peak, and waste rate. These metrics, benchmarked over time, reveal whether the operation is improving. Explore kiosk expansion opportunities — if one site works, the model may be replicable at other locations with similar footfall.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Audit the kiosk space: measure, draw a floor plan, and map equipment placement
Optimise the workflow layout so core service actions require zero wasted steps
Define a focused kiosk menu of 10-15 items that the setup can execute brilliantly
Remove menu items that slow service or require impractical ingredients for the format
Calculate the theoretical maximum throughput (drinks per hour) of the current setup
Create a Kiosk Opening and Closing Checklist specific to the site
Train staff on the solo operation workflow and customer queue management

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop comprehensive Kiosk SOPs for opening, peak operation, and closing
Establish a daily stock management and ordering system sized to kiosk storage capacity
Implement kiosk-specific food safety protocols including handwashing and temperature monitoring
Create a Kiosk Maintenance Log for equipment and site condition tracking
Train all kiosk staff on solo operation, food safety, and queue management
Set up waste tracking to monitor and reduce daily waste volumes
Establish a supplier delivery schedule aligned to kiosk storage constraints

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Introduce a pre-ordering system (QR code or app) to reduce queue times
Analyse hourly sales data to optimise staffing levels throughout the day
Develop a weather contingency plan for stock and staffing adjustments
Build a regular customer recognition and loyalty programme
Track kiosk-specific KPIs: revenue per square metre, drinks per hour, waste rate
Evaluate replicability of the kiosk model for additional locations

deliverables: Kiosk Floor Plan and Workflow Layout diagram
Kiosk Menu Design with cost-per-serve analysis

tools.description: Floor plan template for mapping equipment, storage, and movement paths within a compact kiosk footprint. Includes reach-zone analysis and step-count optimisation for the primary operator position.
Site-specific checklist covering equipment startup, stock check, milk prep, till float, and cleanliness for opening, plus shutdown, stock count, waste recording, cleaning, and security for closing.
Daily stock management template: closing stock count, next-day sales forecast by item, order calculation, delivery confirmation, and variance tracking to minimise both stockouts and waste.
Comprehensive SOP for kiosk operation covering solo workflow, peak-hour batching, customer queue management, food safety protocols, handwashing procedures, and emergency contingencies.
Weekly dashboard tracking kiosk-specific metrics: revenue per square metre, drinks per staff hour, peak throughput rate, waste rate, and customer count with trend lines for continuous improvement.

failure_modes: Stock runs out during peak hours because ordering was based on guesswork rather than data, losing sales and frustrating customers
Workflow layout forces unnecessary movement, reducing throughput and increasing customer wait times during peak
Food safety protocols lapse because there is no back-of-house separation — surfaces are not cleaned between tasks
Solo operator becomes overwhelmed during unexpected demand spikes with no backup available
kpis: Peak-hour throughput of at least 40 drinks per hour with a two-person kiosk operation
Stock-out incidents occurring on fewer than 5% of operating days
Daily waste value below 3% of daily revenue
Revenue per square metre exceeding comparable full-size cafe operations in the same area
constraints: Kiosk footprint is fixed and cannot be expanded — all optimisation must work within existing dimensions
Power supply may be limited, constraining equipment choices (e.g., no second group head on the machine)
Water access varies by site — some kiosks have mains water, others rely on tanks requiring daily refilling
Local authority licensing and planning conditions may restrict operating hours, signage, or food preparation activities

time_load.L1: 4 hours (space audit, workflow redesign, menu definition, checklist creation)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (SOP development, stock system setup, food safety protocols, staff training)
time_load.L3: 2 hours/month (data analysis, KPI review, supplier coordination, continuous improvement)

blocks.review_protocol: Daily stock and waste check at close by operator. Weekly sales and performance review by manager. Monthly KPI dashboard review by owner. Quarterly workflow and menu optimisation assessment.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block provides the complete operational framework for running a coffee kiosk — from workflow layout and focused menu design to stock management and food safety in a compact format. It turns tight space constraints into efficient, high-revenue-per-metre operations.
implementation_risks: Kiosk sites with unreliable power or water supply require contingency planning for equipment-down scenarios
Solo operation demands exceptional multitasking — not all staff are suited to kiosk work
Council regulations on kiosk operations vary significantly by local authority — verify all permits before committing to a site
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate for kiosk and coffee_van venue types. Core operational block for compact format operations. Pairs with B205 (Coffee Van Operations) for mobile-specific extensions.

blocks.name: Coffee Van Operations
blocks.purpose: Addresses the unique operational logistics of running a mobile coffee van, covering vehicle maintenance, location strategy, event booking, licensing, self-contained water and power systems, setup and teardown routines, and the mobile-specific challenges that differentiate van operations from any fixed-site format.

detailed_objectives: Establish a complete mobile setup and teardown procedure that gets the van operational in minimum time
Develop a location strategy covering regular pitches, events, markets, and private bookings
Manage self-contained utilities: water tank capacity, generator or battery power, waste water disposal
Maintain vehicle roadworthiness, equipment function, and food safety compliance while mobile
Build a booking and scheduling system for events, markets, and corporate engagements
secondary_objectives: Maximise revenue by optimising the weekly location schedule based on footfall data and profit per pitch
Ensure all mobile trading licences, street trading consents, and event permits are current and compliant
Create a distinctive mobile brand presence that is recognisable across multiple locations
Develop contingency plans for vehicle breakdown, generator failure, and adverse weather

blocks.why_this_matters: A coffee van is a business on wheels — it combines every challenge of running a cafe with the additional complexity of transport, self-contained utilities, location variability, and regulatory compliance across multiple local authorities. The appeal is obvious: low fixed costs, the ability to go where customers are, and the flexibility to trade at events, markets, office parks, and festivals. But the failure rate for coffee vans is high because operators underestimate the logistics. A van that runs out of fresh water at 10am has to close. A generator that fails during a music festival means zero revenue from an event that cost hundreds of pounds in pitch fees. A vehicle that breaks down on the motorway en route to a wedding booking creates a reputational disaster. Street trading licences vary by council — a pitch that is legal in one borough may require a different consent in the next. Water tank capacity determines how many drinks can be served before refilling, and grey water tanks must be disposed of legally, not dumped in a car park drain. The espresso machine, grinder, and fridge must survive vehicle vibration and temperature extremes during transport, requiring shock-mounting and thermal management. Event bookings require contracts, deposits, and precise logistical planning: arrival time, power access, water availability, waste disposal, and load-in restrictions. Each of these operational details, if managed well, creates a smooth and profitable mobile business. If managed badly, any one of them can ruin a trading day. This block provides the comprehensive operational framework that separates professional mobile coffee operators from hobbyists with a van.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Create a comprehensive Van Setup Checklist covering every step from arrival at a location to first drink served. This typically includes: park and level the vehicle, deploy the serving hatch and canopy, connect to mains power or start the generator (allowing 5 minutes warm-up before loading equipment), check fresh water tank level and top up if needed, check grey water tank is empty, start the espresso machine (20-30 minutes heat-up for most machines), load fresh milk into the fridge, set out cups and lids, stock food items, set up the till or card reader, display the menu board, and perform a test shot before opening. Time this entire sequence — aim to have the van operational within 30-45 minutes of arrival. Create a parallel Teardown Checklist in reverse order, including grey water secured for disposal, equipment powered down, perishables stored safely, van secured, and departure. Week 2: Audit all licences and permits. Street trading requires consent from each local authority where you trade — check which councils cover your regular pitches and confirm all consents are valid and renewed before expiry. For events and markets, confirm whether the organiser's licence covers your trading or if you need your own Temporary Event Notice. Verify vehicle insurance covers commercial mobile food trading, not just standard vehicle use. Confirm your food business is registered with the local authority where the van is stored (home address counts) as required under EU-retained food hygiene regulations. Check gas safety certificates if the van uses LPG, and electrical safety certificates for the generator and all equipment.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a Location Strategy Document. Categorise locations into: regular pitches (weekly spots with predictable footfall and revenue), events and festivals (higher risk, higher reward — pitch fees range from £50 to £500+, but a good event can generate a full week's revenue in a day), markets (weekly or monthly, lower pitch fees, community-building), and private bookings (weddings, corporate events, parties — premium pricing with deposits). For each regular pitch, track: revenue, footfall, weather impact, and net profit after pitch fees and travel costs. Build a weekly schedule that maximises revenue while managing travel time and fatigue. Create a Booking Management System for events and private work: enquiry form, quote template, booking confirmation with deposit, event logistics checklist (power, water, access, timings), and post-event invoice. Establish a Vehicle Maintenance Schedule covering: MOT renewal, service intervals, tyre condition, generator servicing, water system sanitisation (monthly flush with food-safe sanitiser), gas safety inspection (annually for LPG), and a pre-journey vehicle check routine. Develop a Water Management Protocol: calculate drinks-per-litre to know exactly how many serves the fresh water tank allows, set a minimum water level trigger for refilling, and identify refill points near regular pitches.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). The coffee van should now operate as a polished mobile brand. Build a location performance database tracking revenue, profit, weather, and customer count for every pitch and event — over 6-12 months, this reveals which locations are genuinely profitable and which are vanity bookings. Use social media to announce daily locations, building a following that tracks the van's movements. Develop a loyalty programme suited to mobile: a stamp card or app that works across all locations. Invest in van aesthetics — the vehicle is the brand, so its appearance matters enormously. Professional signage, lighting, and a clean, well-maintained exterior create premium perception. Explore multi-van expansion if demand warrants — the operational systems developed should be replicable across additional vehicles. Establish relationships with event organisers and wedding planners who can provide regular premium bookings. Conduct annual reviews of all licences, insurance, and vehicle compliance to ensure nothing lapses. Build a network with other mobile food traders for mutual support, backup, and referrals.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Create a comprehensive Van Setup Checklist from arrival to first drink served
Create a matching Teardown Checklist covering shutdown, waste, and departure
Time the setup and teardown procedures, aiming for 30-45 minutes each
Audit all licences: street trading consents, food business registration, gas and electrical certificates
Verify vehicle insurance covers commercial mobile food trading
Confirm fresh water and grey water tank capacities and calculate drinks-per-fill
Perform a full vehicle and equipment safety check

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a Location Strategy Document categorising regular pitches, events, markets, and bookings
Track revenue and profit per location to identify the most profitable pitches
Create a Booking Management System with enquiry form, quote, confirmation, and invoice templates
Establish a Vehicle Maintenance Schedule covering MOT, servicing, and equipment checks
Develop a Water Management Protocol with drinks-per-litre calculations and refill planning
Build a weekly trading schedule maximising revenue and minimising dead travel time
Create an Event Logistics Checklist for private and festival bookings

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Build a location performance database tracking profitability across all pitches over 12 months
Use social media to announce daily locations and build a mobile following
Invest in van branding, signage, and appearance as the primary marketing asset
Develop relationships with event organisers and wedding planners for premium bookings
Explore multi-van expansion using replicable operational systems
Conduct annual compliance reviews of all licences, insurance, and vehicle roadworthiness

tools.description: Step-by-step laminated checklists for van deployment and pack-down: vehicle positioning, utility connections, equipment startup sequence, stock staging, and reverse for teardown including waste disposal.

Per-location log recording each trading day: revenue, pitch fee, travel cost, fuel, drinks served, weather conditions, and calculated net profit. Used to rank locations and optimise the weekly schedule.

Complete booking workflow: enquiry response template, quote calculator (per-head or flat fee), booking confirmation with deposit terms, event logistics checklist (power, water, access), and post-event invoice.
Annual maintenance calendar tracking MOT, service intervals, generator maintenance, water tank sanitisation, gas safety inspection, tyre checks, and pre-journey vehicle condition checks.

Register of all licences, permits, and certificates with issue dates, expiry dates, renewal reminders, and responsible authority contact details. Covers street trading consents, food registration, insurance, gas, and electrical safety.

failure_modes: Fresh water runs out mid-service because drinks-per-litre was not calculated, forcing an early close and lost revenue
Street trading consent lapses and the operator is fined or moved on by council enforcement officers
Generator failure at an event with no mains power backup means zero service capability for the entire booking
Vehicle breakdown en route to a paid event booking creates both immediate revenue loss and long-term reputational damage
kpis: Setup time from arrival to first drink served consistently under 40 minutes
Zero licence or compliance lapses across all trading locations in any 12-month period
Average revenue per trading day exceeding breakeven by at least 50% across all locations
Vehicle and equipment downtime below 3% of scheduled trading days per year
constraints: Fresh water and grey water tank capacities impose a hard ceiling on drinks per fill — larger tanks add weight and reduce fuel efficiency
Generator noise and emissions may be restricted at certain locations, requiring battery or mains alternatives
Each local authority has different street trading consent requirements, fees, and application timelines
Vehicle weight limits (3.5 tonnes for standard licence) constrain equipment and water capacity

time_load.L1: 6 hours (checklist creation, licence audit, vehicle and equipment check, water capacity calculation)
time_load.L2: 12 hours (location strategy, booking system, maintenance schedule, water protocol, tracking setup)
time_load.L3: 3 hours/month (performance analysis, schedule optimisation, compliance review, relationship building)

blocks.review_protocol: Pre-journey vehicle check before every trading day. Weekly location revenue review. Monthly water system sanitisation and equipment maintenance check. Quarterly licence and compliance register review. Annual full vehicle service and strategy review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block covers everything specific to mobile coffee van operations: vehicle management, self-contained water and power, location strategy, event bookings, licensing, and the logistical discipline that separates professional mobile operators from unreliable ones.
implementation_risks: Vehicle breakdowns can be mitigated but never eliminated — always have a recovery plan and customer notification process
Pitch fee costs can erode margins if location profitability is not tracked rigorously
Event bookings require deposits and contracts — verbal agreements lead to no-shows and disputes
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate only for coffee_van venue type. Extends B204 (Kiosk Operations) with mobile-specific logistics. Critical dependency for any mobile trading operation.

blocks.name: Barista Competition Programme
blocks.purpose: Creates a structured programme that uses barista competitions — both internal and external — as a development tool for advancing technical skills, building team motivation, raising the cafe's industry profile, and creating a culture of excellence. This block covers internal throwdowns, preparation for regional and national SCA competitions, budgeting for competition entry, and leveraging competition success for marketing and recruitment.

detailed_objectives: Establish regular internal barista throwdowns as a fun, low-stakes development activity
Identify and prepare team members who aspire to compete in external SCA-sanctioned competitions
Develop competition-grade skills in espresso, milk texturing, latte art, and signature drinks
Build a competition budget covering entry fees, practice coffee, travel, and equipment
Leverage competition participation and results for brand positioning and recruitment marketing
secondary_objectives: Create a pipeline of highly skilled baristas whose competition experience elevates daily service quality
Connect the cafe to the broader specialty coffee community through competition networks
Boost team morale and pride through shared competitive experiences and celebrations of achievement
Attract ambitious baristas who seek employers that support their competitive ambitions

blocks.why_this_matters: Barista competitions are the most effective accelerator of technical skill development in the coffee industry. The preparation process demands a level of precision, consistency, and understanding that far exceeds what daily cafe service requires — and that is precisely the point. A barista preparing for a latte art competition practises hundreds of pours, developing muscle memory and milk texturing intuition that makes their daily service flawless. One preparing for an espresso competition studies extraction theory, water chemistry, and sensory evaluation at a depth that transforms how they approach every shot they pull. The skills developed in competition practice cascade directly into better coffee quality, faster diagnosis of problems, and more confident customer engagement. Beyond individual development, competitions build team culture. Internal throwdowns — informal competitions where team members compete in latte art, blind tasting, or speed challenges — create energy, camaraderie, and healthy rivalry. They give quieter team members a chance to shine and reveal hidden talent. External competitions connect the cafe to the wider specialty coffee community, building relationships with other baristas, roasters, and equipment suppliers that bring knowledge and opportunities back to the business. From a commercial perspective, competition participation is powerful marketing. A cafe that can say its barista placed in the UK Latte Art Championship or competed at the regional brewing competition has a credibility claim that no amount of advertising can replicate. It signals to customers that this cafe takes its craft seriously. It signals to prospective employees that this is a place where talent is developed, not just exploited. The investment is modest — entry fees, practice coffee, and occasional travel — relative to the return in skills, culture, and brand value.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Start with internal throwdowns. These require almost no budget and create immediate engagement. Organise the first throwdown as a latte art competition — it is the most visual, accessible, and fun category. Rules: each barista pours three drinks, the team votes on the best pour (or invite a guest judge for impartiality), and the winner gets a small prize (a bag of specialty coffee, a voucher, or simply bragging rights). Schedule throwdowns monthly — alternate between latte art, espresso accuracy (closest to a target recipe), blind tasting, and speed challenges. Week 2: Research the external competition calendar. The UK Barista Championship, UK Brewers Cup, UK Latte Art Championship, and UK Coffee in Good Spirits Championship are all sanctioned by the SCA and run through regional heats. Identify the nearest regional heat, its date, entry requirements, and fees. Share the information with the team — gauge interest without pressure. If someone expresses interest, begin supporting them with dedicated practice time and mentorship from the head barista.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Formalise the competition programme. Create a Competition Programme Policy covering: how internal throwdowns are scheduled and judged, how external competition candidates are identified and supported, what financial support the cafe provides (entry fees, practice coffee, travel expenses), and what time commitment is expected from the competitor (practice sessions during or outside work hours). For external competitors, develop a Preparation Plan. SCA competitions typically require: a signature drink (an original recipe showcasing creativity), a technical espresso service (consistency, workflow, time management), and a presentation to judges (communication, knowledge, engagement). Practice should begin at least 8 weeks before the competition date, with weekly sessions building toward the full routine. Source practice coffee from the roaster — many roasters will sponsor competition coffee in exchange for being credited at the event. Build a Competition Kit: the competitor needs their preferred equipment (grinder, tamper, distribution tool, milk pitcher, cups) tuned and familiar. Document the preparation process — it becomes training material for future competitors.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Competition culture should now be embedded in the cafe's identity. Support at least one external competition entry per year from the team.
